- Environmental impact assessment (EIA)

These are exactly the application areas KTU examiners love to cite in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight (or Nine) Elements of Visual Interpretation

The human eye picks up visual signatures through a structured set of attributes. These are known as the **Elements of Interpretation** (also called **Image Interpretation Keys** or **Clues**). KTU examiners expect the **8 standard elements** plus the modern addition of **Association/Site**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 + 1 Elements (Mnemonic: "T-S-S-P-T-Sh-S-A-L")**
> **T**one · **S**hape · **S**ize · **P**attern · **T**exture · **Sh**adow · **S**ite · **A**ssociation · **L**ocation

---

### 2.1.1 TONE (or Colour)

**Definition:** Tone is the relative **brightness** or darkness of an object on a black-and-white image. On a colour composite, it is referred to as **Colour** (or Hue + Saturation).

**Why it matters:** Tone is the most fundamental element. Without contrast in tone, no other element can be detected.

**Engineering principle:**
- In **panchromatic** imagery → use *tone*
- In **multispectral colour composites** → use *colour*
- Dark tone = high absorption (e.g., water bodies, wet soil)
- Light tone = high reflectance (e.g., sand, concrete, snow)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *tone* and *texture*. Tone refers to single-pixel brightness; Texture refers to the spatial *variation* of tone across many pixels.

---

### 2.1.2 SHAPE

**Definition:** The geometric form or outline of an object.

**Why it matters:** Man-made features have regular geometric shapes; natural features have irregular shapes.

| Feature Type | Typical Shape |
|---|---|
| Airport runway | Long rectangle / elongated strip |
| Circular irrigation field | Perfect circle (pivot irrigation) |
| River | Sinuous, irregular |
| Building | Square/Rectangle |
| Volcano | Cone |
| Tree | Irregular blob |

---

### 2.1.3 SIZE

**Definition:** The dimensional magnitude of an object as measured relative to other objects or to the image scale.

**Why it matters:** A small white blob could be a house OR a factory. Knowing the **scale** (e.g., 1:50,000) converts the blob into a measurement in metres.

**Critical Relationship — The Scale Equation:**

$$S = \frac{d}{D}$$

Where:
- $S$ = Representative Fraction (Scale, e.g., 1:50,000)
- $d$ = Distance on the photograph (in cm)
- $D$ = Actual ground distance (in cm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** Always convert blob-size to ground dimensions before concluding. This is where 7 marks are awarded.

---

### 2.1.4 PATTERN

**Definition:** The orderly spatial arrangement of objects.

**Why it matters:** Reveals cultural / anthropogenic activities.

**Examples:**
- **Gridiron pattern** → planned city streets (Manhattan-style)
- **Radial pattern** → circular / concentric streets
- **Dendritic pattern** → drainage network on uniform rock
- **Centred pattern** → circular irrigation; targets
- **Contour pattern** → terraced agriculture on hills

---

### 2.1.5 TEXTURE

**Definition:** The frequency of tonal change within an image — i.e., the **visual smoothness or roughness** produced by an aggregation of small features too small to be seen individually.

**Why it matters:** Differentiates surfaces that have the same tone but different spatial frequency.

| Texture Class | Appearance | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Smooth** | No tonal variation, uniform | Still water body, paved road |
| **Fine** | Small tonal variations, almost smooth | Grassland, young forest |
| **Medium** | Moderate grain | Mature forest, mature crops |
| **Coarse** | Large, distinct tonal patches | Rocky terrain, broken forest canopy |
| **Stippled** | Salt-and-pepper appearance | Mixed land use, scrubland |

---

### 2.1.6 SHADOW

**Definition:** The silhouette or dark area cast by an object when illuminated by the sun.

**Why it matters (Double-edged sword):**
- ✅ **Positive use:** Reveals the **profile (height, shape)** of objects — towers, bridges, tall trees, monuments.
- ❌ **Negative use:** Can **obscure** ground features beneath the shadow.

**KTU caveat:** Shadows are useful only when the sun elevation is moderate (not noon overhead, not zero at sunset).

---

### 2.1.7 SITE (Location/Topographic Position)

**Definition:** The geographic / topographic setting in which a feature occurs.

**Why it matters:** Features have characteristic locations. For example:
- A **bridge** is found at a river crossing.
- A **factory** is typically near a transport corridor or raw material.
- A **levee** is on a floodplain edge.

---

### 2.1.8 ASSOCIATION

**Definition:** The relationship between a feature and its surroundings.

**Why it matters:** It is the "contextual deduction" element — you see what ELSE is nearby.

**Classic example:** A small rectangle near a railway line + a town edge + smoke plumes = **Factory**, not a house.

---

### 2.1.9 LOCATION (Geographic Context)

Sometimes treated as part of *Site*, but KTU 2024 syllabus lists it as a separate element. It refers to the **absolute geographic coordinates** and **regional setting** (e.g., Kerala's coast, Western Ghats).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to avoid markdown breaking.

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scale: $S = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | $S$ = scale, $d$ = photo dist, $D$ = ground dist | dimensionless | Converting pixel size to ground size |
| 2 | Ground Resolved Distance: $GRD = \dfrac{H}{f} \cdot d_{p}$ | $H$ = flying height, $f$ = focal length, $d_{p}$ = detector pixel size | metres | Minimum object size visible |
| 3 | Sun Elevation Angle effect on Shadow: $h = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | $h$ = object height, $L$ = shadow length, $\alpha$ = sun elevation | metres | Estimating building/tree heights |
| 4 | Image Scale (vertical photo): $S = \dfrac{f}{H}$ | $f$ = focal length, $H$ = flying height above terrain | dimensionless | Determining photo scale |
| 5 | Area: $A_{ground} = A_{image} \cdot S^{2}$ | $A$ = area | $\text{m}^{2}$ | Computing ground coverage |
| 6 | Texture frequency: $f_{texture} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{spatial}}$ | $f$ = frequency, $\lambda$ = spatial wavelength | cycles/mm | Classifying texture classes |
| 7 | Minimum detectable object: $D_{min} = GRD$ | same as #2 | metres | Resolution limit |
| 8 | Relief displacement: $r = \dfrac{h \cdot d}{H}$ | $r$ = displacement, $h$ = object height, $d$ = distance from principal point, $H$ = flying height | mm on photo | Tall-building distortion |

> [!NOTE]
> Formulas #1, #2, #4, #5, #8 are the **5 most-tested** in KTU 2024 board papers. Memorize them with units.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | How Interpretation Elements Are Used |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Site selection for highways; drainage pattern analysis (Pattern + Texture) |
| **Environmental Engg** | Land-use / land-cover mapping (Tone + Association) |
| **Agricultural Engg** | Crop identification (Texture + Pattern + Tone) |
| **Urban Planning** | Settlement mapping (Shape + Size + Association) |
| **Geology** | Fault / lineament detection (Pattern + Tone) |
| **Forestry** | Species / canopy mapping (Texture + Shadow + Tone) |
| **Disaster Mgmt** | Flood extent (Tone + Site) |
| **Defence** | Target identification (Shape + Size + Shadow + Association) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Image Scale and Ground Resolved Distance (GRD)

### Derivation 1 — Image Scale (Vertical Aerial Photograph)

**Starting Principle:** Similar triangles between the camera lens and ground.

Consider a vertical aerial photograph taken from flying height $H$ above ground, using a camera of focal length $f$. For an object of ground length $D$ on the terrain, its image length $d$ is formed on the film.

**Step 1: Geometry of similar triangles.**
The lens-to-ground distance is $H$. The lens-to-film distance is $f$. The triangles formed are similar:

$$\frac{d}{D} = \frac{f}{H}$$

**Step 2: Rearranging for scale.**

$$S = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{f}{H}$$

**Step 3: Example — KTU Board Standard.**
A camera has $f = 152 \text{ mm}$ and flies at $H = 3000 \text{ m}$. Find the scale.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{f}{H} \\
  &= \frac{152 \text{ mm}}{3000 \cdot 1000 \text{ mm}} \\
  &= \frac{152}{3 \times 10^{6}} \\
  &= 1 : 19{,}736.8 \\
  &\approx 1 : 20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation.**
A scale of **1:20,000** means 1 mm on photo = 20,000 mm = **20 m on ground**. So an object of 1 cm on photo is **200 m on ground**.

---

### Derivation 2 — Ground Resolved Distance (GRD)

**Starting Principle:** GRD is the smallest ground object that can be *resolved* (seen as separate) on the photograph. It depends on the detector's instantaneous field of view.

**Step 1: Define angular resolution.**

The smallest angle $\theta$ a detector can distinguish is approximately:

$$\theta = \frac{d_{p}}{f}$$

where $d_{p}$ is the pixel/ detector size.

**Step 2: Project this angle to the ground.**

For a vertical photograph at altitude $H$:

$$GRD = H \cdot \theta = H \cdot \frac{d_{p}}{f}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GRD &= \frac{H \cdot d_{p}}{f} \\
    &= \frac{d_{p}}{f / H} \\
    &= \frac{d_{p}}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result:** A smaller scale fraction (e.g., 1:50,000 is larger number = SMALLER scale) means **larger GRD** = poorer resolution.

**Step 4: Numerical example (KTU favourite).**
Pixel size $= 6 \text{ μm}$, focal length $= 150 \text{ mm}$, flying height $= 500 \text{ km}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GRD &= \frac{500 \times 10^{3} \text{ m} \cdot 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}}{150 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}} \\
    &= \frac{3.0}{0.15} \\
    &= 20 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a Landsat-like sensor can resolve **~20 m ground objects**.

---

### Derivation 3 — Object Height from Shadow Length

**Starting Principle:** Trigonometry of the right triangle formed by object, shadow, and sun ray.

**Step 1: Sun elevation angle.**

Let $\alpha$ = sun elevation angle (angle between sun ray and horizontal ground). Shadow length $L$ on the ground relates to object height $h$ by:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{h}{L}$$

**Step 2: Solving for height.**

$$h = L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 3: Numerical example.**

Shadow length = 50 m, sun elevation = 30°. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 50 \cdot \tan(30°) \\
  &= 50 \cdot 0.577 \\
  &= 28.87 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to a **9-storey building** (≈ 3 m/floor).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Which Element to Use When

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **the single most valuable revision tool** for the 14-mark KTU board question "Discuss the elements of visual interpretation with examples."

| Feature to Identify | Primary Element | Supporting Elements | Example / Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water body vs. shadow | **Tone** (very dark) + Site | Shape | Reservoir shape is geometric; shadow is irregular |
| Coniferous vs. deciduous forest | **Texture** (fine vs. coarse) | Tone | Both are dark — texture distinguishes them |
| Airport runway | **Shape** (long rectangle) | Size, Association | Don't confuse with road |
| Stadium | **Shape** (oval/circle) + Size | Association (near city) | Could be confused with racetrack |
| Railway line | **Shape** (very long, straight) + Pattern | Association | Roads are straighter; rail has sharper curves |
| Sand dune | **Shape** (crescent) + Pattern | Shadow | Crescent shape = barchan dune |
| Volcanic cone | **Shape** (cone) + Shadow | Site (along tectonic belt) | Profile visible only in shadow |
| Crop field | **Pattern** (regular grid) | Tone | Distinguish from natural grassland |
| Building height | **Shadow** | — | Use Derivation 3 above |
| Fault line | **Pattern** (linear) + Tone | Site | Straight line cutting across topography |
| Residential area | **Pattern** + Tone | Association | Houses have regular spacing |
| Industrial area | **Size** + Association | Shape, Tone (smoke plumes) | Large buildings + chimney shadow |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Table — Panchromatic vs. Colour vs. CIR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare the interpretation capability of B&W, true colour, and false colour composite images."*

| Property | Panchromatic (B&W) | True Colour (RGB) | False Colour Composite (CIR: NIR-R-G) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bands used | 1 broad band | R, G, B | NIR, R, G |
| Tone / Colour basis | **Tone** (grayscale) | **Colour** | **Colour** (shifted) |
| Vegetation appearance | Mid-grey | Green | **Bright red** (signature feature) |
| Water | Dark | Blue | **Black / very dark** |
| Urban | Light grey | White/grey | Light blue-grey |
| **Best element exploited** | Shape, Size, Shadow, Pattern | All 8+1 | Tone/Colour + Pattern |
| **Resolution** | Highest (e.g., 0.3 m) | Moderate (e.g., 2 m) | Lower (e.g., 10 m) |
| **Best for** | Engineering structures | Visual realism | Vegetation & water studies |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List any six elements of visual image interpretation with one example each.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

The six elements of visual interpretation are:

1. **Tone** — Relative brightness of objects. *Example:* Water appears dark-toned on a panchromatic image.
2. **Shape** — Geometric form of objects. *Example:* Airport runways are long rectangles.
3. **Size** — Dimensional magnitude relative to scale. *Example:* A small white speck at 1:50,000 scale represents a building, not a vehicle.
4. **Pattern** — Spatial arrangement. *Example:* Dendritic drainage pattern on uniform rock.
5. **Shadow** — Silhouette of objects. *Example:* Tower profile seen via its shadow.
6. **Association** — Relationship with surroundings. *Example:* A small building near a railway platform = station.

*Other acceptable elements:* Texture, Site, Location.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Differentiate between Selective Key and Elimination Key.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Selective Key | Elimination Key |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Helps the interpreter **identify** a feature by showing typical appearance | Helps the interpreter **rule out** features that don't match given criteria |
| Usage | Reference photos of *known* features shown alongside | Step-by-step decision flowchart |
| Best for | Trained interpreters, common features | Complex images with many similar features |
| Format | Annotated photos / sketches | Dichotomous (yes/no) decision trees |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the elements of visual image interpretation. Highlight the role of each element with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vertical aerial photograph was captured with a camera of focal length **152.4 mm** from a flying height of **4500 m** above ground. If the photograph shows a building shadow of length **3.2 mm** and the sun elevation angle at the time of exposure was **45°**, determine: **(i)** the scale of the photograph, **(ii)** the actual length of the shadow on the ground, and **(iii)** the height of the building. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The elements of visual image interpretation are the visual cues that an interpreter uses to identify features on an image. They are:

**1. Tone:** The relative brightness of an object. Dark tones indicate low reflectance (water); light tones indicate high reflectance (sand, concrete).

**2. Shape:** The geometric outline. Man-made features (rectangles, circles) vs. natural (irregular).

**3. Size:** Relative dimensions after applying scale. A blob at 1:10,000 scale could be a building or a vehicle.

**4. Pattern:** Spatial arrangement. Dendritic drainage, gridiron streets.

**5. Texture:** Visual roughness from aggregated small features. Smooth water vs. coarse forest.

**6. Shadow:** Reveals profile (positive) and obscures ground (negative).

**7. Site:** Topographic / geographic setting. Bridges on rivers, factories on transport corridors.

**8. Association:** Contextual deduction. A small building + rail line = station.

**9. Location:** Regional setting — Kerala coast vs. Western Ghats.

**Conclusion:** No single element is sufficient. Interpreters must apply **all relevant elements together** for confident identification.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $f = 152.4 \text{ mm}$, $H = 4500 \text{ m} = 4{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}$, shadow on photo $L_{photo} = 3.2 \text{ mm}$, sun elevation $\alpha = 45°$.

**(i) Scale of photograph:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{f}{H} \\
  &= \frac{152.4 \text{ mm}}{4{,}500{,}000 \text{ mm}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{29{,}527.6} \\
  &\approx 1 : 29{,}528
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual shadow length on ground:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{ground} &= L_{photo} \cdot \frac{1}{S} \\
           &= 3.2 \text{ mm} \cdot 29{,}528 \\
           &= 94{,}489.6 \text{ mm} \\
           &\approx 94.49 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Height of building using $h = L_{ground} \cdot \tan(\alpha)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 94.49 \cdot \tan(45°) \\
  &= 94.49 \cdot 1 \\
  &= 94.49 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to a **~30-storey tall structure** (≈ 3 m per floor).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the concept of an Image Interpretation Key. Compare the Selective Key and Elimination Key with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of **0.5 milliradians**. It is mounted on a platform flying at an altitude of **705 km**. Calculate: **(i)** the ground resolution cell (pixel size on ground), **(ii)** the smallest object that can be detected assuming a 2 × 2 pixel requirement, and **(iii)** the area covered by a single pixel. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Definition:**
An **Image Interpretation Key** is a reference document (containing photos, sketches, or descriptions) used by interpreters to identify features in aerial/satellite images by comparing the unknown image with the documented signatures of known features.

**Types:**

**1. Selective Key:**
- Presents typical images (photos or sketches) of features.
- Interpreter visually matches the unknown image with key examples.
- *Example:* A forestry key shows typical photo signatures of teak, eucalyptus, and bamboo plantations.

**2. Elimination Key:**
- Uses a decision tree / dichotomous chart.
- Interpreter applies yes/no questions to eliminate features that don't match.
- *Example:* "Is the feature linear?" → Yes → Check for road, rail, river, canal, pipeline. Apply additional filters (colour, width, context) to isolate.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Selective Key | Elimination Key |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Visual matching | Logical deduction |
| Format | Annotated photos | Decision tree / flow chart |
| Speed | Fast for trained users | Slower but accurate |
| Reliability | High for common features | High for ambiguous scenes |

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $IFOV = 0.5 \text{ mrad} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$, $H = 705 \text{ km} = 705{,}000 \text{ m}$.

**(i) Ground resolution cell (pixel size on ground):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GRD &= H \cdot IFOV \\
    &= 705{,}000 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 352.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Smallest detectable object (2 × 2 pixel rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{min} &= 2 \cdot GRD \\
        &= 2 \cdot 352.5 \\
        &= 705 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Area of one pixel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{pixel} &= GRD^{2} \\
          &= 352.5^{2} \\
          &= 124{,}256.25 \text{ m}^{2} \\
          &\approx 0.124 \text{ km}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches **Landsat-1 / Landsat-2 MSS** specifications (≈ 79 m GRD originally; here computed for illustration).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tone with Texture** — *Tone* is single-pixel brightness; *Texture* is the spatial frequency of tone change. Mixing these loses **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always write "mm", "m", "degrees" explicitly. Missing units = **−1 mark each**.
>
> 3. **Using $|$ (pipe) inside markdown tables** — Causes formatting errors. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
>
> 4. **Not stating the formula before substituting** — KTU examiners award a **method mark** (1–2 marks) even if the final answer is wrong. Always write the formula.
>
> 5. **Listing elements without examples** — In the 7-mark question, each element MUST have an example. Without examples, expect a **30% deduction**.
>
> 6. **Writing "Colour" for panchromatic images** — Colour applies ONLY to multispectral images. Use "Tone" for panchromatic. Common mistake.
>
> 7. **Misnaming "Interpretation Key" as "Legend"** — A map legend is for the reader; an Interpretation Key is for the *interpreter*. Different concept.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ Visual interpretation = **human eye + brain** on aerial/satellite images.
- ✅ **8 + 1 elements:** Tone, Shape, Size, Pattern, Texture, Shadow, Site, Association, Location (Mnemonic: **T-S-S-P-T-Sh-S-A-L**).
- ✅ **Tone** = single-pixel brightness; **Texture** = spatial variation across pixels.
- ✅ **Shadow** = double-edged sword (reveals height, hides ground).
- ✅ **Pattern** = spatial arrangement; **Texture** = frequency of tonal change.
- ✅ **Interpretation Key** = reference document for identification.
- ✅ **Selective Key** = photo-matching (visual); **Elimination Key** = decision-tree (logical).
- ✅ **Scale formula:** $S = f / H$ (vertical photo).
- ✅ **GRD formula:** $GRD = H \cdot d_p / f$.
- ✅ **Shadow-height formula:** $h = L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$.
- ✅ **CIR (False Colour Composite)** renders vegetation as **bright red** — a signature feature for vegetation studies.
- ✅ Panchromatic has highest spatial resolution; uses **tone** only.
- ✅ **Site + Association** are the "context" elements — used when other elements are ambiguous.
- ✅ **GRD decreases** with lower altitude, smaller pixel size, and longer focal length.
- ✅ **Relief displacement** = $r = h \cdot d / H$ (relevant for tall objects near photo edges).
- ✅ Interpreters MUST use **multiple elements together** — single-element identification is unreliable.
- ✅ **Map Legend ≠ Interpretation Key** (legend is for the user; key is for the interpreter).
- ✅ Sun elevation angle should be **moderate** (30°–60°) for best shadow-based interpretation.
- ✅ **Panchromatic** uses **Tone**; **Multispectral** uses **Colour**.
- ✅ Engineering applications: urban planning, agriculture, forestry, geology, disaster assessment, defence.
- ✅ KTU 2024 board favourite 14-marker: *"Discuss the elements of visual interpretation with examples"* — write all 9 elements with one example each and a concluding sentence on combined use.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Image Interpretation: Visual Interpretation Elements and Interpretation Key**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Image Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Image Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Image Processing (DIP) in Remote Sensing** is the use of computer algorithms to manipulate and interpret digital raster data acquired by remote sensors. A **digital image** is a two-dimensional, discrete function $f(x,y)$ where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (planimetric) coordinates, and the amplitude of $f$ at any pair of coordinates $(x, y)$ is called the **intensity** or **grey level** (also termed **Digital Number / DN**) of the image at that point. When $x$, $y$, and the amplitude values of $f$ are all finite, discrete quantities, the image is called a **digital image**.

In remote sensing, the value $f(x, y)$ is derived from the **spectral radiance** $L(\lambda)$ reflected or emitted from the Earth's surface, captured by the sensor's detector elements, quantized into a finite number of bits, and gridded into picture elements (**pixels**).

The image is mathematically represented as an $M \times N$ matrix of DN values:

$$f(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} f(0,0) & f(0,1) & \cdots & f(0,N-1) \\ f(1,0) & f(1,1) & \cdots & f(1,N-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f(M-1,0) & f(M-1,1) & \cdots & f(M-1,N-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

Each pixel in a multispectral image holds a **vector of DN values** (one per band), e.g., for Landsat-8 OLI: Band 2 (Blue), Band 3 (Green), Band 4 (Red), Band 5 (NIR), etc.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Coloured Mosaic":**
> Imagine you are staring at a giant **Mona Lisa painting** on a wall. Now take a fine mesh net and overlay it on the painting. Each tiny square in the net is a **pixel**. The colour of that square (let's say "Light Olive Green = 127") is its **Digital Number (DN)**. The whole painting is therefore a **matrix of numbers**, and the act of reading those numbers and modifying them (making the greens greener, sharpening the edges, removing haze) is **Digital Image Processing**.
>
> In a satellite image of Kerala, the sensor takes a $185 \text{ km} \times 185 \text{ km}$ patch of the ground, chops it up into $30 \text{ m} \times 30 \text{ m}$ squares (pixels), and records the brightness from each square in, say, 11 different colours (bands). The computer's job is to "read" these brightness numbers and convert them into a meaningful land-cover map.

## 1.3 Resolution: The Four Pillars

The **information content** of a digital image is governed by **four mutually independent resolutions**. Every KTU exam question on "image quality" hinges on these:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Spatial Resolution** — Size of the pixel on the ground (e.g., $30 \text{ m}$ for Landsat-8 OLI; $0.30 \text{ m}$ for WorldView-3 panchromatic). Smaller value = higher detail.
> - **Spectral Resolution** — Number and width of wavelength bands the sensor records. (e.g., Landsat-8 has 11 bands, hyperspectral sensors have $>$ 200 bands).
> - **Radiometric Resolution** — Number of bits used to store the DN. (8-bit = $2^8 = 256$ levels; 11-bit = $2048$ levels; 12-bit = $4096$ levels).
> - **Temporal Resolution** — Revisit time of the satellite over the same point. (e.g., 16 days for Landsat-8; ~1 day for Sentinel-2 constellation).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Quantization Levels:** $L = 2^k$, where $k$ is the **radiometric resolution in bits**.
- **File Size (bytes):** $S = M \times N \times B \times (k/8)$, where $B$ = number of bands.
- **NDVI range:** strictly bounded, $-1 \leq \text{NDVI} \leq +1$.
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (used in sensor calibration).
- **DN** is dimensionless (relative); converting DN to radiance requires **radiometric calibration coefficients** $L_{min}$ and $L_{max}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Digital Image Processing Pipeline

The complete sequence of operations performed on a raw satellite image is logically partitioned into the following sequential stages:

### Stage 1: Pre-Processing (Radiometric, Geometric, Atmospheric)
- **Why?** Raw DN values are corrupted by the sun's illumination angle, atmospheric scattering (especially Rayleigh and Mie), sensor noise, and platform instability.
- **How?** The DN is converted to **Top-of-Atmosphere (TOA) radiance**, then to **TOA reflectance**, then to **Surface Reflectance** using models like **6S**, **MODTRAN**, or **Dark Object Subtraction (DOS)**.
- **Geometric correction** uses **Ground Control Points (GCPs)** and a **polynomial transformation** to register the image to a real-world coordinate system (e.g., UTM, WGS-84).

### Stage 2: Image Enhancement
- **Why?** To make the visual interpretation easier for the human eye and to amplify subtle features.
- **How?** Operations on individual pixels (**point operations**) or on neighbourhoods of pixels (**local/ spatial operations**). Includes:
  - **Contrast Stretching** (Linear, Min-Max, Percentage, Standard Deviation).
  - **Histogram Equalization** (redistributes DNs to follow a uniform distribution).
  - **Spatial Filtering** (Low-pass = smoothing/sharpening; High-pass = edge detection).
  - **Band Ratios & Vegetation Indices** (mathematical combination of bands).

### Stage 3: Information Extraction
- **Image Classification** — assigning each pixel to a thematic class (e.g., water, forest, urban).
- **Change Detection** — comparing two co-registered images of the same area from different dates.
- **Feature Extraction** — edges, lines, textures, shapes.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam-grade and must be memorised verbatim.**

| Sl. No. | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|:-------:|:-------------|:-----------------------:|:------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Quantization Levels | $L = 2^k$ | $k$ = radiometric resolution (bits) | Sensor design, storage planning |
| 2 | Raw File Size | $S = M \cdot N \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{k}{8}$ bytes | $M,N$ = rows, cols; $B$ = bands | Data volume estimation for missions |
| 3 | DN to TOA Radiance | $L_\lambda = G_{rescale} \cdot DN + B_{rescale}$ | $G, B$ = gain, bias (from metadata) | Converts sensor number to physical units ($\text{W/m}^2\text{/sr/}\mu\text{m}$) |
| 4 | TOA Reflectance | $\rho_\lambda = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L_\lambda \cdot d^2}{ESUN_\lambda \cdot \cos\theta_s}$ | $d$ = Earth–Sun distance (AU); $ESUN$ = exo-atmospheric irradiance; $\theta_s$ = solar zenith angle | Removes solar illumination geometry |
| 5 | NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) | $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | NIR, Red = surface reflectance bands | Vegetation health, biomass, crop monitoring |
| 6 | Normalized Burn Ratio (NBR) | $\text{NBR} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{SWIR}}{\text{NIR} + \text{SWIR}}$ | NIR, SWIR = spectral bands | Fire severity mapping |
| 7 | Linear Contrast Stretch | $DN_{out} = \left(\dfrac{DN_{in} - DN_{min}}{DN_{max} - DN_{min}}\right) \cdot (L-1)$ | $L$ = number of output levels | Display enhancement |
| 8 | Mean (image statistic) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y)$ | $f(x,y)$ = DN value | Image brightness characterization |
| 9 | Variance (image statistic) | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum (f(x,y) - \bar{x})^2$ | $\sigma^2$ = variance | Texture, contrast measure |
| 10 | Histogram Equalization Transform | $s_k = (L-1) \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} \dfrac{n_j}{N}$ | $n_j$ = count of pixels with grey level $j$; $N$ = total pixels | Contrast enhancement |
| 11 | Convolution (spatial filter) | $g(x,y) = \sum_{s} \sum_{t} w(s,t) \cdot f(x+s, y+t)$ | $w$ = filter kernel | Edge detection, smoothing |
| 12 | Overall Classification Accuracy | $OA = \dfrac{\sum \text{Diagonal elements of confusion matrix}}{\text{Total reference pixels}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) | Validates classified map |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For ratio-based indices (NDVI, NBR, SAVI), the resulting value is unitless and bounded between $-1$ and $+1$ when **reflectance** values are used, but can be unbounded ($-2$ to $+2$) when raw DN is used — this is a very common KTU viva question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture** (Kerala context): NDVI time-series from Sentinel-2 to monitor **cardamom and coconut plantations** in Idukki/Wayanad.
- **Urban Planning (Kerala State Remote Sensing Agency – KSRSAC):** Supervised classification of Kochi/Trivandrum for **land-use / land-cover (LULC)** change.
- **Disaster Management (KSDMA):** Change detection of pre- and post-2018 Kerala floods using NBR.
- **Forestry:** Texture and band-ratio analysis for **deforestation and encroachment** detection in Western Ghats.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustiveness Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: NDVI from Raw DN (Two Methods)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A Landsat-8 OLI pixel in Wayanad Forest has the following band reflectance values (after atmospheric correction): $\text{NIR} = 0.45$, $\text{Red} = 0.08$. Compute NDVI and interpret.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula from the cheat sheet:**

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{0.45 - 0.08}{0.45 + 0.08}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.45 - 0.08 = 0.37$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.45 + 0.08 = 0.53$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division:**

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{0.37}{0.53} = 0.6981...$$

**Step 5 — Round to standard 4 decimal places:**

$$\text{NDVI} \approx 0.6982$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation (KTU expected answer):**

$$\text{Since } \text{NDVI} > 0.6 \Rightarrow \text{Dense, healthy vegetation (Closed Forest Canopy).}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard NDVI interpretation bands (must memorise):**
> - Water: $-0.1$ to $0.1$
> - Bare Soil: $0.1$ to $0.2$
> - Sparse Vegetation: $0.2$ to $0.4$
> - Moderate Vegetation: $0.4$ to $0.6$
> - Dense Vegetation: $> 0.6$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linear Min-Max Contrast Stretch

**Problem:** A 3-bit image has a range of DN values from $DN_{min} = 12$ to $DN_{max} = 200$. A particular pixel has $DN_{in} = 88$. Find the contrast-stretched output DN.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$DN_{out} = \left( \frac{DN_{in} - DN_{min}}{DN_{max} - DN_{min}} \right) \cdot (L - 1)$$

**Step 2 — For a 3-bit image, the number of levels is:**

$$L = 2^k = 2^3 = 8 \text{ levels (0 to 7)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$DN_{out} = \left( \frac{88 - 12}{200 - 12} \right) \cdot (8 - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator and denominator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 88 - 12 = 76$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 200 - 12 = 188$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the fraction:**

$$\frac{76}{188} = 0.4042...$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $(L-1) = 7$:**

$$DN_{out} = 0.4042 \times 7 = 2.8298...$$

**Step 7 — Round to nearest integer DN (since output must be an integer index):**

$$DN_{out} \approx 3$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:** A pixel of brightness 88 (which appeared as a "mid-grey" in the original 8-bit image) is now mapped to a level of 3 in a 3-bit (8-level) stretched image, optimizing display contrast.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Histogram Equalization Transformation

**Problem:** A $4 \times 4$ image has 16 pixels with the following grey-level distribution: 0 occurs 4 times, 1 occurs 6 times, 2 occurs 4 times, 3 occurs 2 times. Find the histogram-equalized output value for input grey level $r_k = 2$.

**Step 1 — Total pixel count:**

$$N = 16$$

**Step 2 — For 2-bit image, the number of grey levels is $L = 4$ (i.e., $0, 1, 2, 3$).**

**Step 3 — Compute the probability (normalized histogram) of each grey level:**

$$P(r_0) = \frac{n_0}{N} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25$$

$$P(r_1) = \frac{n_1}{N} = \frac{6}{16} = 0.375$$

$$P(r_2) = \frac{n_2}{N} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25$$

$$P(r_3) = \frac{n_3}{N} = \frac{2}{16} = 0.125$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cumulative distribution function (CDF) up to $k = 2$:**

$$\text{CDF}(r_2) = \sum_{j=0}^{2} P(r_j) = 0.25 + 0.375 + 0.25 = 0.875$$

**Step 5 — Apply the histogram equalization formula $s_k = (L-1) \cdot \text{CDF}(r_k)$:**

$$s_2 = (4 - 1) \cdot 0.875 = 3 \cdot 0.875 = 2.625$$

**Step 6 — Round to nearest valid integer level:**

$$s_2 \approx 3$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** All pixels originally at grey level 2 will now be displayed at grey level 3 (the highest level), since the cumulative distribution up to level 2 covered 87.5% of the image — pushing the output to the bright end of the stretched range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Computation of Image Mean and Standard Deviation

**Problem:** A $2 \times 2$ image has pixel DN values: $[[120, 130], [110, 140]]$. Compute mean, variance, and standard deviation.

**Step 1 — Total pixel count:** $N = 4$.

**Step 2 — Sum of all DN values:**

$$\sum f = 120 + 130 + 110 + 140 = 500$$

**Step 3 — Mean (average brightness):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f}{N} = \frac{500}{4} = 125$$

**Step 4 — Compute the squared deviations $(f(x,y) - \bar{x})^2$ for each pixel:**

$$\text{Pixel 1: } (120 - 125)^2 = (-5)^2 = 25$$

$$\text{Pixel 2: } (130 - 125)^2 = (5)^2 = 25$$

$$\text{Pixel 3: } (110 - 125)^2 = (-15)^2 = 225$$

$$\text{Pixel 4: } (140 - 125)^2 = (15)^2 = 225$$

**Step 5 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum (f - \bar{x})^2 = 25 + 25 + 225 + 225 = 500$$

**Step 6 — Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum (f - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{500}{4} = 125$$

**Step 7 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{125} \approx 11.18$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation:** A mean of 125 (mid-range for 8-bit) and standard deviation of 11.18 indicate a **low-contrast image**, suitable candidate for histogram equalization or contrast stretching.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 4.1** shows the canonical Digital Image Processing workflow. **Diagram 4.2** shows the classification taxonomy. All nodes are clean alphanumeric IDs with raw text labels (no markdown inside mermaid).


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **spatial resolution** and **spectral resolution** of a remote sensing image. Give one example sensor for each category.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Spatial Resolution** is the size of the smallest object on the ground that can be resolved by the sensor, equivalent to the area covered by a single pixel (e.g., **$30 \text{ m}$** for Landsat-8 OLI multispectral bands; **$0.30 \text{ m}$** for WorldView-3 panchromatic). Higher spatial resolution means a smaller pixel footprint and finer detail.
- **Spectral Resolution** refers to the number and width of wavelength bands a sensor can record. A sensor with 200 narrow bands of width $10 \text{ nm}$ has very high spectral resolution (e.g., **Hyperion** on EO-1), while a sensor with 3 broad bands (RGB) has low spectral resolution (e.g., **SPOT HRV panchromatic**).
- Key differentiator: Spatial = *where*; Spectral = *what colour/wavelength*.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2.** Define **NDVI**. Why is the Red band subtracted from NIR in the numerator? What is its range when using reflectance values?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** is a band-ratio index used to quantify vegetation greenness and vigour.
- **Formula:** $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$
- **Reason for subtracting Red from NIR:** Healthy vegetation strongly **absorbs Red light** (for photosynthesis) and strongly **reflects NIR** (due to leaf mesophyll scattering). The difference is therefore high for vegetation and low for non-vegetation.
- **Range:** When using **reflectance values** (which are bounded $[0, 1]$), NDVI is bounded: **$-1 \leq \text{NDVI} \leq +1$**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Most students lose marks in Part B by writing definitions without diagrams, skipping the worked numerical example, and missing the *engineering interpretation* of the final answer. You **MUST** include: (1) the formula, (2) the substitution, (3) the numerical evaluation, and (4) a one-line interpretation in a real-world context.

### **Question A — Module 2 (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the four types of resolutions in a digital image. Discuss the trade-off between spatial, spectral, and radiometric resolution with respect to data volume. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The four resolutions that govern the quality and information content of a digital remote sensing image are:

**1. Spatial Resolution** — The ground area represented by one pixel. E.g., Landsat-8 OLI multispectral $= 30 \text{ m}$, panchromatic $= 15 \text{ m}$, Sentinel-2 $= 10 \text{ m}/20 \text{ m}/60 \text{ m}$ depending on band.

**2. Spectral Resolution** — Number and width of wavelength intervals the sensor can differentiate. Multispectral sensors (3–15 bands, e.g., Landsat-8 has 11); Hyperspectral (200+ bands, e.g., AVIRIS, PRISMA).

**3. Radiometric Resolution** — Number of bits used to quantize the energy recorded. 8-bit = 256 levels; 11-bit = 2048 levels; 12-bit = 4096 levels (e.g., Sentinel-2 MSI = 12-bit).

**4. Temporal Resolution** — Time gap between two successive acquisitions of the same area. Landsat-8 = 16 days, Sentinel-2 = 5 days, MODIS = 1–2 days.

**Trade-off and Data Volume:**
The file size of an image is given by:
$$S = M \times N \times B \times \frac{k}{8} \text{ bytes}$$

Improving **any** one resolution increases $S$:
- Doubling spatial resolution (halving pixel size) → quadruples $M$ and $N$ → data volume × 4.
- Doubling spectral resolution (doubling bands $B$) → data volume × 2.
- Adding 4 bits to radiometric resolution (e.g., 8→12 bit) → data volume × 1.5.

Hence, satellite design is a compromise. For example, **WorldView-3** chooses high spatial + moderate spectral + 11-bit; **Hyperion** chooses moderate spatial + very high spectral + 12-bit; **MODIS** chooses low spatial + moderate spectral + 12-bit + 1-day revisit.

---

**(b)** A Landsat-8 OLI image of a region in Thrissur has the following band reflectances: NIR = $0.42$, Red = $0.10$, SWIR-1 = $0.15$. Compute **NDVI** and **NBR**. Comment on the land-cover likely present. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — NDVI Formula:**

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{0.42 - 0.10}{0.42 + 0.10} = \frac{0.32}{0.52} = 0.6154 \approx 0.62$$

**Step 3 — Interpret NDVI:**
Since NDVI $> 0.6$, the pixel represents **dense vegetation** (forest or mature crop).

**Step 4 — NBR Formula:**

$$\text{NBR} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{SWIR}}{\text{NIR} + \text{SWIR}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{NBR} = \frac{0.42 - 0.15}{0.42 + 0.15} = \frac{0.27}{0.57} = 0.4737 \approx 0.47$$

**Step 6 — Interpret NBR:**
A positive NBR of $0.47$ indicates **healthy, unburned vegetation with high moisture content** (since SWIR-1 is highly sensitive to leaf/soil moisture). This rules out burned or dry areas.

**Step 7 — Combined Conclusion:** The pixel represents **healthy, dense, moist vegetation** — most likely a **paddy field, coconut plantation, or forest** typical of Thrissur district, Kerala.

---

### **Question B — Module 2 (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **supervised classification** procedure. Compare Maximum Likelihood and Minimum Distance classifiers. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Flowchart of Supervised Classification:**
1. **Define study area** and acquire pre-processed multi-band image.
2. **Identify training areas** (ROIs) representing each land-cover class (water, forest, urban, agriculture).
3. **Compute the spectral signature** (mean vector and covariance matrix) of each class from training pixels.
4. **Apply a decision rule** to every unknown pixel in the image.
5. **Generate the classified map**.
6. **Perform accuracy assessment** using an independent set of reference pixels (confusion matrix → Kappa, OA).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Minimum Distance | Maximum Likelihood |
|:--------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| Spectral Statistic Used | Mean vector only | Mean vector + Covariance matrix |
| Probability Assumption | No probability model | Assumes multivariate normal distribution |
| Computational Cost | Very low | High (matrix inversion) |
| Class Shape in Feature Space | Spherical / hypersphere | Ellipsoidal (oriented) |
| Accuracy on Overlapping Classes | Poor | Significantly better |
| Sensitivity to Training Sample Size | Low | High (requires robust statistics) |
| Best Used When | Quick exploratory mapping | Production-grade thematic maps |

**Working Equations:**

For **Minimum Distance** (Euclidean):

$$D_i = \sqrt{\sum_{b=1}^{B} (x_b - \mu_{i,b})^2}$$

The pixel is assigned to the class $i$ with the **smallest** $D_i$.

For **Maximum Likelihood** (Gaussian assumption):

$$P(x \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{B/2} \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i)\right)$$

where $\mu_i$ is the mean vector, $\Sigma_i$ is the covariance matrix of class $\omega_i$. The pixel is assigned to the class with the **highest** $P(x \mid \omega_i)$.

---

**(b)** A $4 \times 4$ image has the following DN values:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 12 & 14 & 16 \\ 11 & 13 & 15 & 17 \\ 12 & 14 & 16 & 18 \\ 13 & 15 & 17 & 19 \end{bmatrix}$$

Calculate the **mean**, **variance**, and **standard deviation** of the image. Is the image suitable for **linear contrast stretching**? Justify. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total pixel count:** $N = 4 \times 4 = 16$.

**Step 2 — Sum of all DN values:**

$$\sum f = (10+12+14+16) + (11+13+15+17) + (12+14+16+18) + (13+15+17+19)$$

Compute each row:
- Row 1 sum: $10+12+14+16 = 52$
- Row 2 sum: $11+13+15+17 = 56$
- Row 3 sum: $12+14+16+18 = 60$
- Row 4 sum: $13+15+17+19 = 64$

Total sum: $\sum f = 52 + 56 + 60 + 64 = 232$

**Step 3 — Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{232}{16} = 14.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute squared deviations for each pixel** (using $(f - 14.5)^2$):

Row 1: $(10-14.5)^2=20.25$; $(12-14.5)^2=6.25$; $(14-14.5)^2=0.25$; $(16-14.5)^2=2.25$
Row 2: $(11-14.5)^2=12.25$; $(13-14.5)^2=2.25$; $(15-14.5)^2=0.25$; $(17-14.5)^2=6.25$
Row 3: $(12-14.5)^2=6.25$; $(14-14.5)^2=0.25$; $(16-14.5)^2=2.25$; $(18-14.5)^2=12.25$
Row 4: $(13-14.5)^2=2.25$; $(15-14.5)^2=0.25$; $(17-14.5)^2=6.25$; $(19-14.5)^2=20.25$

**Step 5 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum (f-\bar{x})^2 = (20.25+6.25+0.25+2.25) + (12.25+2.25+0.25+6.25) + (6.25+0.25+2.25+12.25) + (2.25+0.25+6.25+20.25)$$

Row sums: $29 + 21 + 21 + 29 = 100$

**Step 6 — Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25$$

**Step 7 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5$$

**Step 8 — Justification for Linear Contrast Stretching:**
The DN values occupy a very narrow range ($10$ to $19$, spread of only $9$ levels) within the 0–255 range of an 8-bit display. The mean is $14.5$ (dark) and $\sigma = 2.5$ is very low. This indicates a **low-contrast, slightly dark image**. Therefore, the image is an **ideal candidate for linear contrast stretching** (e.g., Min-Max) to expand the narrow $10$–$19$ range to the full $0$–$255$ range, dramatically improving visual interpretability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Digital Image Processing**

- **Digital image** = 2D discrete function $f(x,y)$, stored as an $M \times N$ matrix of **DN values**.
- **Pixel** = picture element = the smallest unit; holds a vector of DNs in multispectral images.
- **Four Resolutions:** Spatial (pixel size on ground), Spectral (number/width of bands), Radiometric ($k$ bits → $2^k$ levels), Temporal (revisit time).
- **File Size:** $S = M \cdot N \cdot B \cdot (k/8)$ bytes.
- **DN → Radiance → Reflectance** is the standard pre-processing chain.
- **NDVI** uses **NIR and Red**; **NBR** uses **NIR and SWIR-1**; both are normalized ratios in range $[-1, +1]$ when using reflectance.
- **NDVI interpretation:** Water $\leq 0.1$, Bare $0.1$–$0.2$, Sparse Veg $0.2$–$0.4$, Moderate $0.4$–$0.6$, Dense $> 0.6$.
- **Contrast Stretching** redistributes DNs to fill the display range; **Histogram Equalization** produces a uniform output histogram.
- **Spatial filters:** Low-pass = smoothing (averaging kernel); High-pass = edge sharpening; Median = noise removal; Sobel/Prewitt = gradient edge detection.
- **Convolution formula:** $g(x,y) = \sum_s \sum_t w(s,t) \cdot f(x+s, y+t)$.
- **Supervised classification** uses training samples; **Unsupervised** (K-means, ISODATA) finds natural clusters.
- **Maximum Likelihood** = best accuracy, uses mean + covariance, assumes multivariate normal; **Minimum Distance** = fastest, uses mean only.
- **Accuracy metrics:** Overall Accuracy (OA), Producer's Accuracy (PA), User's Accuracy (UA), Kappa coefficient — all derived from the **Confusion Matrix**.
- **Common pitfall:** Using raw DN in NDVI formula (unbounded output) vs. using reflectance (bounded $[-1, +1]$).
- **Kerala context:** KSRSAC uses these techniques for LULC, KSDMA for flood mapping, KAU for crop monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
