| **Rows & Columns** | Number of cells along the y and x axes |
| **Bands / Layers** | Multiple grids stacked (e.g., RGB, NIR, SWIR) |
| **NoData** | Sentinel value (commonly **-9999** or **0**) for missing data |

---

## 1.4 Attribute Data

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Attribute Data** refers to the **non-spatial descriptive information** associated with geographic features. It is conventionally stored in **tabular form** (rows = features, columns = attributes) and is **linked to spatial features via a unique identifier (ID)**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Spreadsheet Sidekick"
If the vector polygon is the *outline* of a district, then the attribute table is the *report card* of that district: name, population, area, literacy rate, rainfall. The geometry tells you **where**; the attributes tell you **what**. The link between them is a unique Feature ID (FID) or Object ID (OID), like a roll number that ties a student's photo to his/her mark sheet.

### Types of Attributes
- **Nominal** — categories with no order (land use type: forest, water, urban)
- **Ordinal** — ranked categories (soil class: low, medium, high fertility)
- **Interval / Ratio** — numeric measurements (elevation in m, temperature in °C)

---

## 1.5 Why Two Models?

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of GIS Data Modelling**
> - **Vector** → best for **discrete, well-defined features** with sharp boundaries (parcels, roads, utilities).
> - **Raster** → best for **continuous phenomena** that vary smoothly across space (elevation, temperature, rainfall, imagery).
> - **Attributes** → mandatory in both, because a feature without description is just a shape.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vector Data Model — In-Depth Analysis

### 2.1.1 Coordinate Geometry Representation
A vector feature is fundamentally a tuple of coordinates.

**Point:**
$$P_i = (x_i, y_i)$$

**Polyline (with $n$ vertices):**
$$L = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$$

**Polygon (closed):**
$$\text{Polygon} = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n), (x_1, y_1)\}$$

### 2.1.2 Topology in Vector Data
Topology is the **logical relationships between spatial features** that remain invariant under elastic transformations (rubber-sheet geometry).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Topological Rules**
> 1. **Connectivity** — which lines connect to which (used for network analysis, e.g., road routing).
> 2. **Contiguity** — which polygons share a common boundary (used for adjacency analysis).
> 3. **Containment** — which polygon encloses which features (used for point-in-polygon overlay).

### 2.1.3 Spaghetti vs Topological Models

| Property | Spaghetti Model | Topological Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Structure** | Independent lines/polygons | Explicit node-arc-polygon tables |
| **Shared boundaries** | Stored twice (digitising errors common) | Stored once, shared via topology |
| **File size** | Larger due to duplication | Smaller, normalised |
| **Analysis** | Slow overlay; prone to slivers | Fast, accurate overlay |
| **Example** | AutoCAD DXF, early shapefiles | ArcInfo Coverage, ArcGIS Geodatabase |

### 2.1.4 Vector Data Formats
- **Shapefile (.shp)** — Esri format, widely used, multi-file (`.shp`, `.shx`, `.dbf`).
- **GeoDatabase (.gdb)** — Esri's object-relational spatial database.
- **GeoJSON / KML** — Web-friendly text formats.
- **GML** — OGC-compliant XML-based vector format.
- **DWG/DXF** — CAD-based formats.

---

## 2.2 Raster Data Model — In-Depth Analysis

### 2.2.1 Grid Structure
A raster is a 2D matrix indexed by row $r$ and column $c$:

$$Z = \begin{bmatrix} z_{1,1} & z_{1,2} & \cdots & z_{1,n} \\ z_{2,1} & z_{2,2} & \cdots & z_{2,n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ z_{m,1} & z_{m,2} & \cdots & z_{m,n} \end{bmatrix}$$

The real-world coordinate of cell $(r, c)$ is computed from the **origin** $(X_0, Y_0)$ and the **cell size** $s$:

$$X_c = X_0 + (c - 1) \cdot s$$

$$Y_r = Y_0 - (r - 1) \cdot s$$

(Subtraction in $Y$ accounts for row index increasing downward.)

### 2.2.2 Cell Size and Resolution
> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution ($R$)** = ground distance covered by one cell side. It is the inverse of detail level.

$$R = \frac{\text{Ground Extent (m)}}{\text{Number of Cells}}$$

- **Coarse resolution (e.g., 1 km)** → small file, less detail, used for global climate studies.
- **Fine resolution (e.g., 0.5 m)** → huge file, high detail, used for cadastral mapping.

### 2.2.3 Raster Data Compression & Encoding
Four classic encoding schemes reduce file size:

| Encoding | Description | Strength |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** | Stores value + run count for identical consecutive cells | Excellent for thematic maps |
| **Quad-Tree** | Recursively subdivides the grid into quadrants until uniform | Efficient for clustered data |
| **Chain Code** | Stores boundary direction codes (0–7) | Compact for line features |
| **Block / Cell-by-Cell** | Stores every cell, no compression | Baseline; largest file |

### 2.2.4 Raster Data Types

| Type | Value Range | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Boolean** | 0 or 1 | Forest / Non-forest mask |
| **Integer (Discrete)** | 0 – 255, 0 – 65535 | Land use class, count |
| **Floating Point (Continuous)** | Real numbers (e.g., -3.4e38 to 3.4e38) | DEM, temperature, NDVI |
| **Imagery (Multi-band)** | Multiple integer/float grids stacked | Landsat-8 OLI (11 bands) |

---

## 2.3 Attribute Data — In-Depth Analysis

### 2.3.1 Relational Database Foundation
Attribute data is organised in **relational tables** following **Edgar F. Codd's relational model**:
- A table is a set of **rows (tuples)** and **columns (attributes)**.
- Each row is uniquely identified by a **primary key** (e.g., FID).
- Tables can be joined via **foreign keys** for normalisation.

### 2.3.2 Types of Database Relationships

| Relationship | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **One-to-One (1:1)** | One record in A links to one in B | Parcel ↔ Owner |
| **One-to-Many (1:N)** | One record in A links to many in B | District → Many Panchayats |
| **Many-to-Many (M:N)** | Multiple records on both sides | Students ↔ Courses |

### 2.3.3 Attribute Operations
- **Selection** — `SELECT * FROM roads WHERE type = 'NH'` (SQL)
- **Classification** — reclassify numeric values into classes (e.g., slope 0–5° = flat).
- **Computation** — add fields, calculate area/length/perimeter.
- **Join / Relate** — merge tables by common key.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Reference Table — Vector vs Raster**

| Parameter | Vector Model | Raster Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basic unit** | Point, line, polygon | Cell (pixel) |
| **Coordinate system** | Explicit $(x, y)$ per vertex | Implicit (row, column → $x, y$) |
| **Topology** | Explicitly stored | Implicit (8-neighbour, 4-neighbour) |
| **Best for** | Discrete features (roads, parcels) | Continuous fields (elevation, imagery) |
| **Storage** | Compact for sparse data | Compact for dense uniform data |
| **Coordinate precision** | High (sub-cm theoretically) | Limited by cell size |
| **Overlay analysis** | Computationally intensive | Fast and simple (cell-by-cell) |
| **Network analysis** | Excellent (nodes + arcs) | Not native |
| **Typical format** | .shp, .gdb, .kml | .tif, .img, .grd, .asc |
| **Data volume** | Small for sparse, large for dense | Large regardless (resolution-driven) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Numerical Formulas (Critical for KTU Numericals)**

| # | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $X_c = X_0 + (c-1)\cdot s$ | Column → X coordinate |
| 2 | $Y_r = Y_0 - (r-1)\cdot s$ | Row → Y coordinate |
| 3 | $R = \text{Extent}/\text{Cells}$ | Resolution calculation |
| 4 | $N = (\text{Extent}/R)^2$ | Number of cells in a square raster |
| 5 | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$ | Length of a polyline |
| 6 | File size (bytes) = Rows × Cols × Bands × Bytes-per-cell | Raster storage estimation |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering** — Vector parcels + raster DEM for cut/fill volume estimation.
- **Urban Planning** — Vector road networks for routing; raster LULC for growth modelling.
- **Environmental Engineering** — Raster NDVI for vegetation health; vector water bodies for buffer analysis.
- **Agriculture** — Raster soil moisture + vector field boundaries for precision farming.
- **Disaster Management** — Raster flood-inundation grids + vector evacuation route networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing the Real-World Coordinate of a Raster Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A raster has its top-left origin at $(X_0, Y_0) = (500000, 4500000)$ in UTM metres, with cell size $s = 10$ m. Find the real-world coordinates $(X, Y)$ of the cell at **row $r = 25$** and **column $c = 18$**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the formulas (from Section 2.2.1):

$$X_c = X_0 + (c - 1) \cdot s$$

$$Y_r = Y_0 - (r - 1) \cdot s$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the X-equation.

$$X_c = 500000 + (18 - 1) \cdot 10$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the bracket.

$$X_c = 500000 + 17 \cdot 10$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.

$$X_c = 500000 + 170 = 500170 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the Y-equation.

$$Y_r = 4500000 - (25 - 1) \cdot 10$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the bracket.

$$Y_r = 4500000 - 24 \cdot 10$$

**Step 7:** Multiply and subtract.

$$Y_r = 4500000 - 240 = 4499760 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8:** Conclude.

$$\boxed{(X, Y) = (500170 \text{ m},\ 4499760 \text{ m})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Y is subtracted:** Row 1 corresponds to the *top* (origin), so as we move down through rows, Y in real-world coordinates *decreases* (in northern hemisphere UTM, where Y points north).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Resolution and File-Size Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A satellite produces an image covering a ground area of **60 km × 60 km** with a spatial resolution of **15 m per pixel**, stored as **unsigned 16-bit integers (2 bytes/pixel)**, single band. Compute (a) the number of rows & columns, (b) total pixels, (c) file size in MB.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute number of columns.

$$N_{\text{cols}} = \frac{\text{Width}}{R} = \frac{60\,000 \text{ m}}{15 \text{ m}} = 4000 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2:** Compute number of rows (image is square).

$$N_{\text{rows}} = 4000 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 3:** Compute total pixels.

$$N_{\text{total}} = 4000 \times 4000 = 16\,000\,000 \text{ pixels} = 1.6 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 4:** Compute raw file size in bytes.

$$\text{Size} = 1.6 \times 10^{7} \times 2 \text{ bytes} = 3.2 \times 10^{7} \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to megabytes (1 MB = 1 048 576 bytes).

$$\text{Size in MB} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{7}}{1.048576 \times 10^{6}} \approx 30.52 \text{ MB}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{rows}} = N_{\text{cols}} = 4000;\quad N_{\text{total}} = 1.6 \times 10^{7};\quad \text{File size} \approx 30.52 \text{ MB}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Length of a Polyline (Vector Distance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A polyline has three vertices: $A(0, 0)$, $B(3, 4)$, $C(7, 1)$. Compute the total length using the Euclidean distance formula.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Distance from $A \to B$:

$$L_{AB} = \sqrt{(3-0)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 2:** Distance from $B \to C$:

$$L_{BC} = \sqrt{(7-3)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 3:** Total length:

$$L_{\text{total}} = L_{AB} + L_{BC} = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Conclude.

$$\boxed{L_{\text{total}} = 10 \text{ units}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Model

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Favourite 14-Mark Question Pattern**
> Examiners often present a scenario and ask: *Justify whether you would use a vector or raster model.* Use the decision matrix below.

| Scenario | Suggested Model | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mapping state boundaries | **Vector (polygon)** | Discrete, sharp boundaries; need adjacency analysis |
| Modelling rainfall distribution | **Raster (continuous)** | Smoothly varying, point-interpolated surface |
| Road network routing | **Vector (topological polyline)** | Connectivity required; nodes + arcs |
| Satellite image classification | **Raster (multi-band)** | Native raster format; pixel-based statistics |
| Utility network (water supply) | **Vector (network)** | Topology, flow direction, valve connectivity |
| Elevation / Slope / Aspect | **Raster (DEM)** | Continuous surface; cell-by-cell derivations |
| Cadastral parcel register | **Vector (polygon + DBMS)** | Precise boundaries linked to ownership attributes |
| Air pollution concentration | **Raster (interpolated grid)** | Continuous phenomenon from point samples |

---

## 3.5 Attribute Data — Worked SQL Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A `districts` table has columns `d_id` (primary key), `name`, `population`, `area_km2`, `state`. Write an SQL query to find districts with population density > 500 persons/km² in Kerala, sorted by density descending.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Density formula (derived attribute, not stored):

$$\text{Density} = \frac{\text{population}}{\text{area\_km2}}$$

**Step 2:** Write the SQL query.

```sql
SELECT d_id, name, population, area_km2,
       (population * 1.0 / area_km2) AS density
FROM districts
WHERE state = 'Kerala'
  AND (population * 1.0 / area_km2) > 500
ORDER BY density DESC;
```

**Step 3:** Note the use of `1.0` to force floating-point division (avoid integer truncation in some SQL engines).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in GIS:** Population density is a **derived attribute** that can be computed in the GIS attribute table using the Field Calculator, then used to create **choropleth maps** (thematic maps shaded by attribute value).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vector vs Raster — Conceptual Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the **conceptual architecture** of how a GIS organises the two data models and links them to attribute data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Geography] --> B{Choose Data Model}
    B -->|Discrete features| C[Vector Data Model]
    B -->|Continuous fields| D[Raster Data Model]
    C --> C1[Point Feature]
    C --> C2[Polyline Feature]
    C --> C3[Polygon Feature]
    D --> D1[Cell Matrix Grid]
    D --> D2[Cell Size Resolution]
    D --> D3[Multiple Bands Layers]
    C1 --> E[Feature ID Link]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    E --> F[Attribute Table]
    D1 --> G[Band Values per Cell]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    F --> H[Spatial Query and Analysis]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Map Product]
```

---

## 4.2 Vector Topological Relationships (Node–Arc–Polygon)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1((Node 1)) --- ARC1[Arc A]
    N2((Node 2)) --- ARC1
    N2 --- ARC2[Arc B]
    N3((Node 3)) --- ARC2
    N1 --- ARC3[Arc C]
    N3 --- ARC3
    ARC1 --> POLY1[Polygon P1]
    ARC2 --> POLY1
    ARC3 --> POLY2[Polygon P2]
    POLY1 -.shared boundary.-> ARC1
    POLY2 -.shared boundary.-> ARC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arc A is the **shared boundary** between Polygon P1 and Polygon P2 — the topological model stores this arc **only once** and links it to two polygon records (left polygon & right polygon), saving storage and avoiding digitising gaps.

---

## 4.3 Raster Cell-Value Encoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw Grid Matrix] --> ENC{Choose Encoding}
    ENC -->|Run-Length| RLE[Sequence of Value-Count Pairs]
    ENC -->|Quad-Tree| QT[Recursive Quadrant Subdivision]
    ENC -->|Block| BLK[Cell-by-Cell Storage]
    ENC -->|Chain Code| CC[Boundary Direction Codes 0-7]
    RLE --> STORE[Compressed Raster File]
    QT --> STORE
    BLK --> STORE
    CC --> STORE
    STORE --> DEC[Decompression on Read]
    DEC --> USE[Map Algebra and Analysis]
```

---

## 4.4 Attribute Data — Relational Linking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPATIAL[Spatial Feature Geometry] -->|FID or OID| LINK{Unique Identifier}
    LINK --> ATT[Attribute Table Row]
    ATT --> COL1[Name]
    ATT --> COL2[Category]
    ATT --> COL3[Numeric Measurement]
    ATT --> COL4[Date or Timestamp]
    COL1 --> OUT[Query Result]
    COL2 --> OUT
    COL3 --> OUT
    COL4 --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
> **One-to-Many Join Example:** A *District* (1) → many *Panchayats* (N). A foreign key `district_id` in the panchayat table points to the primary key in the district table, allowing spatial-attribute joins.

---

## 4.5 Vector-to-Raster and Raster-to-Vector Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vector Polygon] -->|Rasterise| R1[Assign Cell Values by Majority Rule]
    R1 --> RA[Rasterised Polygon Layer]
    RA -->|Vectorise| R2[Edge Detection and Polygon Tracing]
    R2 --> RV[New Vector Polygon]
    V -.approximation error.-> RA
    RA -.generalisation loss.-> RV
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** 2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions appear in **University Exam Part A** and **Internal Assessment tests**. Direct, definition-style answers of 3–4 sentences are expected.

---

### **Q1. Differentiate between vector and raster data models.**  `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Vector Model | Raster Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data structure** | Points, lines, polygons built from $(x, y)$ coordinates | Matrix of uniform cells (pixels) |
| **Representation** | Discrete features with sharp boundaries | Continuous fields as a regular grid |
| **Coordinate storage** | Explicit for every vertex | Implicit via row/column index |
| **Best suited for** | Roads, parcels, utilities | Imagery, DEM, temperature surfaces |
| **Example format** | Shapefile (`.shp`) | GeoTIFF (`.tif`) |

---

### **Q2. What is attribute data in GIS? How is it linked to spatial features?**  `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**
Attribute data is the **non-spatial descriptive information** about a geographic feature — for example, the name, area, and population of a district polygon. It is stored in a **relational table** where each row corresponds to one feature and each column represents one property.

The link between the spatial geometry and its attribute row is established through a **unique identifier** — the **Feature ID (FID)** or **Object ID (OID)** — which acts as the primary key in the attribute table. This allows GIS queries to retrieve geometry and its properties together (e.g., *"show all districts with population > 1 million"*).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** In the End Semester Exam (ESE), each module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. Marks are awarded for: diagram (2), explanation (3), justification/comparison (2).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Vector and Raster Data Models**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **vector data model** in detail. Discuss its components (point, line, polygon), topology, and the difference between spaghetti and topological models. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the **raster data model** with a neat sketch. Discuss resolution, cell types, and the four encoding methods used for raster data compression. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Vector Data Model**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The vector data model represents real-world features as discrete geometric primitives defined by **x, y coordinate pairs**. The three basic primitives are:
- **Point** — a single coordinate pair (e.g., a bore well).
- **Line / Polyline** — an ordered set of vertices connected by straight segments (e.g., a road).
- **Polygon** — a closed chain of line segments enclosing an area (e.g., a land parcel).

**Step 2 — Topology (2 marks):**
Topology captures logical relationships that are invariant under geometric transformations. The three primary topological properties are:
- **Connectivity** — which arcs meet at which nodes (used in network tracing).
- **Contiguity (Adjacency)** — which polygons share a common arc.
- **Containment** — which points/lines lie inside which polygons (point-in-polygon test).

**Step 3 — Spaghetti vs Topological (2 marks):**
- **Spaghetti Model:** Each feature is stored as a list of independent coordinates. Shared boundaries are duplicated, leading to **sliver polygons** and large file sizes. Suitable for simple cartography.
- **Topological Model:** Stores explicit node, arc, and polygon tables with left/right polygon IDs. Shared boundaries are stored **once**, enabling accurate overlay, adjacency, and network analysis. Used in ArcInfo Coverages and Geodatabases.

**Step 4 — Formats (1 mark):** Common formats include Shapefile (`.shp`), GeoDatabase (`.gdb`), GeoJSON, KML, GML, and DWG.

#### **Model Solution (b) — Raster Data Model**

**Step 1 — Definition with sketch (2 marks):**
The raster model represents space as a **regular grid of cells (pixels)**, each holding a single numeric value. The figure shows an $m \times n$ matrix where the cell at row $r$, column $c$ stores value $Z_{r,c}$ representing elevation, land cover, reflectance, etc.

**Step 2 — Resolution (2 marks):**
Resolution $R$ is the ground distance of one cell side. **Higher resolution (smaller $R$)** gives more detail but increases file size. For example, Sentinel-2 multispectral imagery is $10$ m, while WorldView-3 panchromatic is $0.3$ m.

**Step 3 — Cell/Value Types (1 mark):**
Boolean (0/1), Integer (categorical or thematic), Floating-point (continuous, e.g., DEM), and Multi-band imagery (stacked rasters like RGB+NIR).

**Step 4 — Encoding Methods (2 marks):**
1. **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** — stores value and run count for sequences of identical cells. Excellent for thematic maps.
2. **Quad-Tree** — recursively splits the grid into four quadrants until uniform. Efficient for clustered data.
3. **Chain Code** — encodes line boundaries as directional codes (0–7). Compact for line features.
4. **Block / Cell-by-Cell** — uncompressed baseline storage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice on Attribute Data & Comparison**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** What are **attribute data** in GIS? Explain different **types of attributes** with examples. Discuss **relational database concepts** (primary key, foreign key, joins) used to manage attribute data. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Compare **vector and raster data models** under the heads: data structure, storage, accuracy, overlay, network analysis, and best-suited applications. State, with justification, which model you would choose for: *(i)* a road network, *(ii)* a Digital Elevation Model, *(iii)* a satellite land-use classification, *(iv)* a village cadastral map. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Attribute Data**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Attribute data is the **non-spatial descriptive information** associated with each geographic feature. It is stored in tabular form and linked to geometry via a unique **Feature ID (FID)**.

**Step 2 — Types of Attributes (3 marks):**
- **Nominal** — categories without order (e.g., land use: forest, water, urban).
- **Ordinal** — ranked categories (e.g., soil fertility: low, medium, high).
- **Interval** — numeric scale with no true zero (e.g., temperature in °C).
- **Ratio** — numeric scale with a true zero (e.g., elevation in m, rainfall in mm).

**Step 3 — Relational Database Concepts (3 marks):**
- **Table** — rows (records) and columns (fields).
- **Primary Key** — uniquely identifies a row (e.g., `parcel_id`).
- **Foreign Key** — field in a child table that references the primary key of a parent table.
- **Join** — combines records from two tables using a common key.
- **Relate** — temporary dynamic association between tables.
- **One-to-Many / Many-to-Many** — relationship types (e.g., one district → many panchayats).

#### **Model Solution (b) — Vector vs Raster Comparison & Application Choice**

**Step 1 — Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Vector | Raster |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data structure | Points, lines, polygons | Grid of cells |
| Storage | Compact for sparse data | Compact for dense uniform data |
| Accuracy | Sub-cm precision possible | Limited by cell size |
| Overlay | Complex; prone to slivers | Fast, cell-by-cell map algebra |
| Network analysis | Native (nodes + arcs) | Not native |
| Best applications | Cadastral, utilities, transport | Imagery, DEM, climate surfaces |

**Step 2 — Application Choices with Justification (3 marks):**
- **(i) Road network** → **Vector**, because routing needs connectivity (nodes) and arcs with attributes like speed limits.
- **(ii) Digital Elevation Model** → **Raster**, because elevation is continuous; cell-by-cell derivations (slope, aspect) are efficient.
- **(iii) Satellite land-use classification** → **Raster**, because classification operates on pixel spectral values; preserves raster resolution.
- **(iv) Village cadastral map** → **Vector (polygon)**, because cadastral boundaries require legal precision and must link to ownership attributes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Vector & Raster Questions**
> 1. **Confusing Raster origin orientation** — Students often write $Y = Y_0 + (r-1) \cdot s$ instead of the correct $Y = Y_0 - (r-1) \cdot s$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** in resolution and distance calculations. **Lose 0.5–1 mark.**
> 3. **Mixing spaghetti and topological models** — Many students incorrectly call shapefiles "topological." Shapefiles are **spaghetti-like** (no built-in topology). ArcInfo Coverages and Geodatabases support topology. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Ignoring FID/OID linkage** between geometry and attribute table — this is **the most skipped concept** in KTU answers. Always mention the unique identifier link. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Listing raster encodings without explaining when each is best** — examiners award marks for *justification*, not just listing. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 6. **Writing $|$ absolute value $|$ with raw pipes in a markdown table** — this **breaks the table rendering** in PDF/Word. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX.
> 7. **Forgetting the sketch/diagram** — KTU explicitly allocates **2 marks for diagrams** in 7-mark sub-parts. Always include a labelled vector/raster diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly)**

**🔹 Vector Data Model**
- Three primitives: **Point (0-D), Line (1-D), Polygon (2-D)**
- Coordinates stored as explicit $(x, y)$ tuples
- Topology: **connectivity, contiguity, containment**
- Spaghetti model: simple, no topology, duplication errors
- Topological model: nodes + arcs + polygons, shared boundaries stored once
- Common formats: `.shp`, `.gdb`, `.kml`, `.geojson`

**🔹 Raster Data Model**
- Data stored as a 2D matrix of cells
- Coordinate formulas:
  $$X_c = X_0 + (c-1)\cdot s,\qquad Y_r = Y_0 - (r-1)\cdot s$$
- Resolution $R$ = ground distance of one cell
- Cell types: Boolean, Integer, Float, Multi-band
- Encodings: **RLE, Quad-Tree, Chain Code, Block**
- Common formats: `.tif`, `.img`, `.grd`, `.asc`

**🔹 Attribute Data**
- Non-spatial descriptive data stored in tables
- Linkage via **FID / OID (Primary Key)**
- Attribute types: **Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio**
- Relational operations: **Select, Join, Relate, Classify, Compute**
- SQL is the standard query language

**🔹 Model Selection**
- **Vector** → discrete, well-defined, sharp-boundary features
- **Raster** → continuous fields, imagery, surfaces
- **Hybrid (Geodatabase + Image)** → modern GIS uses BOTH simultaneously

**🔹 High-Yield Formulas**
- Polyline length: $L = \sum \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$
- Cell coordinate: $X_c = X_0 + (c-1) \cdot s$
- Resolution: $R = \text{Extent} / \text{Cells}$
- File size: $\text{Bytes} = \text{Rows} \times \text{Cols} \times \text{Bands} \times \text{BPC}$

**🔹 KTU Exam Mantra**
> *"Define → Diagram → Differentiate → Justify → Compute."*
> Always follow this **5-step answer structure** for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map Projections and Coordinate Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Map Projections and Coordinate Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🗺️ Module 3: Map Projections and Coordinate Systems

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Map Projection** is a systematic mathematical transformation that converts the three-dimensional curved surface of the Earth (or any celestial body modeled as a sphere or spheroid) into a two-dimensional flat plane, which can then be represented as a map. This transformation is defined by a set of mathematical equations relating the geographic coordinates (latitude $\phi$ and longitude $\lambda$) of points on the Earth's surface to Cartesian coordinates (X, Y) on the developable surface (cylinder, cone, or plane).

A **Coordinate System (or Coordinate Reference System - CRS)** is a reference framework used to uniquely define the position of a point in 2D or 3D space. In GIS and Remote Sensing, it serves as the foundational scaffolding upon which all spatial data is georeferenced.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between **Coordinate Systems** (the rules for assigning coordinates - e.g., WGS 1984) and **Map Projections** (the mathematical formulae used to flatten the curved Earth - e.g., Transverse Mercator). A coordinate system may or may not be projected, but every projection is a part of a projected coordinate system.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to flatten the **peel of an orange** onto a flat table without tearing or stretching it. You will quickly notice the following:

1. **You cannot do it without distortion** — the peel will either tear, stretch, or wrinkle.
2. The **shape of the peel changes** depending on how you press it down.
3. If you carefully **slice the peel into sections** before flattening, you can preserve local shapes better but introduce gaps.

This is precisely the problem of **map projection**. The orange peel is the Earth (a curved 3D surface), and the table is the flat 2D map. A map projection is simply a **"set of rules"** describing how to perform this flattening — choosing what to preserve (area, shape, distance, or direction) and what to sacrifice.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lampshade:** Think of a cylindrical lampshade. The lampshade is a cylinder (developable surface), and the curved wall represents the Earth. When you unroll the lampshade into a flat rectangle, the "lines" on the lampshade become a flat grid. The mathematical rules that describe this unrolling are a **cylindrical projection**. Different projection families are simply different "lampshade shapes" (cylinders, cones, or flat planes) onto which we project the Earth's features.

### 1.1.3 The Need for Map Projections — Physical and Mathematical Necessity

- The Earth is an **oblate spheroid** (slightly flattened at the poles), with an equatorial radius of approximately **6,378.137 km** and a polar radius of **6,356.752 km**.
- Maps used in engineering, surveying, and GIS applications are inherently **2D planar representations** (paper, screen, plotter output).
- All quantitative spatial analysis (area calculation, distance measurement, overlay analysis) requires a **planar, metric framework** with consistent units.
- Without a defined projection, spatial data from different sources cannot be **overlaid accurately**, leading to positional errors of meters to kilometers.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** A typical engineering-scale map of Kerala (say 1:50,000) without a proper projection will have positional errors of up to **1 km** when overlaid with WGS-84 data. With a proper UTM projection, this error is reduced to **< 10 m**.

### 1.1.4 Why No Single Projection is "Perfect"

It is mathematically impossible (proven by **Gauss's Theorema Egregium** in differential geometry) to flatten a curved surface onto a plane without distortion. Therefore, every projection **distorts at least one of the following properties**:

| Property Preserved | Property Sacrificed | Example Projection |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape (Conformal)** | Area | Mercator |
| **Area (Equal-Area)** | Shape | Albers Equal-Area Conic |
| **Distance (Equidistant)** | Area & Shape | Equidistant Cylindrical |
| **Direction (Azimuthal)** | Distance & Area | Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area |

---

## 1.2 Classification of Coordinate Systems

A coordinate system in GIS is broadly classified into the following hierarchical types:

### 1.2.1 Geographic Coordinate System (GCS)

A **GCS** uses a three-dimensional spherical surface to define locations on the Earth. It specifies locations using two angular measurements:
- **Latitude ($\phi$):** The angle north or south of the Equator, ranging from **-90° to +90°**.
- **Longitude ($\lambda$):** The angle east or west of the Prime Meridian, ranging from **-180° to +180°**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Concept:** A GCS includes a **Datum** (mathematical model of the Earth's shape) and a **Prime Meridian** (zero longitude reference). The most common datum is **WGS 1984**, used by GPS satellites.

### 1.2.2 Projected Coordinate System (PCS)

A **PCS** is the result of applying a map projection to a GCS. It defines positions using linear units (meters, feet) on a flat 2D plane via Cartesian coordinates (Easting, Northing or X, Y).

### 1.2.3 Geocentric / Earth-Centered Coordinate System

This is a 3D Cartesian system (X, Y, Z) with its origin at the Earth's center of mass. Used primarily for satellite orbit calculations and GPS internal computations. Example: **WGS 84 (X, Y, Z)**.

### 1.2.4 Local / Arbitrary Coordinate System

A user-defined system with arbitrary origin and orientation. Common in construction sites, mining, and local engineering surveys where absolute global positions are unnecessary.

### 1.2.5 Vertical Coordinate System

Defines heights or depths relative to a vertical datum (e.g., mean sea level). Used for elevation modeling (DEMs) and contour generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain coordinate systems," always begin with a clear hierarchy diagram (GCS → PCS → Local) and explicitly mention the **datum** and **unit** (angular vs linear) for each.

---

## 1.3 Datum and Spheroid — The Geometric Foundation

### 1.3.1 Spheroid (Ellipsoid)

A **spheroid** (or ellipsoid of revolution) is a mathematical model that approximates the Earth's shape. It is defined by two parameters:
- **Semi-major axis (a):** Equatorial radius.
- **Semi-minor axis (b):** Polar radius.
- **Flattening (f):** $f = \frac{a - b}{a}$
- **Eccentricity squared (e²):** $e^2 = \frac{a^2 - b^2}{a^2}$

For **WGS 84**: $a = 6378137.0$ m, $f = 1/298.257223563$

For **Everest 1830 (used in India historically)**: $a = 6377276.345$ m, $f = 1/300.8017$

### 1.3.2 Datum

A **Datum** is a reference system that defines the position of the spheroid relative to the center of the Earth. There are two types:

1. **Geocentric Datum:** Spheroid center coincides with Earth's center of mass (e.g., WGS 84, ITRF).
2. **Local Datum:** Spheroid is shifted to fit a particular region (e.g., Everest Datum for India, NAD 27 for North America).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The Indian Context — Kaveri's Coordinates:** India uses the **Kalyanpur (Everest) Datum** with the **India and Adjacent Countries (IAC) Polyconic Projection** for Survey of India (SOI) maps. The shift between Everest and WGS 84 datum can be up to **~700 m** in some regions of India. Modern Indian projects use the **WGS 84 datum** with **UTM** projection.

---

## 1.4 The Developable Surfaces — Geometric Basis of Projections

A **developable surface** is a geometric surface that can be flattened into a plane without distortion (stretching or tearing). There are three classical developable surfaces:

### 1.4.1 Cylinder (Cylindrical Projections)

The Earth is conceptually enclosed in a cylinder. Light (or projection rays) passes through the Earth's surface onto the cylinder, which is then "unrolled" into a flat rectangle.

- **Tangent case:** Cylinder touches the Earth along a great circle (typically the Equator or a Meridian).
- **Secant case:** Cylinder cuts through the Earth along two small circles, reducing distortion over a wider area.

**Examples:** Mercator, Transverse Mercator, Cylindrical Equal-Area, UTM.

### 1.4.2 Cone (Conical Projections)

A cone is placed over the Earth, typically with its apex along the Earth's rotational axis. The cone is then "unrolled" into a flat sector (fan shape).

- **Tangent case:** Cone touches the Earth along one parallel (standard parallel).
- **Secant case:** Cone cuts through the Earth along two parallels (two standard parallels), minimizing distortion over a broad band.

**Examples:** Lambert Conformal Conic, Albers Equal-Area Conic.

### 1.4.3 Plane (Azimuthal / Zenithal Projections)

A flat plane is placed tangent to (or secant through) the Earth at a single point. The projection is radial from this point.

**Examples:** Stereographic, Gnomonic, Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area, Orthographic.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Visual Intuition:** Cylindrical = "Earth in a paper towel roll", Conical = "Ice cream cone on Earth", Azimuthal = "Glass plate placed on Earth". Each yields a different geometry of distortion on the unrolled map.

---

## 1.5 Properties of Map Projections

### 1.5.1 Conformality (Orthomorphism)

A projection is **conformal** if it preserves **local shapes and angles**. This means that at any point on the map, the scale is the same in all directions (isotropic scale), and meridians intersect parallels at right angles. The scale factor varies from point to point, but the **angular relationships** are preserved.

- **Conformal projections** are essential for **navigation** and **aeronautical charts**.
- Example: **Mercator** (used by Google Maps, marine navigation).

### 1.5.2 Equivalence (Equal-Area)

A projection is **equal-area** (or equivalent) if it preserves **relative areas**. The area of any region on the map is proportional to its actual area on Earth. Shapes are distorted, but areas are accurate.

- Essential for **thematic mapping** (population density, land use, deforestation).
- Example: **Albers Equal-Area Conic** (used by USGS for US maps).

### 1.5.3 Equidistance

A projection is **equidistant** if it preserves **distances** from a specific point or along specific lines (e.g., all meridians). True equidistance for all points is impossible on a flat map.

- Example: **Equidistant Cylindrical** (Plate Carrée).

### 1.5.4 Azimuthality (True Direction)

A projection is **azimuthal** if it preserves **directions (azimuths)** from a central point to all other points.

- Example: **Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area**, **Azimuthal Equidistant** (used by UN flag emblem).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** No projection can be simultaneously conformal AND equal-area (except for the trivial case of a globe). This is a fundamental result of cartography derived from **Tissot's Indicatrix** (an ellipse that shows distortion at a point). For a sphere, Tissot's Indicatrix is a circle (no angular distortion) for conformal maps and an ellipse with constant area for equal-area maps — these two conditions are mutually exclusive.

---

## 1.6 Tissot's Indicatrix — The Diagnostic Tool

**Tissot's Indicatrix** is an infinitesimally small circle drawn on the Earth's surface. When projected onto the map, it becomes an **ellipse** whose shape and size reveal the nature and magnitude of distortion:

- **Circle on map** = Conformal (only at the chosen point, scale may vary).
- **Ellipse with constant area** = Equal-Area.
- **Ellipse with variable shape** = Compromise projection.
- **Elongated ellipse** = High distortion in one direction.

This is a standard analytical tool in cartography and a **favorite KTU 2-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Mathematical Framework of Projections

A map projection is fundamentally a pair of functions:

$$X = f_1(\phi, \lambda)$$

$$Y = f_2(\phi, \lambda)$$

Where:
- $\phi$ = geodetic latitude
- $\lambda$ = longitude (measured from the central meridian)
- $X, Y$ = projected Cartesian coordinates

The **inverse transformation** is:

$$\phi = g_1(X, Y)$$

$$\lambda = g_2(X, Y)$$

For most projections, the transformation is derived from one of the following **geometric models**:

### 2.1.1 Perspective (Geometric) Projections

These are derived by projecting points from the Earth's surface along straight lines emanating from a **specific perspective point**:

- **Gnomonic:** Perspective point at the Earth's center.
- **Stereographic:** Perspective point at the antipode of the tangent point.
- **Orthographic:** Perspective point at infinity (parallel rays).

### 2.1.2 Analytic (Mathematical) Projections

These are constructed by specifying desired properties (e.g., conformality, equal-area) and solving the resulting differential equations. Example: **Lambert Conformal Conic** is derived by solving the condition that Tissot's indicatrix remains a circle.

### 2.1.3 Modification / Composite Projections

These combine properties of multiple projections or apply modifications to reduce distortion. Example: **Miller Cylindrical** (modified Mercator), **Robinson Projection** (compromise).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Projections

### 2.2.1 Mercator Projection (Conformal Cylindrical)

- **Developable Surface:** Cylinder tangent at the Equator.
- **Standard Parallel:** Equator (0°).
- **Key Property:** Conformal — preserves angles and local shapes.
- **Distortion:** Severe area distortion near the poles (Greenland appears larger than Africa, though Africa is 14× larger in reality).

**Mathematical Form (for a sphere of radius R):**

$$x = R \cdot \lambda$$

$$y = R \cdot \ln\left[\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$$

**Use Cases:** Marine navigation (rhumb lines appear as straight lines), web maps (Google Maps, Bing).

### 2.2.2 Transverse Mercator (TM)

- **Developable Surface:** Cylinder tangent along a **meridian** (instead of the Equator).
- **Key Property:** Conformal; minimal distortion along the central meridian and within a narrow strip ±3° of it.
- **Use Cases:** UTM, Gauss-Krüger, national grids of many countries (including India for high-precision surveys).

### 2.2.3 Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM)

- **Developable Surface:** Cylinder secant to the Earth along two small circles ~180 km on each side of the central meridian.
- **Scale Factor at Central Meridian:** **0.9996** (less than 1 — this is a secant projection).
- **Zone System:** The Earth is divided into **60 zones**, each 6° of longitude wide, numbered 1 to 60 eastward from the 180° meridian.
- **Latitude Bands:** The Earth is divided into 20 latitude bands (C to X, excluding I and O), each 8° tall.
- **Origin:** For each zone, the origin is at the intersection of the central meridian and the Equator, with a **false easting of 500,000 m** and a **false northing of 0 m (Northern Hemisphere) or 10,000,000 m (Southern Hemisphere)**.

**Kerala lies in UTM Zones 43N (most of Kerala) and 44N (eastern parts).**

### 2.2.4 Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC)

- **Developable Surface:** Cone secant to the Earth along two standard parallels.
- **Key Property:** Conformal; excellent for mid-latitude regions with east-west extent.
- **Use Cases:** Aviation charts, weather maps, USA state maps.

### 2.2.5 Albers Equal-Area Conic

- **Key Property:** Equal-area.
- **Use Cases:** Thematic maps, atlas maps of countries/continents.

### 2.2.6 Polyconic Projection

- **Developable Surface:** A series of cones, one for each parallel — hence "poly" (many) "conic".
- **Use Cases:** Historically used by the **Survey of India** (India and Adjacent Countries Polyconic) and USGS quadrangle maps.
- **Key Property:** Each parallel is a circular arc of correct length; the central meridian is a straight line.

### 2.2.7 Stereographic Projection (Azimuthal)

- **Developable Surface:** Plane tangent to the sphere at a single point.
- **Perspective Point:** At the antipode of the tangent point.
- **Key Property:** Conformal; preserves circles as circles (a unique property).
- **Use Cases:** Polar regions (Arctic/Antarctic maps), geological mapping, photogrammetry.

### 2.2.8 Gnomonic Projection (Azimuthal)

- **Perspective Point:** Center of the Earth.
- **Key Property:** All great circles appear as **straight lines**.
- **Distortion:** Extreme near the edges; cannot represent a hemisphere cleanly beyond 90° from the center.
- **Use Cases:** Navigation across oceans (great circle routes plotted and then transferred to Mercator).

### 2.2.9 Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area (LAEA)

- **Key Property:** Equal-area AND azimuthal from the central point.
- **Use Cases:** Continental maps (Africa, Europe, Australia in many atlases), UN emblem.

### 2.2.10 Robinson Projection

- **Key Property:** A compromise projection — neither conformal nor equal-area, but visually pleasing.
- **Use Cases:** World maps in National Geographic (1988-1998).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All formulas below use $\vert$ for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Vertical pipe `|` is never used inside table cells.

### 2.3.1 Core Coordinate System Formulas

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $f = \frac{a - b}{a}$ | Flattening of the spheroid | $a$ = semi-major axis (m), $b$ = semi-minor axis (m) |
| $e^2 = \frac{a^2 - b^2}{a^2}$ | First eccentricity squared | Dimensionless, $0 \leq e^2 < 1$ |
| $e'^2 = \frac{a^2 - b^2}{b^2}$ | Second eccentricity squared | Dimensionless |
| $N = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1 - e^2 \sin^2\phi}}$ | Radius of curvature in the prime vertical | $N$ in meters, $\phi$ in radians |
| $M = a(1 - e^2)\left(1 - e^2\sin^2\phi\right)^{-3/2}$ | Radius of curvature in the meridian | $M$ in meters |
| $R = \sqrt{M \cdot N}$ | Gaussian mean radius of curvature | $R$ in meters |

### 2.3.2 Mercator Projection (Sphere) — Forward Equations

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $x = R \cdot \lambda$ | Easting (linear scale of longitude) |
| $y = R \cdot \ln\left[\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$ | Northing (secant-based formula) |
| $\Delta\lambda = \lambda - \lambda_0$ | Longitude difference from central meridian |

### 2.3.3 UTM Specific Parameters

| Parameter | Value / Formula |
|---|---|
| Zone Width | $6°$ longitude |
| Zone Number | $Z = \lfloor\frac{\lambda + 180}{6}\rfloor + 1$ |
| Central Meridian | $\lambda_0 = (Z - 1) \cdot 6 - 180 + 3$ |
| Scale Factor ($k_0$) | $0.9996$ |
| False Easting | $500,000$ m |
| False Northing (N. Hemisphere) | $0$ m |
| False Northing (S. Hemisphere) | $10,000,000$ m |

### 2.3.4 Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC) — Forward Equations (Sphere)

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $n = \ln\left(\frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right) / \ln\left(\frac{\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_2}{2}\right)}{\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_1}{2}\right)}\right)$ | Cone constant |
| $F = \frac{\cos\phi_1 \cdot \tan^n\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi_1}{2}\right)}{n}$ | Scale factor for the projection |
| $\rho = F \cdot \tan^n\left(\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Polar radius |
| $\rho_0 = F \cdot \tan^n\left(\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\phi_0}{2}\right)$ | Reference polar radius |
| $x = \rho \cdot \sin(n \cdot \Delta\lambda)$ | Easting |
| $y = \rho_0 - \rho \cdot \cos(n \cdot \Delta\lambda)$ | Northing |

### 2.3.5 Distortion Formulas

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $h = \frac{\text{Length on Map}}{\text{Length on Ground}} \cdot \text{Denominator}$ | Representative Fraction (RF) |
| $k = \frac{\text{Map Distance}}{\text{Ground Distance}}$ | Scale factor (at a point) |
| $\omega = 2 \arcsin\left(\frac{a - b}{a + b}\right)$ | Maximum angular distortion (Tissot) |

### 2.3.6 Tissot's Indicatrix Semiaxes

For a general projection, Tissot's ellipse semiaxes ($a_T, b_T$) are given by:

$$a_T^2 = \frac{E}{2}\left(1 + \sqrt{1 - \frac{G^2}{E^2}}\right)$$

$$b_T^2 = \frac{E}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{G^2}{E^2}}\right)$$

Where $E$ and $G$ are the **first** and **second fundamental quantities** derived from the projection's partial derivatives. For **conformal** maps: $a_T = b_T$ (circle). For **equal-area** maps: $a_T \cdot b_T = \text{const}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use Case | Typical Projection |
|---|---|---|
| **Cadastral / Land Records (India)** | Plot-level survey, ownership | Kalyanpur Datum + Polyconic / UTM |
| **Urban Planning** | Master plans, zoning | UTM (state-specific zone) / State Plane |
| **Aviation** | Aeronautical charts | Lambert Conformal Conic |
| **Marine Navigation** | Sea routes, rhumb lines | Mercator |
| **Geology & Mining** | Borehole data, lease boundaries | Local Transverse Mercator / UTM |
| **Forestry** | Forest cover, biomass | Albers Equal-Area Conic |
| **Climate Science** | Global temperature grids | Robinson / Winkel Tripel |
| **GPS / Smartphones** | Real-time positioning | WGS 84 + UTM (in background) |
| **Disaster Management** | Flood / cyclone mapping | UTM / State Plane |
| **Defence (India)** | Military grid | WGS 84 / UTM |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Engineering Context:** For civil engineering projects in Kerala (NH, railway, metro), the standard is **WGS 84 datum** with **UTM Zone 43N** (CM 75°E). Earlier SOI topomaps (1:50,000) used **Everest 1830** datum and **Polyconic Projection**, requiring datum transformation before overlay.

---

## 2.5 Datum Transformation

When two datasets are in different datums, a transformation is required. The simplest is the **3-parameter (Helmert) transformation**:

$$X_t = T_x + (1 + s) \cdot R_x(\theta_x) R_y(\theta_y) R_z(\theta_z) \cdot X_s$$

Where:
- $X_t$ = target coordinates
- $X_s$ = source coordinates
- $T_x, T_y, T_z$ = translations (in meters)
- $\theta_x, \theta_y, \theta_z$ = rotations (in arc-seconds, converted to radians)
- $s$ = scale difference (dimensionless, in ppm)

For higher accuracy, a **7-parameter transformation** (Bursa-Wolf model) is used, which adds 3 rotations and 1 scale factor to the 3 translations. The Indian Kalyanpur → WGS 84 transformation typically uses a 5-parameter model (3 translations + 2 rotations) for SOI data.

---

## 2.6 Conceptual Process Flow

The end-to-end process of working with a projected coordinate system in GIS involves:

1. **Define the Spheroid** (a, b, f)
2. **Select the Datum** (geocentric or local)
3. **Choose the Projection Type** (Cylindrical, Conic, Azimuthal)
4. **Set Projection Parameters** (central meridian, standard parallels, false easting/northing, scale factor)
5. **Apply the Forward Equations** to convert $(\phi, \lambda) \rightarrow (E, N)$
6. **Validate** using ground control points
7. **Document** the CRS in metadata (EPSG code, e.g., EPSG:32643 for WGS 84 / UTM Zone 43N)

> [!NOTE]  
> **EPSG Codes:** The **European Petroleum Survey Group (EPSG)** maintains a database of CRS definitions. Each CRS has a unique integer code. Examples: **EPSG:4326** = WGS 84 GCS, **EPSG:32643** = WGS 84 / UTM Zone 43N, **EPSG:24383** = Kalianpur 1975 / UTM Zone 43N.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Radius of Curvature in the Prime Vertical (N)

**Objective:** Derive the expression for $N$, the radius of curvature in the direction perpendicular to the meridian, as a function of latitude $\phi$.

**Geometric Setup:** Consider an oblate spheroid with semi-major axis $a$ and semi-minor axis $b$. At a point P on the spheroid at geodetic latitude $\phi$, the meridian plane contains the Earth's axis, and the prime vertical plane is perpendicular to it.

**Step 1 — Parametric Representation of the Ellipsoid**

A meridian is an ellipse. In the meridian plane, the X-axis points along the equatorial radius, and the Z-axis along the polar radius. The parametric form of the ellipse is:

$$X = a \cdot \cos\beta$$

$$Z = b \cdot \sin\beta$$

where $\beta$ is the **parametric latitude** (reduced latitude).

**Step 2 — Relate $\beta$ to $\phi$ (Geodetic Latitude)**

The geodetic latitude $\phi$ is the angle between the normal to the spheroid and the equatorial plane. From differential geometry, the relationship is:

$$\tan\beta = \frac{b}{a} \cdot \tan\phi = (1 - f) \cdot \tan\phi$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Normal Vector**

The outward unit normal to the ellipsoid at $(\beta)$ is given by:

$$\hat{n} = \left(\frac{\cos\beta}{a}, \frac{\sin\beta}{b}\right) \cdot \text{normalization factor}$$

After algebraic manipulation (see any geodesy text), the components of the normal vector projected onto the equatorial plane (which is the projection of the normal onto the X-axis) yield the **prime vertical radius of curvature**.

**Step 4 — Final Result**

The prime vertical radius of curvature $N$ is the distance from the spheroid surface to the Z-axis along the normal direction. The result is:

$$N = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1 - e^2 \sin^2\phi}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification**

At the Equator ($\phi = 0°$): $N = a$ ✓ (the normal lies entirely in the equatorial plane, reaching the Z-axis at distance $a$).
At the Pole ($\phi = 90°$): $N = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1 - e^2}} = \frac{a^2}{b}$ ✓ (larger than $a$, the cross-section of the polar curvature is widest here).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mercator Projection (Sphere) — Rhumb Line Property

**Objective:** Derive the Mercator y-coordinate formula from the conformal (angle-preserving) condition.

**Step 1 — The Conformality Condition**

For a projection to be conformal, the infinitesimal scale must be the same in all directions. In the $(\phi, \lambda)$ plane on the sphere, the actual arc lengths are:

$$ds_{\phi} = R \cdot d\phi \quad \text{(along meridian)}$$

$$ds_{\lambda} = R \cdot \cos\phi \cdot d\lambda \quad \text{(along parallel)}$$

The infinitesimal map distances are $dx$ and $dy$. Conformality requires:

$$\frac{dx}{ds_{\lambda}} = \frac{dy}{ds_{\phi}} = k(\phi, \lambda)$$

Since we align the X-axis with the Equator and Y-axis with the central meridian, the simplest choice is:

$$x = R \cdot \lambda$$

This satisfies conformality along the Equator.

**Step 2 — Solve for y**

From the conformality condition:

$$\frac{dx}{R \cos\phi \cdot d\lambda} = \frac{dy}{R \cdot d\phi}$$

Substituting $x = R\lambda$ gives $\frac{dx}{d\lambda} = R$:

$$\frac{R}{R \cos\phi} = \frac{dy}{R \cdot d\phi}$$

$$dy = \frac{R \cdot d\phi}{\cos\phi} = R \cdot \sec\phi \cdot d\phi$$

**Step 3 — Integrate**

$$y = R \int \sec\phi \, d\phi = R \cdot \ln\left[\sec\phi + \tan\phi\right] + C$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sec\phi + \tan\phi = \tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$:

$$y = R \cdot \ln\left[\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right] + C$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Condition**

Set $y = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (Equator): $\ln[\tan(\pi/4)] = \ln(1) = 0$, so $C = 0$.

**Step 5 — Final Mercator Formulas**

$$x = R \cdot \lambda$$

$$y = R \cdot \ln\left[\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$$

This is the **sphere-based** Mercator projection. The ellipsoidal version (used in practice) is more complex, using the **isometric latitude** instead of the geographic latitude.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: UTM Zone Number and Central Meridian

**Objective:** Given a longitude $\lambda$, derive the UTM zone number $Z$ and central meridian $\lambda_0$.

**Step 1 — Zone Definition**

UTM divides the world into 60 zones of 6° longitude each, starting at $\lambda = -180°$ (Zone 1 starts at $-180°$ to $-174°$).

**Step 2 — Zone Number Formula**

The first zone spans $[-180°, -174°)$. The general formula is:

$$Z = \left\lfloor\frac{\lambda + 180°}{6°}\right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3 — Central Meridian Formula**

The central meridian of zone $Z$ is at the midpoint of the zone, which is $3°$ offset from the western boundary:

$$\lambda_0 = -180° + 6° \cdot (Z - 1) + 3° = 6° \cdot Z - 183°$$

Equivalently: $\lambda_0 = -177° + 6° \cdot (Z - 1)$ — but the cleanest form is:

$$\lambda_0 = (Z - 1) \cdot 6° - 177°$$

**Numerical Verification:** For Kerala ($\lambda \approx 76°$ E):

$$Z = \left\lfloor\frac{76 + 180}{6}\right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor\frac{256}{6}\right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 42.67 \rfloor + 1 = 42 + 1 = 43$$

So Kerala is in **Zone 43**. The central meridian is:

$$\lambda_0 = (43 - 1) \cdot 6° - 177° = 42 \cdot 6° - 177° = 252° - 177° = 75°$$

Therefore, the central meridian for Kerala is **75°E** ✓ (this matches the standard UTM Zone 43N specification).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Scale Factor Variation in UTM

**Objective:** Derive the scale factor $k$ as a function of the distance from the central meridian in a Transverse Mercator projection.

**Step 1 — Setup**

For a Transverse Mercator projection with a secant cylinder, the scale factor is unity (1.0) at the two lines of intersection (the standard meridians) and less than 1 along the central meridian.

**Step 2 — General TM Scale Formula**

For a Transverse Mercator projection with scale factor $k_0$ at the central meridian, the scale factor at a point easting $E$ (in meters) from the central meridian is approximately:

$$k \approx k_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{E^2}{2 R^2}\right)$$

Where $R$ is the mean radius of the Earth (~6,371,000 m).

**Step 3 — UTM Specific Case**

In UTM, $k_0 = 0.9996$ along the central meridian. The standard meridians (where $k = 1$) are at $\pm 180,000$ m (approximately $\pm 1.6°$ from the central meridian) — though more precisely at $\pm 3°$ from the CM in some references.

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

For a point 100 km east of the central meridian ($E = 100,000$ m):

$$k \approx 0.9996 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{(100,000)^2}{2 \cdot (6,371,000)^2}\right) = 0.9996 \cdot (1 + 0.000123) \approx 0.9997$$

This represents a **distortion of about 30 cm per km**, which is generally acceptable for most engineering applications.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Flattening Calculation for WGS 84

**Objective:** Compute the flattening $f$ of the WGS 84 spheroid.

**Given:**
- Semi-major axis $a = 6,378,137.0$ m
- Semi-minor axis $b = 6,356,752.3142$ m

**Step 1 — Apply the Formula**

$$f = \frac{a - b}{a} = \frac{6378137.0 - 6356752.3142}{6378137.0}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator Calculation**

$$a - b = 6378137.0 - 6356752.3142 = 21384.6858 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Division**

$$f = \frac{21384.6858}{6378137.0} = 0.0033528$$

**Step 4 — Express as Inverse**

$$f = \frac{1}{298.257223563}$$

This is the **official WGS 84 flattening constant**, used universally in GPS computations and modern GIS.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Projection

| Application Scenario | Recommended Projection | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Topographic mapping of India (legacy) | Polyconic (Everest 1830) | SOI standard pre-2000 |
| Topographic mapping of India (modern) | UTM (WGS 84) | International compatibility |
| Web mapping (Google Maps) | Web Mercator (EPSG:3857) | Conformal, cacheable tiles |
| World population maps | Mollweide / Robinson | Equal-area compromise |
| Aviation charts of mid-latitudes | Lambert Conformal Conic | Conformal, good for east-west extent |
| Polar region studies | Stereographic | Conformal, handles high latitudes |
| India cadastral / land records | UTM Zone 43N/44N (WGS 84) or Local TM | Metric, distortion < 1:2500 |
| Ocean navigation | Mercator | Rhumb lines are straight |
| Trans-oceanic flights | Gnomonic (planning) → Mercator (execution) | Great circle routes |
| UN emblem | Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area | Equal-area + true direction from center |
| World climate models | Equal-Area Cylindrical / EASE-Grid | Area-true for mass balance |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — UTM Forward Computation (Sphere Approximation)

**Given:** A point in Kerala with $\phi = 10.0°$ N, $\lambda = 76.5°$ E. Compute its UTM coordinates using sphere approximation (R = 6,371,000 m).

**Step 1 — Determine Zone**

$$Z = \left\lfloor\frac{76.5 + 180}{6}\right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor\frac{256.5}{6}\right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 42.75 \rfloor + 1 = 43$$

**Step 2 — Central Meridian**

$$\lambda_0 = 75° \text{ E}$$

**Step 3 — Longitude Difference**

$$\Delta\lambda = 76.5° - 75° = 1.5° = 0.02618 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Forward TM Equations (Sphere)**

For a sphere-based Transverse Mercator:

$$x = R \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$y = R \cdot \left[\ln\left(\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right)\right] \cdot \text{(scaled for TM)}$$

For more accuracy, we use the full Transverse Mercator series. The leading terms are:

$$x = k_0 \cdot R \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\Delta\lambda^2 \cos^2\phi}{6}\right]$$

$$y = k_0 \cdot R \cdot \left[\phi + \frac{\Delta\lambda^2 \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Substitution**

With $\phi = 10° = 0.17453$ rad, $\cos\phi = 0.98481$, $\sin\phi = 0.17365$:

$$x \approx 0.9996 \cdot 6371000 \cdot 0.02618 \cdot 0.98481 \cdot \left[1 + \frac{(0.02618 \cdot 0.98481)^2}{6}\right]$$

$$x \approx 0.9996 \cdot 6371000 \cdot 0.02579 \cdot 1.000111 \approx 164,253 \text{ m}$$

Adding the false easting:

$$E = 500,000 + 164,253 = 664,253 \text{ m}$$

For the northing (since this is Northern Hemisphere, no false northing needed):

$$y \approx 0.9996 \cdot 6371000 \cdot \left[0.17453 + \frac{(0.02618)^2 \cdot 0.17365 \cdot 0.98481}{2}\right]$$

$$y \approx 0.9996 \cdot 6371000 \cdot [0.17453 + 0.0000586]$$

$$y \approx 0.9996 \cdot 6371000 \cdot 0.17459 \approx 1,111,140 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** The UTM coordinates of the point are approximately $(E, N) = (664,253 \text{ m}, 1,111,140 \text{ m})$ in UTM Zone 43N.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Note:** The above uses sphere approximation. For exam purposes, the sphere-based formulas are sufficient. Production GIS software uses the more complex **ellipsoidal Transverse Mercator** (e.g., the **Redfearn series** or **Karney's method**), but these are not required at the B.Tech level.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Three Developable Surfaces (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This block diagram maps the geometric concept of developable surfaces used in projections. Due to the Mermaid node identifier constraints, all node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters) and labels contain raw uppercase text without formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[EARTH SURFACE] --> B1[SPHERE OR SPHEROID]
    B1 --> C1{CHOOSE DEVELOPABLE SURFACE}
    C1 -->|CYLINDER| D1[CYLINDRICAL PROJECTION]
    C1 -->|CONE| D2[CONICAL PROJECTION]
    C1 -->|PLANE| D3[AZIMUTHAL PROJECTION]
    D1 --> E1[UNROLL CYLINDER INTO RECTANGLE]
    D2 --> E2[UNROLL CONE INTO FAN SECTOR]
    D3 --> E3[KEEP PLANE FLAT]
    E1 --> F1[EXAMPLES: MERCATOR, TM, UTM]
    E2 --> F2[EXAMPLES: LCC, ALBERS, POLYCONIC]
    E3 --> F3[EXAMPLES: STEREOGRAPHIC, GNOMONIC, LAEA]
    F1 --> G1[MAP WITH GRATICULE]
    F2 --> G2[MAP WITH GRATICULE]
    F3 --> G3[MAP WITH GRATICULE]
    G1 --> H1[SPATIAL DATA READY FOR GIS]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
```

**Explanation:** The diagram shows the conceptual pipeline from the curved Earth, through the choice of developable surface, to the unrolled flat map. Each developable surface yields a distinct projection family, but all converge to a usable map.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Coordinate System Hierarchy

This diagram shows the classification hierarchy of coordinate systems in GIS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[COORDINATE REFERENCE SYSTEM CRS] --> Y1[GEOGRAPHIC COORDINATE SYSTEM GCS]
    Z1 --> Y2[PROJECTED COORDINATE SYSTEM PCS]
    Z1 --> Y3[GEOCENTRIC 3D SYSTEM]
    Z1 --> Y4[LOCAL ARBITRARY SYSTEM]
    Y1 --> X1[COMPONENTS: DATUM, ANGULAR UNITS, PRIME MERIDIAN]
    Y2 --> W1[COMPONENTS: GCS, PROJECTION, LINEAR UNITS, PARAMETERS]
    Y3 --> V1[COMPONENTS: X Y Z FROM EARTH CENTER]
    Y4 --> U1[COMPONENTS: ARBITRARY ORIGIN AND ORIENTATION]
    X1 --> T1[EXAMPLE: WGS 1984 EPSG 4326]
    W1 --> T2[EXAMPLE: WGS 84 UTM 43N EPSG 32643]
    V1 --> T3[EXAMPLE: WGS 84 ECEF]
    U1 --> T4[EXAMPLE: SITE GRID IN METERS]
```

**Explanation:** This hierarchy is essential for understanding how a coordinate system is structured. The GCS is the foundation; the PCS builds on it with a projection. A datum is the "anchor" that ties the abstract mathematical spheroid to the physical Earth.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Tissot's Indicatrix and Distortion Types

This diagram maps the four classical distortion patterns in projection, with Tissot's Indicatrix as the diagnostic ellipse.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ORIGINAL SMALL CIRCLE ON EARTH] --> Q1[PROJECTED ONTO MAP]
    Q1 --> R1{WHAT PROPERTY IS PRESERVED}
    R1 -->|CONFORMAL| S1[CIRCLE REMAINS CIRCLE VARIABLE RADIUS]
    R1 -->|EQUAL AREA| S2[ELLIPSE BUT CONSTANT AREA]
    R1 -->|EQUIDISTANT| S3[ELLIPSE WITH ONE RADIUS = 1]
    R1 -->|COMPROMISE| S4[GENERAL ELLIPSE NO INVARIANT]
    S1 --> T1[NO ANGULAR DISTORTION LOCAL]
    S2 --> T2[NO AREA DISTORTION]
    S3 --> T3[NO DISTANCE DISTORTION ALONG GIVEN LINE]
    S4 --> T4[BALANCED VISUAL APPEAL]
    T1 --> U1[EX: MERCATOR]
    T2 --> U2[EX: ALBERS]
    T3 --> U3[EX: AZIMUTHAL EQUIDISTANT]
    T4 --> U4[EX: ROBINSON]
```

**Explanation:** Tissot's Indicatrix is the visual diagnostic tool that reveals the type of distortion at any point on a map. A perfect circle means conformal; a constant-area ellipse means equal-area.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: UTM Zone Layout — Earth's Longitude Division

This block diagram illustrates the 60 UTM zones, with Kerala highlighted in Zone 43N.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ZONE1[ZONE 1 180W TO 174W] --> ZONE10[ZONE 10]
    ZONE10 --> ZONE20[ZONE 20]
    ZONE20 --> ZONE30[ZONE 30]
    ZONE30 --> ZONE40[ZONE 40]
    ZONE40 --> ZONE43[ZONE 43 INCLUDES KERALA]
    ZONE43 --> ZONE44[ZONE 44 EASTERN INDIA]
    ZONE44 --> ZONE50[ZONE 50]
    ZONE50 --> ZONE60[ZONE 60]
    ZONE43 --> DETAIL1[CENTRAL MERIDIAN 75E]
    ZONE43 --> DETAIL2[SCALE FACTOR 0.9996]
    ZONE43 --> DETAIL3[FALSE EASTING 500000 M]
    ZONE43 --> DETAIL4[FALSE NORTHING 0 N HEMISPHERE]
    ZONE1 --> POLE1[POLAR REGIONS USE UPS]
    ZONE60 --> POLE2[POLAR REGIONS USE UPS]
```

**Explanation:** UTM uses 6° zones, with Kerala falling in Zone 43N. The poleward regions (above 84°N and below 80°S) use the **Universal Polar Stereographic (UPS)** system instead of UTM.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Datum Transformation Pipeline (Helmert)

This diagram shows the workflow for transforming coordinates between datums (e.g., Kalyanpur → WGS 84).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[SOURCE COORDINATES IN DATUM A] --> A1[CONVERT GEOGRAPHIC TO GEOCENTRIC ECEF]
    A1 --> A2[APPLY 7 PARAMETER HELMERT TRANSFORMATION]
    A2 --> A3[TX TY TZ TRANSLATIONS]
    A2 --> A4[RX RY RZ ROTATIONS]
    A2 --> A5[S SCALE]
    A3 --> A6[COMPUTE TARGET ECEF]
    A4 --> A6
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[CONVERT BACK TO GEOGRAPHIC]
    A7 --> A8[TARGET COORDINATES IN DATUM B]
    A8 --> A9[IF NEEDED APPLY PROJECTION]
    A9 --> RESULT[FINAL PROJECTED COORDINATES]
```

**Explanation:** Datum transformation is essential when integrating legacy Indian data (Everest/Kalyanpur) with modern GPS data (WGS 84). The Helmert transformation shifts and rotates the coordinate axes to align the two reference frames.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6: Indian Coordinate Reference System Evolution

This block diagram tracks the evolution of Indian CRS standards for engineering and surveying.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[PRE 1957 EVEREST 1830 SOI MAPS] --> H2[1957 KALYANPUR 1975 REVISED EVEREST]
    H2 --> H3[2000 ONWARDS WGS 84 ADOPTED FOR GPS]
    H3 --> H4[2020 NGIS NATIONAL GEOSPATIAL POLICY UTM WGS 84 MANDATORY]
    H1 --> P1[POLYCONIC PROJECTION LAMBERT CONFORMAL CONIC]
    H2 --> P2[POLYCONIC OR LCC DEPENDING ON REGION]
    H3 --> P3[UTM 43N 44N 45N 46N 47N]
    H4 --> P4[UTM AND INDIA NAP 1975 GRID MODERN]
    P1 --> NOTE[SHIFTS OF 200 TO 700 M POSSIBLE BETWEEN OLD AND NEW]
    P2 --> NOTE
    P3 --> NOTE
    P4 --> NOTE
```

**Explanation:** India's geospatial infrastructure has transitioned from local datums (Everest) to global standards (WGS 84). The shift can cause positional discrepancies of hundreds of meters in legacy data if not properly transformed.

---

## 4.7 Diagram 7: Workflow for Selecting a Projection

This diagram provides a decision-flow for choosing an appropriate projection for a given engineering task.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[WHAT IS YOUR STUDY AREA EXTENT] --> Q2[LOCAL CITY SCALE LESS THAN 100 KM]
    Q2 --> Q3[USE UTM OR LOCAL TM]
    Q1 --> Q4[REGIONAL STATE SCALE 100 TO 1000 KM]
    Q4 --> Q5[USE UTM OR LCC IF EAST WEST EXTENT]
    Q1 --> Q6[CONTINENTAL OR WORLD SCALE]
    Q6 --> Q7[USE EQUAL AREA: ALBERS, LAMBERT AZIMUTHAL, MOLLWEIDE]
    Q3 --> R1[NEED SHAPE PRESERVATION: USE CONFORMAL TM]
    Q3 --> R2[NEED AREA PRESERVATION: USE LOCAL EQUAL AREA]
    Q5 --> R3[AVIATION: LCC]
    Q5 --> R4[NAVIGATION: MERCATOR]
    Q7 --> R5[POLAR: STEREOGRAPHIC]
    Q7 --> R6[WORLD ATLAS: ROBINSON OR WINKEL TRIPEL]
```

**Explanation:** Projection selection depends on (a) study area extent, (b) property to preserve (shape vs area vs distance), and (c) purpose (navigation vs thematic vs analytical). This flowchart is a useful decision aid for KTU practical assignments.

---

## 4.8 Diagram 8: Data Flow in a GIS with Projections

This diagram illustrates how spatial data flows through projection systems within a GIS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INPUT DATA SHP GEOJSON RASTER] --> B1[IDENTIFY CRS METADATA]
    B1 --> C1{CRS KNOWN}
    C1 -->|YES| D1[ASSIGN EPSG CODE]
    C1 -->|NO| D2[USE CONTROL POINTS TO DERIVE]
    D1 --> E1[REPROJECT TO COMMON CRS]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[APPLY ANALYSIS OR OVERLAY]
    F1 --> G1[GENERATE OUTPUT MAP OR PRODUCT]
    G1 --> H1[EXPORT WITH CRS METADATA]
    H1 --> END[FINAL DELIVERABLE]
```

**Explanation:** In a GIS workflow, data is often collected in different CRSs. A "common CRS" must be assigned before any spatial analysis (overlay, intersection, buffer) to avoid positional errors. The EPSG code is the lingua franca for CRS identification.
