Discuss its applications in civil engineering. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A buffer is a polygon (or zone) of a specified width drawn around a point, line, or polygon feature at a uniform or variable distance. **Types of Buffers (with descriptions):** 1. **Fixed-Distance Buffer** - Constant width on all sides. - Used for regulatory setbacks, e.g., 100 m around a heritage building. 2. **Variable-Distance Buffer** - Width depends on an attribute value. - Example: Louder siren → wider no-construction zone. 3. **Multiple-Ring (Concentric) Buffer** - Several buffers at different distances. - Example: 1 km, 2 km, 5 km rings around a fire station. 4. **Doughnut Buffer (Ring Buffer)** - Annular zone between two distances. - Example: Zone between 500 m and 1 km from a river. 5. **Setback Buffer (One-sided)** - Buffer applied on only one side of a line. - Example: 50 m on the right-of-way side of a highway. **Schematic (Text):** ``` Fixed Buffer Multiple-Ring Buffer ┌───┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ ● │ │ ┌───────┐ │ └─┬─┘ │ │ ┌───┐ │ │ ───┴─── │ │ │ ● │ │ │ │ │ └───┘ │ │ │ │ R2 │ │ │ └───────┘ │ │ R3 │ └─────────────┘ ``` **Applications in Civil Engineering:** | Application | Buffer Use | |---|---| | Highway design | Setback from road centreline | | Bridge construction | No-build zone along river banks | | Environmental impact | Sensitive-zone protection around lakes | | Urban planning | Green-belt demarcation | | Pipeline laying | Safety corridor around gas lines | | Flood-plain mapping | 100-year flood buffer | *(Marks: 1 for definition + 3 for buffer types with diagrams + 3 for applications = 7)* --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **raster overlay** technique of spatial analysis. With reference to a **Weighted Linear Combination (WLC)** for **landfill site suitability**, write the model equation and explain each term. **Model Answer:** **Raster Overlay Definition:** Raster overlay is a cell-by-cell operation in which two or more raster layers are mathematically combined (e.g., added, multiplied, weighted-summed) to produce a new raster layer representing some derived phenomenon such as suitability, risk, or index. **Weighted Linear Combination (WLC) for Landfill Suitability:** $$ S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_i $$ where - $S$ = final suitability score at a given cell (range typically 1–9 or 0–100). - $n$ = number of input criteria layers (e.g., slope, soil, geology, distance to road, distance to settlement, depth to water table). - $w_i$ = weight (relative importance) of the $i$-th criterion, satisfying the constraint $$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1 $$ - $r_i$ = reclassified score (rating) of criterion $i$ at the cell, with higher = more suitable (or vice versa depending on convention). **Reclassification of $r_i$:** Each input raster is reclassified into a common suitability scale (e.g., 1 = least suitable, 9 = most suitable) before combination. **Example for Landfill:** | Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Score $r_i$ Range | |---|---|---| | Distance from water body | 0.25 | 1–9 | | Depth to water table | 0.20 | 1–9 | | Slope (gentle preferred) | 0.15 | 1–9 | | Soil permeability (low preferred) | 0.20 | 1–9 | | Distance from settlement | 0.10 | 1–9 | | Distance from road | 0.10 | 1–9 | | **Sum of weights** | **1.00** | — | **Interpretation of Output:** Cells with $S \ge 7$ are "Highly Suitable", $5 \le S < 7$ are "Moderately Suitable", and $S < 5$ are "Not Suitable" for landfill. **Limitations:** Weights are often subjective (analyst bias); compensates poorly (a strong score in one factor can offset a weak score in another); does not account for fuzzy/uncertain boundaries. *(Marks: 2 for raster overlay concept + 3 for WLC equation and terms + 2 for the worked example and limitations = 7)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Where B.Tech Students Lose Marks in Spatial Analysis Questions (KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern):** > > 1. **Confusing Buffer with Multi-ring Buffer** — a 1-mark loss if the diagram is missing concentric rings. > 2. **Forgetting the formula constraint** $\sum w_i = 1$ in WLC — examiners deduct 1 mark explicitly for "missing weight constraint." > 3. **Skipping the choice of overlay type** — always state whether you are using Union, Intersection, or Difference **before** writing the procedure. > 4. **Using `|` for absolute value in answer sheets** — although it is allowed in written exams, the **markdown/PDF auto-graded assignments** will break. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. > 5. **Confusing Local, Focal, Zonal, Global** raster operations — this single confusion costs 2–3 marks. > 6. **Forgetting to mark units** on final answers (metres, hectares, degrees). > 7. **Writing only the algorithm name (e.g., "Dijkstra") without steps** — full mark requires 4–5 numbered steps. > 8. **Omitting the table in vector overlay answers** — Venn-style diagrams are mandatory for 7-mark sub-parts. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Spatial analysis = Where + What + How far + Why together?** - [x] Five pillars: **Query, Measurement, Proximity, Overlay, Network**. - [x] Raster operations: **Local, Focal, Zonal, Global** — memorise the distinction. - [x] Five vector overlays: **Union (OR), Intersection (AND), Difference (NOT), Sym Diff (XOR), Identity**. - [x] **Buffer types:** Fixed, Variable, Multiple-Ring, Doughnut, Setback. - [x] **Network solvers:** Route, Service Area, Closest Facility, OD Matrix, VRP, Location-Allocation. - [x] **Euclidean distance:** $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ — always show derivation. - [x] **NDVI range:** $[-1, +1]$; formula $(\text{NIR} - \text{Red}) / (\text{NIR} + \text{Red})$. - [x] **IDW:** exact interpolator, weight $= d_{i0}^{-p}$, $p \in [1, 3]$, cannot model anisotropy. - [x] **WLC constraint:** $\sum w_i = 1$ — write this **every time**. - [x] **Dijkstra complexity:** $O((V + E) \log V)$ with a binary heap. - [x] **Moran's I range:** $[-1, +1]$; positive = clustered, negative = dispersed. - [x] **Kernel density** depends on bandwidth $h$ — too small = noisy, too large = over-smoothed. - [x] **Always include a labelled diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — diagrams are worth 2–3 marks standalone. - [x] **Mention real-world application** at the end of every long answer — examiners reward "engineering relevance." - [x] **Planar vs Geodesic** — use geodesic for distances greater than **10 km** (KTU board-favoured rule of thumb). - [x] **Slope formula:** $\text{slope} = \arctan(\Delta z / \Delta x)$ — units in degrees or percent rise. - [x] **Aspect formula:** $\arctan(\partial z / \partial y) / (-\partial z / \partial x)$ — output in compass degrees 0°–360°. - [x] **Hot-spot analysis** uses **Getis-Ord Gi*** — high positive z-score = hot spot. - [x] **Interpolation vs Extrapolation** — IDW/Kriging should NEVER extrapolate outside the sampled extent. > [!TIP] > **Final 30-Second Mnemonic for Spatial Analysis:** > **"Q-M-P-O-N-R"** → **Q**uery, **M**easurement, **P**roximity, **O**verlay, **N**etwork, **R**aster. The "R" actually contains L–F–Z–G inside it. Master this skeleton and you can answer 80% of the KTU Module 3 paper.

topics.title: Spatial Interpolation Using GIS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spatial Interpolation Using GIS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spatial Interpolation** is the procedure of estimating the value of a spatially continuous variable (attribute) at an unsampled location **s₀**, using the known values of that attribute measured at a set of sampled locations **sᵢ** within the same study area. Formally, it generates a **continuous raster surface** from a set of discrete point measurements, where the output cell value **Z(s₀)** is predicted as a weighted function of neighboring observations **Z(sᵢ)**.

Mathematically, the general interpolation estimator is expressed as:

$$Z(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot Z(s_i)$$

where:
- **Z(s₀)** = predicted attribute value at the unsampled location **s₀**
- **Z(sᵢ)** = observed attribute value at the sampled location **sᵢ**
- **λᵢ** = weight assigned to the *i*-th sample point
- **n** = number of neighboring samples used in estimation

The fundamental **First Law of Geography** (Tobler, 1970) underpins this entire discipline:

> *"Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Temperature Guess Game":** Imagine you are standing in your college campus with a thermometer, but it broke! You have temperature readings from **5 weather stations** scattered around Kerala. To estimate the temperature at your exact location, you intuitively use the **nearest station** and give a **small influence** to the **farther ones**. That is exactly what spatial interpolation does! The nearer a sample point is, the more "say" it gets in estimating the value at your unknown location. GIS automates this guess with rigorous mathematical weights (**λᵢ**) instead of gut-feeling.

## 1.3 Why is Spatial Interpolation Needed in GIS?

In real-world engineering and environmental studies, we almost never have data at **every** point on the Earth's surface. We always have:
- **Soil samples** at a few boreholes
- **Rainfall** measured at a few stations
- **Groundwater levels** at scattered wells
- **Pollution concentrations** at monitoring stations

To produce a **continuous map** (e.g., a rainfall contour map of Kerala), GIS must "fill in" the gaps. That filling process is **spatial interpolation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the PEAGT521 Module 3 framework, students are expected to clearly distinguish between **Deterministic** and **Geostatistical** interpolation methods, and demonstrate competence in the **IDW equation**, **Kriging variogram concepts**, and **TIN-based** terrain modelling.

## 1.4 Spatial Interpolation — Classification Overview

| Class | Sub-Class | Key Methods | Underlying Principle |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Deterministic** | Exact vs Approximating | IDW, Spline, TIN, Nearest Neighbor, Trend Surface | Mathematical functions of distance/direction only |
| **Geostatistical** | Stochastic | Simple Kriging, Ordinary Kriging, Universal Kriging | Probabilistic model (variogram) + spatial autocorrelation |
| **Hybrid / Modern** | Machine Learning | Empirical Bayesian Kriging, Regression Kriging | Combines deterministic + statistical with auxiliary variables |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Interpolation (Step-by-Step)

Every interpolation method in GIS follows a generic four-step pipeline:

1. **Neighborhood Definition:** Identify which sampled points **sᵢ** will influence the prediction at **s₀**. Common choices are:
   - **Fixed radius** (all points within distance **R**)
   - **Fixed count** (e.g., the **8 nearest** points)
   - **Quadrant search** (split direction-wise)
2. **Weight Calculation:** Compute the influence weight **λᵢ** for each selected sample, based on:
   - Distance (for IDW)
   - Spatial autocorrelation (for Kriging)
   - Local geometry (for TIN)
3. **Estimation:** Apply the linear estimator to produce **Z(s₀)**.
4. **Quality Assessment:** Validate using **cross-validation** (leave-one-out) and report **RMSE / MAE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Weighting Matters:** A simple **arithmetic mean** of all points is mathematically possible but ignores the *geography* of the samples. Sophisticated weighting schemes exploit the fact that closer points usually carry more relevant information than distant ones.

## 2.2 Method 1 — Inverse Distance Weighted (IDW) Interpolation

The IDW method assumes that the value at an unsampled point is a **weighted average** of values at sampled points, with weights being **inversely proportional to a power of distance**.

### Conceptual Logic Steps

- **Step A:** For each known point **sᵢ**, compute its Euclidean distance **dᵢ** to the unknown point **s₀**.
- **Step B:** Assign a weight **λᵢ = 1 / dᵢᵖ**, where **p** is the *power parameter* (commonly **p = 1, 2, or 3**).
- **Step C:** Normalize the weights so that they sum to 1: **wᵢ = λᵢ / Σ λⱼ**.
- **Step D:** Compute the prediction as a weighted sum.

### Properties

- **Exact interpolator:** IDW reproduces the observed values exactly at the sample points (no smoothing).
- **Isotropic:** Weights depend only on distance, not on direction.
- **Tunable:** Increasing **p** makes the surface more "spiky" around data points; smaller **p** gives smoother results.
- **Limit:** IDW cannot exceed the maximum or fall below the minimum of the input values — known as the **"bull's-eye" effect** around isolated points.

## 2.3 Method 2 — Triangulated Irregular Network (TIN) Interpolation

TIN is a **vector-based** interpolation technique that constructs a network of **non-overlapping triangles** from the irregularly spaced sample points. Each triangle vertex is a known data point.

- **Step A:** Connect the points using the **Delaunay Triangulation** rule (no point lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle — ensures maximally equilateral triangles).
- **Step B:** Within each triangle, fit a **planar surface** (linear function of *x* and *y*) defined by the three corner values.
- **Step C:** For any query point **s₀**, locate the enclosing triangle, then use the **barycentric coordinates** to compute the interpolated value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> TIN is widely used in **Digital Elevation Model (DEM)** generation from scattered survey points. It is the standard for engineering-grade terrain surfaces in **AutoCAD Civil 3D**, **ArcGIS**, and **QGIS**.

## 2.4 Method 3 — Spline Interpolation

Spline fits a **piecewise polynomial** (typically cubic) function through the data points, minimizing the **bending energy** (second derivative). Two main variants:

- **Regularized Spline:** Produces a smooth, possibly overshooting surface. Good for **gently varying** phenomena.
- **Tension Spline:** Tunes the stiffness; suitable when you want the surface to honor known minima and maxima (e.g., **water table surfaces**).

The **total bending energy** minimized is:

$$E = \iint \left[ \left( \frac{\partial^2 z}{\partial x^2} \right)^2 + 2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 z}{\partial x \partial y} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial^2 z}{\partial y^2} \right)^2 \right] dx\, dy$$

## 2.5 Method 4 — Kriging (Geostatistical Interpolation)

Kriging is the most mathematically rigorous interpolation method, originating from the **mining industry** (developed by Danie G. Krige and formalized by Georges Matheron). Unlike deterministic methods, Kriging treats the spatial attribute as a **regionalized variable** and explicitly models its **spatial autocorrelation** through the **variogram**.

### Core Concepts

- **Regionalized Variable:** A variable that is intermediate between a *truly random* variable and a *completely deterministic* one — it shows spatial structure.
- **Stationarity:** Assumption that the mean and variance of **Z(s)** are constant across the study area (or within defined local neighbourhoods).
- **Variogram:** A function describing how *dissimilarity* between two values increases with the **lag distance h** between them.

### Empirical (Experimental) Variogram

$$\gamma(h) = \frac{1}{2 N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} \left[ Z(s_i) - Z(s_i + h) \right]^2$$

where:
- **γ(h)** = semivariance at lag distance **h**
- **N(h)** = number of sample pairs separated by distance **h**
- **Z(sᵢ), Z(sᵢ + h)** = paired sample values

### Common Theoretical Variogram Models

| Model | Equation | Behaviour |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Spherical** | $\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ \frac{3h}{2a} - \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{h}{a} \right)^3 \right]$ for $h \le a$ | Reaches sill at range **a**; classic for **elevation, soil** |
| **Exponential** | $\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ 1 - e^{-h/a} \right]$ | Approaches sill asymptotically; suitable for **groundwater** |
| **Gaussian** | $\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ 1 - e^{-(h/a)^2} \right]$ | Very smooth near origin; good for **contamination plumes** |
| **Linear** | $\gamma(h) = C_0 + b \cdot h$ | No sill; used for **monotonically varying** fields |

Where:
- **C₀** = nugget effect (micro-scale variation + measurement error)
- **C₀ + C** = sill (total variance)
- **a** = range (distance beyond which samples are independent)

### Ordinary Kriging System

The Kriging weights **λᵢ** are found by solving the **Lagrange-augmented system**:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j \cdot \gamma(s_i, s_j) + \mu = \gamma(s_i, s_0) \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j = 1$$

where **μ** is the **Lagrange multiplier** ensuring unbiasedness. The prediction is:

$$Z^*(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot Z(s_i)$$

The associated **Kriging variance** is:

$$\sigma_K^2(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \cdot \gamma(s_i, s_0) + \mu$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kriging's Unique Edge:** Unlike IDW or Spline, Kriging returns not just a **prediction** but also a **prediction error (variance)** at every location — a critical advantage for risk-based engineering decisions.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical reference** for all PEAGT521 spatial-interpolation numerical problems. Memorize the **IDW equation**, the **variogram**, and the **Kriging unbiasedness constraint**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Use in Exam |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **General Estimator** | $Z(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i Z(s_i)$ | Foundation of all methods |
| 2 | **IDW Weight** | $\lambda_i = \dfrac{1}{d_i^{\,p}}$ | 3- and 7-mark derivations |
| 3 | **IDW Prediction** | $Z(s_0) = \dfrac{\sum \left( Z(s_i) / d_i^{\,p} \right)}{\sum \left( 1 / d_i^{\,p} \right)}$ | Standard estimator for IDW |
| 4 | **Empirical Variogram** | $\gamma(h) = \dfrac{1}{2N(h)} \sum \left[ Z(s_i) - Z(s_i + h) \right]^2$ | Kriging 7-mark derivations |
| 5 | **Kriging System** | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j \gamma(s_i, s_j) + \mu = \gamma(s_i, s_0)$ | Solve matrix for weights |
| 6 | **Unbiasedness** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = 1$ | Mandatory constraint |
| 7 | **Kriging Variance** | $\sigma_K^2 = \sum \lambda_i \gamma(s_i, s_0) + \mu$ | Error estimation |
| 8 | **Spherical Variogram** | $\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ \dfrac{3h}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{h}{a} \right)^3 \right]$, $h \le a$ | Most common in exams |
| 9 | **TIN (Planar)** | $Z = a + bx + cy$ within each triangle | Surface generation |
| 10 | **Spline Energy** | $E = \iint \left[ \left( z_{xx} \right)^2 + 2 \left( z_{xy} \right)^2 + \left( z_{yy} \right)^2 \right] dx\, dy$ | Concept question |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Interpolation Use-Case |
|:---|:---|
| **Civil Engineering** | DEM generation for **highway alignment** and **cut-fill volume** estimation |
| **Environmental Engg.** | Groundwater **contour mapping**, **air quality** surface from monitoring stations |
| **Agricultural Engg.** | **Soil nutrient** and **NDVI** mapping for precision farming |
| **Mining / Geology** | **Ore grade** estimation — the birthplace of Kriging |
| **Urban Planning** | Population density surface, **flood-risk** mapping |
| **Hydrology** | Rainfall surface (**isohyets**) from sparse rain-gauges |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — IDW Equation from First Principles

### Problem Setup

We have **n = 3** sample points with known values, and we need to predict the value at an unsampled location **s₀**. Let the sampled points be labelled **P₁, P₂, P₃**.

**Given data:**

| Point | Coordinates (x, y) | Observed Value Z |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P₁ | (2, 5) | **10** |
| P₂ | (6, 4) | **14** |
| P₃ | (4, 8) | **12** |
| **s₀** (query) | **(5, 6)** | **?** |

We choose **power parameter p = 2**.

### Step 1 — Compute Euclidean Distances

Using the standard Euclidean formula:

$$d = \sqrt{(x_0 - x_i)^2 + (y_0 - y_i)^2}$$

**For P₁ → s₀:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(5 - 2)^2 + (6 - 5)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$$

**For P₂ → s₀:**

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(5 - 6)^2 + (6 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

**For P₃ → s₀:**

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(5 - 4)^2 + (6 - 8)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

### Step 2 — Compute Raw IDW Weights

Using the raw weight formula:

$$\lambda_i = \frac{1}{d_i^{\,p}}$$

with **p = 2**:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{(3.162)^2} = \frac{1}{10.000} = 0.1000$$

$$\lambda_2 = \frac{1}{(2.236)^2} = \frac{1}{5.000} = 0.2000$$

$$\lambda_3 = \frac{1}{(2.236)^2} = \frac{1}{5.000} = 0.2000$$

### Step 3 — Normalize the Weights

Compute the sum of raw weights:

$$\sum \lambda_i = 0.1000 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 = 0.5000$$

Normalize each weight so they sum to 1:

$$w_1 = \frac{0.1000}{0.5000} = 0.20$$

$$w_2 = \frac{0.2000}{0.5000} = 0.40$$

$$w_3 = \frac{0.2000}{0.5000} = 0.40$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.20 + 0.40 + 0.40 = 1.00$. The unbiasedness condition is satisfied. The point P₁, being farthest, gets the **smallest** weight — confirming IDW's spatial logic.

### Step 4 — Compute the IDW Estimate

$$Z^*(s_0) = w_1 Z_1 + w_2 Z_2 + w_3 Z_3$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$Z^*(s_0) = (0.20 \times 10) + (0.40 \times 14) + (0.40 \times 12)$$

$$Z^*(s_0) = 2.00 + 5.60 + 4.80$$

$$\boxed{Z^*(s_0) = 12.40}$$

### Step 5 — Effect of the Power Parameter (Comparative Insight)

If we had chosen **p = 1** (linear IDW), the same procedure yields:

- $\lambda_1 = 1/3.162 = 0.316$
- $\lambda_2 = 1/2.236 = 0.447$
- $\lambda_3 = 1/2.236 = 0.447$
- Sum = 1.210
- Weights: **0.261, 0.369, 0.369**
- Prediction: $(0.261 \times 10) + (0.369 \times 14) + (0.369 \times 12) = 12.16$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As **p** increases, the nearest points dominate the prediction more strongly. A higher **p** is appropriate when the attribute is known to vary sharply over short distances (e.g., **soil pH**). A lower **p** is better for **gradually varying** fields (e.g., **regional temperature**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variogram Computation from Sample Data

### Problem Setup

Six (6) sample points on a **straight transect** at equal spacing of **1 km** (h = 1). Compute the **empirical variogram** for lag distance **h = 1 km**.

**Given measurements (in metres of elevation):**

| Pair | sᵢ (km) | Z(sᵢ) (m) | sᵢ + h (km) | Z(sᵢ + h) (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 102 | 1 | 105 |
| 2 | 1 | 105 | 2 | 110 |
| 3 | 2 | 110 | 3 | 112 |
| 4 | 3 | 112 | 4 | 115 |
| 5 | 4 | 115 | 5 | 117 |

### Step 1 — Compute Squared Differences for Each Pair

$$[Z(s_i) - Z(s_i + h)]^2$$

| Pair | Calculation | Squared Difference |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $(102 - 105)^2$ | **9** |
| 2 | $(105 - 110)^2$ | **25** |
| 3 | $(110 - 112)^2$ | **4** |
| 4 | $(112 - 115)^2$ | **9** |
| 5 | $(115 - 117)^2$ | **4** |

### Step 2 — Sum the Squared Differences

$$\sum [Z(s_i) - Z(s_i + h)]^2 = 9 + 25 + 4 + 9 + 4 = 51$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Empirical Variogram Formula

The number of pairs at lag **h = 1** is **N(h) = 5**.

$$\gamma(1) = \frac{1}{2 N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} \left[ Z(s_i) - Z(s_i + h) \right]^2$$

$$\gamma(1) = \frac{51}{2 \times 5} = \frac{51}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma(1) = 5.10 \; m^2}$$

### Step 4 — Repeat for Lag h = 2 km (Multi-Step Extension)

For **h = 2 km**, recompute the pairs (now fewer are available):

| Pair | Calculation | Squared Difference |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $(102 - 110)^2$ | **64** |
| 2 | $(105 - 112)^2$ | **49** |
| 3 | $(110 - 115)^2$ | **25** |
| 4 | $(112 - 117)^2$ | **25** |

$$\gamma(2) = \frac{1}{2 \times 4} (64 + 49 + 25 + 25) = \frac{163}{8} = 20.375 \; m^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $\gamma(1) = 5.10 < \gamma(2) = 20.38$. Semivariance **increases** with lag, confirming **spatial autocorrelation**: nearby elevations are more similar than distant ones. This is the bedrock of Kriging.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ordinary Kriging System for n = 3

### Setup

We have 3 known sample points **Z(s₁), Z(s₂), Z(s₃)** and wish to estimate **Z(s₀)**. Let the semivariances between every pair be denoted **γᵢⱼ** and between each sample and the target as **γᵢ₀**.

### The Lagrangian Minimization

Kriging weights are obtained by **minimizing the estimation variance** subject to the unbiasedness constraint. The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sigma_K^2 - 2\mu \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i - 1 \right)$$

Expanding and minimizing the partial derivatives with respect to **λᵢ** and **μ** yields the system:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 \cdot \gamma_{11} + \lambda_2 \cdot \gamma_{12} + \lambda_3 \cdot \gamma_{13} + \mu &= \gamma_{10} \\
\lambda_1 \cdot \gamma_{21} + \lambda_2 \cdot \gamma_{22} + \lambda_3 \cdot \gamma_{23} + \mu &= \gamma_{20} \\
\lambda_1 \cdot \gamma_{31} + \lambda_2 \cdot \gamma_{32} + \lambda_3 \cdot \gamma_{33} + \mu &= \gamma_{30} \\
\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\gamma_{11} & \gamma_{12} & \gamma_{13} & 1 \\
\gamma_{21} & \gamma_{22} & \gamma_{23} & 1 \\
\gamma_{31} & \gamma_{32} & \gamma_{33} & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\lambda_1 \\ \lambda_2 \\ \lambda_3 \\ \mu
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\gamma_{10} \\ \gamma_{20} \\ \gamma_{30} \\ 1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Although solving the full 4×4 matrix manually is rare in KTU exams, you must be able to **set up the system** and **interpret its structure**. Most marks go to the **variogram component** rather than matrix inversion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Interpolation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison matrix is **high-yield** for KTU 2-mark definition questions and 7-mark "compare and contrast" questions.

| Criterion | IDW | TIN | Spline | Kriging |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Class** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Deterministic | Geostatistical |
| **Exact / Approximating** | Exact | Exact | Exact (regularized) or smooth (tension) | Exact |
| **Weighting Basis** | Distance | Local geometry | Curvature minimization | Variogram (autocorrelation) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Provides Error Estimate?** | No | No | No | **Yes (Kriging variance)** |
| **Handles Anisotropy?** | No (isotropic) | No | No | **Yes (directional variogram)** |
| **Best Suited For** | Uniformly dense data | Terrain surfaces | Smooth phenomena | Sparse, autocorrelated data |
| **Sensitivity to Outliers** | High | Medium | Medium | Low (with robust variogram) |
| **Boundary Effects** | Bull's-eye | Discontinuity at edges | Edge oscillations | Smoother edges |
| **Implementation in QGIS / ArcGIS** | SAGA, Geostatistics | 3D Analyst, QGIS TIN | Geostatistical Analyst | Geostatistical Analyst |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Spatial Interpolation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SPATIAL INTERPOLATION START] --> B{Method Class?}
    B -->|Deterministic| C[Det Methods]
    B -->|Geostatistical| D[Geo Methods]
    B -->|Hybrid ML| E[Modern Methods]

    C --> C1[Nearest Neighbor]
    C --> C2[IDW]
    C --> C3[Spline]
    C --> C4[TIN]
    C --> C5[Trend Surface]

    D --> D1[Simple Kriging]
    D --> D2[Ordinary Kriging]
    D --> D3[Universal Kriging]
    D --> D4[Co-Kriging]

    E --> E1[Empirical Bayesian Kriging]
    E --> E2[Regression Kriging]

    C2 --> F2[Weight equals 1 over d to the power p]
    D2 --> F3[Variogram plus unbiasedness]
    C4 --> F4[Delaunay Triangulation plus planar patch]

    A --> G[Output Continuous Surface Z xy]
    G --> H[Cross Validation RMSE MAE]
    H --> I[KTU Module 3 Result]
```

## 4.2 IDW Workflow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Sample Points shapefile] --> A2[Compute Euclidean Distance d to s zero]
    A2 --> A3[Apply Inverse Power Weight 1 divided by d to p]
    A3 --> A4[Normalize Weights to sum 1]
    A4 --> A5[Weighted Sum to get Z s0]
    A5 --> A6[Generate Raster Surface]
    A6 --> A7[Cross Validation Loop]
    A7 -->|If RMSE acceptable| A8[Final Map Output]
    A7 -->|If RMSE high| A9[Adjust power p or neighborhood]
    A9 --> A2
```

## 4.3 Kriging Workflow — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Sample Data] --> K2[Compute Pairwise Distances h]
    K2 --> K3[Empirical Variogram gamma h]
    K3 --> K4[Fit Theoretical Model Spherical or Exponential]
    K4 --> K5[Extract Nugget Range Sill]
    K5 --> K6[Build Gamma Matrix for Target Point]
    K6 --> K7[Solve Kriging Linear System]
    K7 --> K8[Obtain Weights lambda and Lagrange mu]
    K8 --> K9[Compute Z star s0]
    K8 --> K10[Compute Kriging Variance sigma K squared]
    K9 --> K11[Output Map with Error Surface]
    K10 --> K11
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Method to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Choose Interpolation Method] --> Q2{Is your data densely and uniformly sampled?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is the attribute varying smoothly?}
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Need error estimates at unsampled locations?}

    Q3 -->|Yes| M1[Use Spline]
    Q3 -->|No| M2[Use IDW with moderate p]

    Q4 -->|Yes| M3[Use Kriging]
    Q4 -->|No| M4[Use IDW]

    Q1 --> Q5{Is the surface a terrain DEM?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| M5[Use TIN]

    M1 --> R[Optimal Surface Output]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    M4 --> R
    M5 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note for ArcGIS / QGIS Users:** In **QGIS**, the **SAGA** and **GRASS** plugins provide IDW, Spline, and TIN tools directly. In **ArcGIS**, the **Geostatistical Wizard** (ArcMap) or **Empirical Bayesian Kriging** tool (ArcGIS Pro) is the industry standard. For a **7-mark** KTU question, screen captures of such tools are a high-scoring supplement — but always pair them with the **mathematical formula** used internally.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define spatial interpolation. List any two deterministic interpolation methods used in GIS.**

**Model Answer:**
> Spatial interpolation is the process of estimating the value of a continuous geographic variable at an unsampled location **s₀** using observed values from surrounding sample points **sᵢ**, expressed generally as $Z(s_0) = \sum \lambda_i Z(s_i)$.
>
> Two deterministic methods are:
> 1. **Inverse Distance Weighted (IDW)** — weights are inversely proportional to distance.
> 2. **Spline** — fits a piecewise polynomial minimizing bending energy.
> *(Acceptable alternatives: TIN, Nearest Neighbor, Trend Surface.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a variogram? State its three key parameters.**

**Model Answer:**
> A **variogram** is a function that quantifies the spatial autocorrelation of a regionalized variable by plotting **semivariance γ(h)** against **lag distance h**.
>
> Its three key parameters are:
> 1. **Nugget (C₀)** — micro-scale variation and measurement error at h = 0.
> 2. **Sill (C₀ + C)** — the total variance reached as the variogram plateaus.
> 3. **Range (a)** — the distance beyond which samples are no longer spatially correlated.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** In IDW numericals, students **forget to normalize** the weights. Marks are deducted when $\sum \lambda_i \ne 1$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In variogram problems, students often **miss the factor of ½** in the denominator. Always show the formula before substitution.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **"exact"** vs **"approximating"** interpolators. Kriging and IDW are **exact**; Trend Surface is **approximating**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing TIN as **"TIN interpolation"** without mentioning **Delaunay Triangulation** — you will lose 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not stating the **assumptions** of Kriging (stationarity, intrinsic hypothesis) — examiners specifically look for this in 7-mark questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Inverse Distance Weighted (IDW) interpolation** method. Derive the IDW prediction equation and discuss the effect of the **power parameter p** on the resulting surface. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three monitoring wells in an industrial area have the following **groundwater depth** measurements:

| Well | Coordinates (x, y) in km | Depth to Water Table (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| W₁ | (1, 1) | **8.5** |
| W₂ | (4, 2) | **6.2** |
| W₃ | (2, 5) | **7.4** |

Estimate the **groundwater depth** at the location **P = (3, 3) km** using **IDW with p = 2**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Definition and Logic:**

IDW is a deterministic interpolation method that estimates the value at an unsampled location **s₀** as a **weighted average** of values at surrounding sampled points **sᵢ**. The fundamental assumption is that **points closer to s₀ have greater influence** than distant ones.

**Diagram (described in text):**

- A central point **s₀** surrounded by 5 sample points at varying distances.
- Concentric circles around s₀ indicate the **distance bands**.
- The arrows pointing inward (thicker = larger weight) represent the influence of each point.

**Derivation of the IDW Equation:**

Let **Z(s₀)** be the unknown value and **Z(sᵢ)** the known values at **i = 1, 2, ..., n** sample points. The basic estimator is:

$$Z(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i Z(s_i)$$

To satisfy the **unbiasedness condition** (no systematic over- or under-estimation), the weights must sum to 1:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = 1$$

IDW assigns weights that are **inversely proportional to a power of the Euclidean distance**:

$$\lambda_i = \frac{1}{d_i^{\,p}}$$

where **dᵢ = distance(s₀, sᵢ)** and **p** is the *power parameter* (typically 1 ≤ p ≤ 3).

Normalizing the weights:

$$w_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j} = \frac{1 / d_i^{\,p}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( 1 / d_j^{\,p} \right)}$$

The IDW prediction is therefore:

$$\boxed{Z(s_0) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Z(s_i)}{d_i^{\,p}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{d_i^{\,p}}}}$$

**Effect of the Power Parameter p:**

| Value of p | Resulting Surface | When to Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **p = 1** | Linear, smooth weighting | Slowly varying phenomena (e.g., regional temperature) |
| **p = 2** | Standard quadratic weighting | **Default choice**, most GIS implementations |
| **p ≥ 3** | Nearest-point dominates, sharp "bull's-eyes" | Highly localized phenomena (e.g., soil contamination hotspots) |

As **p → ∞**, IDW converges to the **Nearest Neighbor** method.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean Distances from P(3, 3) to each well:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (3-1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2.828 \; km$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(3-4)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} = 1.414 \; km$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (3-5)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} = 2.236 \; km$$

**Step 2 — Compute Raw Weights (p = 2):**

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{(2.828)^2} = \frac{1}{8.000} = 0.1250$$

$$\lambda_2 = \frac{1}{(1.414)^2} = \frac{1}{2.000} = 0.5000$$

$$\lambda_3 = \frac{1}{(2.236)^2} = \frac{1}{5.000} = 0.2000$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Weights:**

$$\sum \lambda_i = 0.1250 + 0.5000 + 0.2000 = 0.8250$$

**Step 4 — Compute IDW Prediction:**

$$Z(P) = \frac{(0.1250 \times 8.5) + (0.5000 \times 6.2) + (0.2000 \times 7.4)}{0.8250}$$

$$Z(P) = \frac{1.0625 + 3.1000 + 1.4800}{0.8250} = \frac{5.6425}{0.8250}$$

$$\boxed{Z(P) = 6.84 \; m}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The estimated water-table depth at the query point is **6.84 m**, which lies between the values of the closest well **W₂ (6.2 m)** and the other two wells — as physically expected. The closest well W₂ dominates because of the inverse-square weighting, contributing **~55%** to the final estimate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**(a)** What is **Kriging**? Explain the **ordinary Kriging system** of equations. Discuss the role of the **variogram** in Kriging with reference to the **spherical model**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a soil-pH survey along a transect, six samples are taken at **1 km** spacing. The observations are:

| Distance xᵢ (km) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **pH** | 6.0 | 6.4 | 6.8 | 6.6 | 7.0 | 7.3 |

Compute the **empirical variogram values** for lag distances **h = 1 km and h = 2 km**. Comment on the trend observed. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Definition:**
**Kriging** is a geostatistical interpolation method that provides the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimate (BLUE)** of an unsampled location's value, using a variogram-based model of spatial autocorrelation. It was developed by **Danie G. Krige** for mining applications and formalized mathematically by **Georges Matheron** (1963).

**Why Kriging is the "Best":**
- **B**est — minimum estimation variance.
- **L**inear — linear combination of known samples.
- **U**nbiased — $\sum \lambda_i = 1$.
- **E**stimate — produces both a prediction and an error (Kriging variance).

**The Ordinary Kriging System:**

For a set of **n** samples and a target location **s₀**, the Kriging weights **λᵢ** and Lagrange multiplier **μ** satisfy:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j \cdot \gamma(s_i, s_j) + \mu &= \gamma(s_i, s_0) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \\
\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_j &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

The Kriging prediction and variance are:

$$Z^*(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i Z(s_i)$$

$$\sigma_K^2(s_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \gamma(s_i, s_0) + \mu$$

**Role of the Variogram:**

The variogram is the **engine of Kriging** — it quantifies the spatial structure of the variable. Three parameters define a typical variogram:

- **Nugget (C₀):** Variation at scales finer than the sampling interval.
- **Range (a):** Distance beyond which correlation is negligible.
- **Sill (C₀ + C):** The asymptotic variance.

**The Spherical Model:**

For lag **h ≤ a**:

$$\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ \frac{3h}{2a} - \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{h}{a} \right)^3 \right]$$

For **h > a**:

$$\gamma(h) = C_0 + C = \text{sill}$$

The spherical model is widely used in **soil science, geology, and mining** because it captures both the **linear behaviour near the origin** and the **plateau at the sill**, fitting most environmental data well.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Squared Differences for h = 1 km (Five Pairs):**

| Pair | Z(sᵢ) | Z(sᵢ₊₁) | Difference | Squared |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 6.0 | 6.4 | 0.4 | 0.16 |
| 2 | 6.4 | 6.8 | 0.4 | 0.16 |
| 3 | 6.8 | 6.6 | −0.2 | 0.04 |
| 4 | 6.6 | 7.0 | 0.4 | 0.16 |
| 5 | 7.0 | 7.3 | 0.3 | 0.09 |

Sum of squared differences = **0.61**, with N(h) = 5 pairs.

$$\gamma(1) = \frac{0.61}{2 \times 5} = \frac{0.61}{10} = 0.061$$

**Step 2 — Squared Differences for h = 2 km (Four Pairs):**

| Pair | Z(sᵢ) | Z(sᵢ₊₂) | Difference | Squared |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 6.0 | 6.8 | 0.8 | 0.64 |
| 2 | 6.4 | 6.6 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 6.8 | 7.0 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 4 | 6.6 | 7.3 | 0.7 | 0.49 |

Sum = **1.21**, with N(h) = 4 pairs.

$$\gamma(2) = \frac{1.21}{2 \times 4} = \frac{1.21}{8} = 0.15125$$

**Step 3 — Summary of Variogram Values:**

| Lag h (km) | Semivariance γ(h) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **0.0610** |
| 2 | **0.1513** |

**Step 4 — Comment on the Trend:**

The semivariance **increases** with lag distance: $\gamma(2) > \gamma(1)$. This is the **expected monotonic behaviour** of a valid variogram and confirms **positive spatial autocorrelation** — soil pH values 1 km apart are more similar than values 2 km apart. To construct a complete variogram model, additional lags (h = 3, 4, 5 km) would be needed, followed by fitting a **theoretical model** (e.g., spherical) to determine the **nugget, range, and sill**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEAGT521 Module 3: Spatial Interpolation**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Spatial Interpolation** — predicting values at unsampled locations from sampled data.
- **First Law of Geography** (Tobler) — near things are more related.
- **Regionalized Variable** — spatially structured yet stochastic variable.
- **Stationarity** — constant mean and variance within the search neighbourhood.

### 🔢 Must-Memorize Formulas
1. **IDW:** $Z(s_0) = \dfrac{\sum (Z(s_i) / d_i^{\,p})}{\sum (1 / d_i^{\,p})}$
2. **Empirical Variogram:** $\gamma(h) = \dfrac{1}{2N(h)} \sum [Z(s_i) - Z(s_i+h)]^2$
3. **Unbiasedness:** $\sum \lambda_i = 1$
4. **Spherical Model:** $\gamma(h) = C_0 + C \left[ \dfrac{3h}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{h}{a} \right)^3 \right]$

### ⚖️ Method Comparisons
- **IDW** — fast, exact, no error estimate, sensitive to outliers.
- **TIN** — best for terrain, uses Delaunay Triangulation, planar interpolation within each triangle.
- **Spline** — smooth surfaces, minimizes bending energy.
- **Kriging** — geostatistical, **BLUE**, provides **error map** via Kriging variance.

### 🎯 KTU Exam Pointers
- Always show the **formula** before substituting values (valuator looks for this).
- State the **assumption** when using Kriging (stationarity).
- For IDW numericals, **verify** that normalized weights sum to **1.00**.
- For variogram numericals, **never forget the ½** in the denominator.
- 7-mark IDW questions expect: **diagram + derivation + parameter p discussion**.
- 7-mark Kriging questions expect: **definition + BLUE expansion + variogram explanation**.

### 🚫 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Confusing **variogram** with **covariogram**.
- Using **sill** and **nugget** interchangeably (they are different).
- Writing IDW as **"I-D-W"** — always say **"Inverse Distance Weighted"** in full.
- Omitting the **search neighbourhood** discussion in IDW questions.
- Forgetting that **TIN is vector-based** (not raster).

### 🛠️ Software Implementation Reference
| Software | Tool | Method |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **ArcGIS Pro** | Geostatistical Wizard | IDW, Kriging, EBK, Spline |
| **QGIS** | SAGA / GRASS plugins | IDW, TIN, Spline |
| **ERDAS Imagine** | Spatial Modeler | IDW |
| **Google Earth Engine** | reduceToVectors + interpolation | IDW (server-side) |
| **Python (open-source)** | PyKrige, scipy.interpolate | Kriging, RBF, Spline |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crop Acreage Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crop Acreage Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crop Acreage Estimation** is defined as the quantitative process of determining the spatial extent (area) of land occupied by a specific crop or a set of crops during a given crop-growing season, using **multispectral/multitemporal satellite remote sensing data** integrated with **Geographic Information System (GIS)** tools, ground truth observations, and statistical sampling techniques.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme for *Remote Sensing and GIS*, crop acreage estimation is recognized as one of the most important operational applications of remote sensing in **agricultural monitoring, food security planning, and national land-use policy formulation**. Agencies like the **National Remote Sensing Centre (NRSC, ISRO)** and the **Ministry of Agriculture, Government of India** routinely use this technique to forecast crop production, identify crop rotation patterns, and assess drought/flood impacts on standing crops.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine you are a school teacher who wants to know how many students are wearing a blue uniform on a particular day.** Instead of asking every single student, you look at an aerial photograph of the playground. You count the number of small patches of blue color, multiply by the average area each student occupies, and arrive at a fair estimate. **Crop acreage estimation works exactly the same way**, except the "students" are pixels of a specific crop color, and the "playground" is an agricultural field captured by a satellite.

Every satellite image is composed of millions of tiny squares called **pixels**, each covering a fixed ground area (e.g., a Landsat-8 pixel covers **30 m × 30 m = 900 m²**). When we group pixels that "look like" wheat, paddy, or cotton (using their spectral signatures), we get polygons whose area is mathematically computed. This is the heart of crop acreage estimation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Source / Sensor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Spatial resolution of optical sensors** | **10 m – 30 m** | Sentinel-2 (10 m), Landsat-8 (30 m) |
| **Crop spectral reflectance (NIR band)** | **0.4 – 0.55** for healthy vegetation | General vegetation range |
| **NDVI for healthy crop** | **0.6 – 0.9** | Sentinel-2 / Landsat-8 |
| **Minimum mapping unit (MMU)** | **2 – 5 ha** for operational crop inventory | NRSC standards |
| **Acceptable classification accuracy** | **> 85% Overall Accuracy** | KTU 2024 / LCCS standards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 PEAGT521 syllabus (Module 4), students are expected to understand the *methodology of acreage estimation, classification accuracy, sampling design, and the role of temporal (multi-date) imagery* in discriminating crops with similar spectral signatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of Crop Acreage Estimation

The entire workflow can be broken down into **six logical stages** that examiners love to test:

1. **Defining the Study Area and Crop Calendar**
   - The agricultural year is divided into distinct **cropping seasons**: Kharif (June–October), Rabi (November–March), and Zaid (March–June).
   - The crop calendar determines *when* to acquire satellite images (phenological stages like sowing, peak vegetative, heading, harvesting).

2. **Satellite Data Acquisition (Multi-temporal)**
   - Multi-date imagery is essential because different crops show peak greenness at different times.
   - Example: Paddy peaks in August, wheat peaks in February in North India.

3. **Pre-processing (Geometric, Radiometric, Atmospheric Correction)**
   - Removes noise so that pixel values represent true ground reflectance.
   - Geometric correction aligns images to a standard map projection (e.g., UTM, WGS-84).

4. **Image Classification (Pixel-based or Object-based)**
   - **Supervised classification** (Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest)
   - **Unsupervised classification** (ISODATA, K-Means)
   - **Hybrid / Object-based** (using GIS vector overlays)

5. **Accuracy Assessment**
   - Building an **Error (Confusion) Matrix** and computing Producer's, User's, Overall Accuracy, and **Kappa Coefficient**.

6. **Area Computation and Report Generation**
   - Multiplying the count of pixels per class by the area per pixel.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why multi-temporal data?** Crops like wheat and mustard look identical in a single-date image (both are green) but their phenology differs — multi-date data captures this "temporal signature."
- **Why supervised over unsupervised?** Acreage estimation requires *known* crop labels (training data from field visits); unsupervised clustering cannot guarantee crop class identity.
- **Why a confusion matrix?** Pure pixel counts can be misleading; a confusion matrix reveals *where* the classifier confuses one crop with another.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** asked in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for this topic. Memorize these rigorously.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Where Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Area of a crop class** | $A_c = N_c \times A_{pixel}$ | Final area computation |
| **Area per pixel** | $A_{pixel} = (\text{spatial resolution in m})^2$ | Pre-classification step |
| **Overall Accuracy (OA)** | $OA = \dfrac{\sum_i X_{ii}}{N} \times 100\%$ | Accuracy assessment |
| **Producer's Accuracy (PA)** | $PA_i = \dfrac{X_{ii}}{X_{+i}} \times 100\%$ | Measures omission error |
| **User's Accuracy (UA)** | $UA_i = \dfrac{X_{ii}}{X_{i+}} \times 100\%$ | Measures commission error |
| **Commission Error** | $CE_i = 100\% - UA_i$ | Reliability of class |
| **Omission Error** | $OE_i = 100\% - PA_i$ | Completeness of class |
| **Kappa Coefficient ($K$)** | $K = \dfrac{N \sum_i X_{ii} - \sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i})}{N^2 - \sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i})}$ | Agreement beyond chance |
| **Expected Agreement ($P_e$)** | $P_e = \dfrac{\sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i})}{N^2}$ | Intermediate term in Kappa |
| **NDVI (vegetation index)** | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Crop vigor & discrimination |
| **Crop proportion estimate (stratified)** | $\hat{P}_s = \dfrac{\sum_h N_h \hat{P}_h}{\sum_h N_h}$ | Sampling-based estimation |

> *Note: In the table above, $X_{ii}$ = diagonal element (correctly classified pixels), $X_{i+}$ = row total, $X_{+i}$ = column total, $N$ = total reference pixels.*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Government of India (FASAL project):** Forecasting Agricultural output using Space, Agro-meteorology, and Land-based observations.
- **Crop insurance (PMFBY):** Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana uses acreage data to settle claims.
- **Precision agriculture:** Variable rate input application depends on accurate crop area maps.
- **International agencies:** FAO, USDA, World Bank rely on remote-sensing-based acreage stats for global food balance sheets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Area Calculation from Pixel Count

**Given:** A satellite image of spatial resolution $R$ meters covering a region with $N_c$ pixels classified as "Wheat."

**To derive:** Total wheat acreage $A_c$ in hectares (ha).

**Step 1: Compute the ground area covered by a single pixel.**
The pixel is a square with side length equal to the spatial resolution $R$.

$$
A_{pixel} = R \times R = R^2 \;\; (\text{in m}^2)
$$

**Step 2: Convert the unit to hectares for standard reporting.**

$$
1 \text{ hectare} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

Therefore, the area of one pixel in hectares is:

$$
A_{pixel} = \frac{R^2}{10{,}000} \;\; (\text{ha})
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by the number of wheat-classified pixels.**

$$
A_c = N_c \times \frac{R^2}{10{,}000} \;\; (\text{ha})
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A Landsat-8 image ($R = 30$ m) has $N_c = 12{,}500$ pixels classified as "Paddy." Compute the paddy acreage.

$$
A_c = 12{,}500 \times \frac{30^2}{10{,}000}
$$

$$
A_c = 12{,}500 \times \frac{900}{10{,}000}
$$

$$
A_c = 12{,}500 \times 0.09
$$

$$
\boxed{A_c = 1{,}125 \text{ hectares}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Kappa Coefficient from a Confusion Matrix

**Given:** A confusion matrix for a 3-class crop classification problem (Wheat, Paddy, Mustard) with $N$ ground-truth samples.

**The confusion matrix is structured as:**

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} & X_{13} \\ X_{21} & X_{22} & X_{23} \\ X_{31} & X_{32} & X_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $X_{ij}$ = number of ground-truth samples of class $i$ classified as class $j$.

**Step 1: Compute the observed agreement (overall accuracy).**

$$
P_o = \frac{\sum_i X_{ii}}{N} = \frac{X_{11} + X_{22} + X_{33}}{N}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the expected agreement by chance.**
The expected count in cell $(i,j)$ is the product of the row total and column total divided by $N$.

$$
E_{ij} = \frac{(X_{i+}) \times (X_{+j})}{N}
$$

where $X_{i+} = \sum_j X_{ij}$ and $X_{+j} = \sum_i X_{ij}$.

**Step 3: Sum the expected diagonal elements.**

$$
P_e = \frac{\sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i})}{N^2}
$$

**Step 4: The Kappa statistic normalizes the agreement beyond chance.**

$$
K = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}
$$

**Substituting the explicit forms:**

$$
K = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\sum_i X_{ii}}{N}\right) - \left(\dfrac{\sum_i X_{i+} X_{+i}}{N^2}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{\sum_i X_{i+} X_{+i}}{N^2}\right)}
$$

**Multiplying numerator and denominator by $N^2$:**

$$
\boxed{K = \frac{N \sum_i X_{ii} - \sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i})}{N^2 - \sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i})}}
$$

**Interpretation Scale (KTU standard):**

| Kappa Value | Agreement Strength |
| :--- | :--- |
| $K \leq 0.20$ | Poor |
| $0.20 < K \leq 0.40$ | Fair |
| $0.40 < K \leq 0.60$ | Moderate |
| $0.60 < K \leq 0.80$ | Substantial |
| $0.80 < K \leq 1.00$ | Almost perfect |

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Producer's and User's Accuracy

**Producer's Accuracy** measures how well the reference pixels of class $i$ are classified (the producer's perspective — completeness):

$$
PA_i = \frac{X_{ii}}{X_{+i}} \times 100\%
$$

**User's Accuracy** measures the reliability of a mapped class — if a user picks a pixel labelled "Wheat," how often is it really wheat?

$$
UA_i = \frac{X_{ii}}{X_{i+}} \times 100\%
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Mini Confusion Matrix):**

A 2-class problem (Wheat vs. Non-Wheat) with the following matrix:

| Classified as → | Wheat | Non-Wheat | Row Total ($X_{i+}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference: Wheat** | 45 | 5 | 50 |
| **Reference: Non-Wheat** | 10 | 40 | 50 |
| **Column Total ($X_{+i}$)** | 55 | 45 | **N = 100** |

**Solution:**

- Overall Accuracy: $OA = \dfrac{45 + 40}{100} \times 100\% = 85\%$
- Producer's Accuracy (Wheat) $= \dfrac{45}{50} \times 100\% = 90\%$
- User's Accuracy (Wheat) $= \dfrac{45}{55} \times 100\% = 81.82\%$
- Omission Error (Wheat) $= 100\% - 90\% = 10\%$
- Commission Error (Wheat) $= 100\% - 81.82\% = 18.18\%$

**Step-by-step Kappa Calculation:**

$$
\sum_i X_{ii} = 45 + 40 = 85
$$

$$
\sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i}) = (50 \times 55) + (50 \times 45) = 2750 + 2250 = 5000
$$

$$
K = \frac{(100 \times 85) - 5000}{100^2 - 5000} = \frac{8500 - 5000}{10000 - 5000} = \frac{3500}{5000} = 0.70
$$

**Result:** $K = 0.70$ → **Substantial agreement** (within KTU's standard interpretation scale).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Workflow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end operational pipeline** of crop acreage estimation as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Problem Definition and Crop Calendar] --> A1[Multi-temporal Satellite Data Acquisition]
    A1 --> A2[Radiometric and Geometric Pre-processing]
    A2 --> A3[Ground Truth Data Collection via GPS Field Surveys]
    A3 --> A4[Delineation of Training Sites for Each Crop]
    A4 --> A5[Supervised Classification MLC or SVM or RF]
    A5 --> A6[Post-classification Smoothing and GIS Overlay]
    A6 --> A7[Accuracy Assessment Using Confusion Matrix]
    A7 --> A7a[Compute OA PA UA and Kappa]
    A7a --> A8{Is Accuracy Greater Than 85 Percent?}
    A8 -- No --> A9[Refine Training Data and Reclassify]
    A9 --> A5
    A8 -- Yes --> A10[Pixel Count per Crop Class]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Area using N times R squared]
    A11 --> A12[Final Crop Acreage Map and Statistical Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop between node A8 and A5 is the **iterative refinement loop** — a high-yield concept frequently tested in KTU essay questions. Examiners reward students who explicitly mention that classification is *not* a one-shot process.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Accuracy Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Reference Pixels from Field] --> B2[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    B3[Classified Pixels from Map] --> B2
    B2 --> B4[Diagonal Sum Equals Correctly Classified]
    B2 --> B5[Row and Column Totals]
    B4 --> B6[Compute Overall Accuracy]
    B5 --> B7[Compute Producer Accuracy]
    B5 --> B8[Compute User Accuracy]
    B4 --> B9[Compute Kappa Coefficient]
    B5 --> B9
    B6 --> B10[Final Accuracy Report]
    B7 --> B10
    B8 --> B10
    B9 --> B10
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **dual input** to the confusion matrix — both the *reference (ground truth)* pixels and the *classified (map)* pixels are mandatory. A common student error is to forget one of these inputs, which is why KTU examiners specifically test the **input sources** of the confusion matrix.

### 4.3 Multi-temporal Crop Discrimination Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Paddy and Wheat look identical in October Image] --> C2[Acquire November Image]
    C2 --> C3[Acquire February Image]
    C3 --> C4[Build Temporal NDVI Profile]
    C4 --> C5[Paddy Peak in August Wheat Peak in February]
    C5 --> C6[Successful Crop Discrimination]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define crop acreage estimation. List any two conventional (non-remote-sensing) methods used for estimating crop area.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Crop acreage estimation is the process of quantitatively determining the spatial extent of land covered by a specific crop during a given growing season, using satellite remote sensing data and ground truth.
Two conventional methods are:
1. **Complete Enumeration (Census Method):** Field-to-field survey by village-level workers covering every parcel.
2. **Sample Survey Method:** Random or stratified random sampling of fields and statistical extrapolation to the whole region.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two methods = 3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the Kappa coefficient in remote sensing classification? State its range and significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Kappa coefficient ($K$) is a statistical measure of classification accuracy that accounts for agreement occurring *by chance*. It is calculated from the confusion matrix.
**Range:** $-1 \leq K \leq 1$ (in practice usually $0 \leq K \leq 1$, where 1 = perfect agreement and $\leq 0$ = no better than random).
**Significance:** Unlike overall accuracy, Kappa discounts the chance agreement and provides a robust, single-value summary of classifier performance. A $K \geq 0.80$ is considered "almost perfect" for operational crop inventory.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark range + 1 mark significance = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> A large number of students **lose 3–4 marks** in acreage estimation questions for the following reasons:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert m² to hectares** in area calculations (always use $1$ ha $= 10{,}000$ m²).
> 2. **Confusing Producer's Accuracy with User's Accuracy** — Producer uses the *column* total, User uses the *row* total.
> 3. **Writing only the overall accuracy** and skipping the Kappa coefficient — examiners expect *both* to be reported for full 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Not mentioning multi-temporal data** — single-date imagery is almost always inadequate for crop discrimination; this is a *must-mention* in any methodology answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the step-by-step methodology of crop acreage estimation using satellite remote sensing. Discuss the role of multi-temporal data with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* The following confusion matrix was obtained for a wheat vs. non-wheat classification problem. Compute the **Overall Accuracy, Producer's Accuracy, User's Accuracy, and Kappa Coefficient** for the wheat class. **(7 marks)**

| Classified as → | Wheat | Non-Wheat | Row Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference: Wheat** | 48 | 2 | 50 |
| **Reference: Non-Wheat** | 8 | 42 | 50 |
| **Column Total** | 56 | 44 | **N = 100** |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Define the study area and crop calendar** (1 mark): Identify Kharif/Rabi/Zaid seasons, dominant crops, and acquisition windows.
2. **Acquire multi-temporal satellite data** (1 mark): Use Sentinel-2 / Landsat-8 at peak vegetative, heading, and harvesting stages to capture phenological differences.
3. **Pre-processing** (1 mark): Geometric and radiometric correction, atmospheric normalization.
4. **Ground truth and training site selection** (1 mark): GPS-based field visits to collect crop labels for representative fields.
5. **Supervised classification** (1 mark): Apply Maximum Likelihood / SVM / Random Forest classifier to label every pixel.
6. **Post-classification refinement and area computation** (1 mark): Compute $A_c = N_c \times R^2 / 10{,}000$ hectares.
7. **Accuracy assessment** (1 mark): Confusion matrix, OA, PA, UA, and Kappa.

**Role of multi-temporal data (additional 1 mark from within the 7):**
Multi-temporal data is essential because crops like wheat and mustard share similar spectral signatures in a single-date image. By observing NDVI peaks at different times, the classifier can separate them. *(Attach the temporal NDVI concept diagram from Section 4.3.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given matrix:
- $X_{11} = 48$ (Wheat → Wheat, correct)
- $X_{12} = 2$ (Wheat → Non-Wheat, omission)
- $X_{21} = 8$ (Non-Wheat → Wheat, commission)
- $X_{22} = 42$ (Non-Wheat → Non-Wheat, correct)
- Row totals: $X_{1+} = 50$, $X_{2+} = 50$
- Column totals: $X_{+1} = 56$, $X_{+2} = 44$
- $N = 100$

**Step 1: Overall Accuracy (2 marks)**
$$
OA = \frac{X_{11} + X_{22}}{N} \times 100\% = \frac{48 + 42}{100} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

**Step 2: Producer's Accuracy for Wheat (2 marks)**
$$
PA_{Wheat} = \frac{X_{11}}{X_{+1}} \times 100\% = \frac{48}{56} \times 100\% = 85.71\%
$$

**Step 3: User's Accuracy for Wheat (1 mark)**
$$
UA_{Wheat} = \frac{X_{11}}{X_{1+}} \times 100\% = \frac{48}{50} \times 100\% = 96\%
$$

**Step 4: Kappa Coefficient (2 marks)**
$$
\sum_i X_{ii} = 48 + 42 = 90
$$

$$
\sum_i (X_{i+} \times X_{+i}) = (50 \times 56) + (50 \times 44) = 2800 + 2200 = 5000
$$

$$
K = \frac{(100 \times 90) - 5000}{100^2 - 5000} = \frac{9000 - 5000}{10000 - 5000} = \frac{4000}{5000} = 0.80
$$

**Final Result:** $OA = 90\%$, $PA_{Wheat} = 85.71\%$, $UA_{Wheat} = 96\%$, $K = 0.80$ (Substantial/Almost perfect agreement).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model 2024]**
*(a)* Describe the **components of a confusion matrix** and explain how Overall Accuracy, Producer's Accuracy, User's Accuracy, and Kappa Coefficient are derived from it. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A Sentinel-2 image (spatial resolution 10 m) is used to map a district. The classification result shows **25,000 pixels** labelled as "Paddy." Calculate the paddy acreage in hectares. Also, briefly explain why multi-date imagery is preferred over a single date for crop discrimination. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of confusion matrix** (1 mark): A square table comparing classified pixels with reference (ground-truth) pixels.
2. **Diagonal elements** ($X_{ii}$) (1 mark): Correctly classified pixels.
3. **Off-diagonal elements** ($X_{ij}$, $i \neq j$) (1 mark): Misclassification — row totals give user's accuracy denominator, column totals give producer's accuracy denominator.
4. **Overall Accuracy derivation** (1.5 marks): $OA = \sum X_{ii} / N \times 100\%$.
5. **PA derivation** (1 mark): $PA_i = X_{ii} / X_{+i}$ — based on column total.
6. **UA derivation** (1 mark): $UA_i = X_{ii} / X_{i+}$ — based on row total.
7. **Kappa derivation** (0.5 marks): $K = (P_o - P_e)/(1 - P_e)$, which corrects for chance agreement.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the spatial resolution and pixel area** (1 mark)
$$
R = 10 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{pixel} = 10^2 = 100 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute total area in m²** (1.5 marks)
$$
A_{total} = N_c \times A_{pixel} = 25{,}000 \times 100 = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Convert to hectares** (1.5 marks)
$$
A_c = \frac{2{,}500{,}000}{10{,}000} = 250 \text{ hectares}
$$

**Final Answer:** Paddy acreage = **250 hectares**.

**Why multi-date imagery? (3 marks):**
- Crops with similar greenness (e.g., wheat and barley) are spectrally inseparable on a single date.
- Multi-date data captures the **temporal NDVI profile** (phenology) which acts as a unique "fingerprint" for each crop.
- For example, paddy peaks in August (monsoon) while cotton peaks in October; wheat peaks in February.
- This temporal signature allows the classifier to separate crops with >85% accuracy, which is the operational threshold set by NRSC for national crop inventory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Crop acreage estimation = quantifying the area of crops using remote sensing + GIS.
- **Why remote sensing?** Objective, synoptic, repetitive, cost-effective compared to manual census.
- **Spatial resolution rule:** A pixel covers $R \times R$ m²; in hectares it is $R^2 / 10{,}000$.
- **Sensors used (KTU syllabus):** Landsat-8 (30 m), Sentinel-2 (10 m), Resourcesat-2 LISS-III (23.5 m), AWIFS (56 m).
- **Key spectral bands:** Red, NIR — basis for NDVI.
- **NDVI formula:** $\dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ — values between **0.6 and 0.9** indicate healthy crops.
- **Classification methods:** Supervised (preferred) vs. Unsupervised (used when training data is scarce).
- **Classifiers commonly used:** Maximum Likelihood, Minimum Distance, Parallelepiped, SVM, Random Forest.
- **Confusion matrix — golden rules:**
   - Rows = Reference (ground truth).
   - Columns = Classification (map).
   - Diagonal = Correct.
   - Column total → denominator of Producer's Accuracy.
   - Row total → denominator of User's Accuracy.
- **Kappa benchmark:** $K \geq 0.80$ = "Almost perfect" for operational use.
- **Multi-date rule:** Minimum **2 dates** (peak vegetative + harvesting) for most crops; **3–4 dates** for complex Rabi/Kharif mixtures.
- **Accuracy target:** Overall Accuracy **> 85%** with **Kappa > 0.80**.
- **Government projects:** FASAL, CHAMAN, NADAM, Crop Cutting Experiments (CCE) — all rely on acreage data.
- **Sampling-based area estimation:** $\hat{A} = N_{sample} \times (\text{mean class proportion})^{-1} \times A_{stratum}$.
- **Unit conversions to remember:**
   - 1 hectare = 10,000 m² = 2.471 acres
   - 1 km² = 100 hectares = 247.1 acres
- **Common crops and their peak NDVI months in India:**
   - **Paddy (Kharif):** August–September
   - **Wheat (Rabi):** February
   - **Sugarcane:** June–October (long duration)
   - **Cotton:** August–October
- **Exam tips:**
   - Always state *both* OA and Kappa in accuracy answers.
   - Always convert pixel area to hectares in the final step.
   - Always mention *multi-temporal* data when asked "which type of imagery is used."
   - Always draw a labeled workflow diagram (full marks require a diagram in long essays).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Crop Water Requirement Using Thermal Remote Sensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Crop Water Requirement Using Thermal Remote Sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Crop Water Requirement (CWR)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** is the total volume of water needed by a crop to grow optimally from sowing to harvest, accounting for evaporative losses, transpiration through leaves, and the soil moisture profile used by the root zone.

Formally, in the **FAO-56 Irrigation & Drainage Paper** framework (adopted universally and referenced by KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus), the net crop water requirement is expressed as:

$$\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0$$

where $\text{ET}_0$ is the **reference evapotranspiration** (from a hypothetical grass surface) and $K_c$ is the dimensionless **crop coefficient** that varies with crop phenology (initial, development, mid-season, late-season stages).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thermal remote sensing does NOT measure water directly. It measures **surface temperature ($T_s$)** and **surface reflectance**, which are then fed into physically-based models to back-calculate water flux (latent heat) — i.e., how much water the crop is *actually* transpiring.

## 1.2 What is Thermal Remote Sensing?

**Thermal Infrared (TIR) Remote Sensing** is the measurement of electromagnetic radiation emitted by the Earth's surface in the wavelength window of approximately **8 µm to 14 µm** (the atmospheric window). The intensity of this emitted radiation is governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, allowing retrieval of kinetic surface temperature:

$$M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

where $M$ is radiant exitance (W m⁻²), $\varepsilon$ is **emissivity** (0 < $\varepsilon$ < 1), $\sigma$ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴** is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is the absolute surface temperature in Kelvin.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fever Thermometer" of the Earth 🍃

Imagine you are a doctor checking the **fever** of a patient. You don't measure the disease itself — you measure **body temperature** as an *indicator* of what's happening inside.

A field of crops works exactly the same way:

- A **well-watered crop** transpires efficiently, behaving like a **sweating athlete**. This evaporative cooling keeps the leaf canopy **cool** (typically 5–10 °C below air temperature at midday).
- A **water-stressed crop** closes its stomata, **stops transpiring**, and behaves like a **feverish patient**. The canopy temperature **rises** sharply above air temperature.

**Thermal remote sensing is the thermal camera that measures this "crop fever."** By comparing the canopy temperature to a reference (air temperature, or to "cold" wet pixels and "hot" dry pixels), we can deduce *how much water the crop is actually using* — without ever touching the soil.

> [!TIP]
> The fundamental insight: **Colder canopy ⇒ more water use; Hotter canopy ⇒ less water use.** Everything in SEBAL, METRIC, and CWSI flows from this elegant principle.

## 1.4 Why Thermal RS for Crop Water?

Traditional CWR methods (lysimeters, Bowen ratio, eddy covariance) are **point measurements**. Thermal satellite sensors like **Landsat-8 TIRS (100 m), Landsat-9 TIRS-2, MODIS (1 km), ECOSTRESS (ISS, 70 m), and Sentinel-3 SLSTR (1 km)** provide **spatially distributed** estimates at field-to-regional scales — something impossible with point instruments.

---

# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
*(Reserved for derivations — see SECTION_3 block further down.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 2.1 The Surface Energy Balance (SEB) — Theoretical Backbone

At the land surface, available radiant energy is partitioned into three turbulent and conductive fluxes. Conservation of energy demands:

$$R_n = G + H + \lambda E$$

where:
- $R_n$ = **Net Radiation** (W m⁻²) — energy available at the surface
- $G$ = **Soil Heat Flux** (W m⁻²) — energy conducted into the soil
- $H$ = **Sensible Heat Flux** (W m⁻²) — energy heating the air
- $\lambda E$ = **Latent Heat Flux** (W m⁻²) — energy used for **evapotranspiration** (the water flux we want)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crop water requirement is recovered from the latent heat flux:
> $$\text{ET}_{\text{instantaneous}} = \frac{\lambda E}{\lambda \rho_w}$$
> where $\lambda$ ≈ **2.45 MJ kg⁻¹** is the latent heat of vaporization, and $\rho_w$ = **1000 kg m⁻³** is water density. The instantaneous value is then extrapolated to daily ET using the **evaporative fraction (EF)** or **reference ET fraction** technique.

## 2.2 Logic Flow — How Thermal RS Estimates ET

1. **Acquire thermal imagery** → retrieve land surface temperature ($LST$ or $T_s$) per pixel.
2. **Compute $R_n$** from broadband albedo, LST, and incoming shortwave/longwave radiation.
3. **Estimate $G$** as an empirical fraction of $R_n$ (function of $T_s$, NDVI, and surface type).
4. **Estimate $H$** using wind speed, air temperature, and aerodynamic resistance — but this requires *internal calibration* via extreme pixels (SEBAL/METRIC trick).
5. **Compute $\lambda E$** as the **residual** of the energy balance.
6. **Convert $\lambda E$ → ET (mm/hr)** and scale to daily/seasonal water use.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann emission law; $T$ in K | W m⁻² |
| 2 | $\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0$ | Actual crop ET; $K_c$ crop coefficient (0.3 – 1.2) | mm day⁻¹ |
| 3 | $R_n = (1 - \alpha) R_s \downarrow + R_L \downarrow - R_L \uparrow$ | Net radiation; $\alpha$ = surface albedo, $R_s$ shortwave, $R_L$ longwave | W m⁻² |
| 4 | $R_n = G + H + \lambda E$ | Surface energy balance | W m⁻² |
| 5 | $G \approx c_G \cdot R_n$ | Soil heat flux; $c_G$ = 0.05 (full veg) to 0.30 (bare soil) | W m⁻² |
| 6 | $H = \rho_a c_p \dfrac{T_0 - T_a}{r_{ah}}$ | Sensible heat; $\rho_a$ air density (1.225 kg m⁻³), $c_p$ = 1013 J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹, $r_{ah}$ aero resistance | W m⁻² |
| 7 | $\lambda E = R_n - G - H$ | Latent heat (residual closure) | W m⁻² |
| 8 | $\text{ET} = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda E}{\lambda \rho_w}$ | Instantaneous ET (per second to hour) | mm hr⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\Lambda = \dfrac{\lambda E}{R_n - G}$ | **Evaporative Fraction (EF)** — self-preservation diurnal constant (≈ 0.7–0.9 well-watered) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{CWSI} = \dfrac{(T_c - T_a) - (T_c - T_a)_{ll}}{(T_c - T_a)_{ul} - (T_c - T_a)_{ll}}$ | **Crop Water Stress Index**; subscripts $ll$ lower limit (wet), $ul$ upper limit (dry) | dimensionless (0 – 1) |
| 11 | $T_s = \dfrac{T_{10} + T_{11}}{2}$ | LST from Landsat-8 split-window bands 10 & 11 | °C or K |
| 12 | $r_{ah} = \dfrac{\ln \left( \dfrac{z_2 - d}{z_{0m}} \right) \ln \left( \dfrac{z_2 - d}{z_{0h}} \right)}{k^2 \, u_z}$ | Aerodynamic resistance; $k$ = 0.41 (von Kármán), $d$ zero-plane displacement, $z_{0m}$, $z_{0h}$ roughness lengths, $u_z$ wind speed at height $z$ | s m⁻¹ |
| 13 | $dT = a + b \cdot T_s$ | **SEBAL dT calibration** — linear anchor between $T_s$ and $(T_0 - T_a)$ from cold & hot pixels | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Pipe symbol avoidance** — in the CWSI formula above, parentheses are used to enclose the lower-limit and upper-limit differences. If you ever see a textbook using $\vert$ for absolute value, replace it with $\vert$ only outside a table, or with `\vert` / `\mid` when typeset in markdown tables (since raw `vert` breaks column separators).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision irrigation scheduling** — drip & sprinkler systems modulated by ET maps (e.g., Jain Irrigation, Netafim).
- **Drought monitoring & insurance** — PMFBY (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana) crop-loss assessment.
- **Canal & reservoir water budgeting** — KW-3, Bhavanisagar, Idukki command area management.
- **Groundwater stress assessment** — detecting over-pumped aquifers (e.g., Punjab, Haryana).
- **Climate-impact studies** — heatwave effects on rabi crops (wheat) in Indo-Gangetic plains.

---

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Derivation: Net Radiation ($R_n$)

The net radiation is the sum of net shortwave and net longwave components:

**Step 1 — Define the components.** Downward shortwave ($R_s \downarrow$) is the solar radiation reaching the surface after atmospheric attenuation. The surface reflects a fraction equal to albedo $\alpha$, so net shortwave is:

$$R_{ns} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot R_s \downarrow$$

**Step 2 — Longwave components.** The atmosphere emits longwave radiation downward ($R_L \downarrow$, sky emission), and the surface emits upward ($R_L \uparrow = \varepsilon_s \sigma T_s^4$). So net longwave is:

$$R_{nl} = R_L \downarrow - \varepsilon_s \sigma T_s^4$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$\boxed{R_n = (1 - \alpha) R_s \downarrow + R_L \downarrow - \varepsilon_s \sigma T_s^4}$$

**Step 4 — Often simplified using $R_{so}$ (clear-sky solar) and an empirical atmospheric emissivity $\varepsilon_a$.** With $\varepsilon_a = 1.08 (-\ln \tau)^{0.265}$ where $\tau$ is atmospheric transmittance:

$$R_n \approx (1 - \alpha) R_s \downarrow + \varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^4 - \varepsilon_s \sigma T_s^4$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensible Heat Flux ($H$) in SEBAL

The aerodynamic transport equation is the starting point:

$$H = \rho_a c_p \dfrac{T_0 - T_a}{r_{ah}}$$

**Problem:** $T_0$ (aerodynamic temperature) is NOT the same as $T_s$ (radiometric surface temperature). Direct substitution introduces 1–3 °C error.

**SEBAL trick — anchor the $dT - T_s$ relationship.** Define the near-surface temperature difference as $dT = T_0 - T_a$. SEBAL assumes $dT$ is a **linear function of $T_s$**:

$$dT = a + b \cdot T_s$$

**Step 1 — Cold pixel (well-watered agriculture).** Here $\lambda E \approx R_n - G$ (so $H_{\text{cold}} = 0$). Solving for $dT_{\text{cold}}$:

$$dT_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{cold}} \cdot r_{ah,\text{cold}}}{\rho_a c_p} = 0$$

Thus the cold pixel gives one linear equation: $0 = a + b T_{s,\text{cold}}$.

**Step 2 — Hot pixel (bare dry soil or severely stressed field).** Here $\lambda E \approx 0$, so $H_{\text{hot}} = R_{n,\text{hot}} - G_{\text{hot}}$ and:

$$dT_{\text{hot}} = \dfrac{(R_{n,\text{hot}} - G_{\text{hot}}) \cdot r_{ah,\text{hot}}}{\rho_a c_p}$$

This provides the second linear equation: $dT_{\text{hot}} = a + b T_{s,\text{hot}}$.

**Step 3 — Solve the two-equation system.**

$$b = \dfrac{dT_{\text{hot}} - dT_{\text{cold}}}{T_{s,\text{hot}} - T_{s,\text{cold}}} = \dfrac{dT_{\text{hot}}}{T_{s,\text{hot}} - T_{s,\text{cold}}}$$

$$a = -b \cdot T_{s,\text{cold}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $H$ for every pixel.**

$$H_i = \rho_a c_p \dfrac{a + b T_{s,i}}{r_{ah,i}}$$

**Step 5 — Close the energy balance.**

$$\lambda E_i = R_{n,i} - G_i - H_i$$

This is the **residual method** — the heart of SEBAL & METRIC.

## 3.3 Derivation: Instantaneous to Daily ET Scaling

The **Evaporative Fraction (EF)** is remarkably stable during daytime (Sugita & Brutsaert, 1991):

$$\Lambda = \dfrac{\lambda E}{R_n - G}$$

**Step 1 — Assume EF is constant from satellite overpass time to sunset.** Daily available energy is:

$$R_{n,24} = (1 - \alpha) R_{s,24} \downarrow + R_{L,24} \downarrow - \varepsilon_s \sigma T_{s,24}^4$$

**Step 2 — Daily latent heat.**

$$\lambda E_{24} = \Lambda \cdot (R_{n,24} - G_{24})$$

**Step 3 — Convert to depth of water (mm/day).**

$$\text{ET}_{24} = \dfrac{\lambda E_{24} \cdot 86\,400}{\lambda \cdot \rho_w}$$

with $\lambda = 2.45 \times 10^6$ J kg⁻¹, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg m⁻³, giving the **86 400** second factor (24 hours × 3600 s).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Major Thermal-RS Methods

| Method | Type | Calibration | Inputs | Strength | Weakness |
|--------|------|-------------|--------|----------|----------|
