| **SEBAL** (Bastiaanssen 1998) | Physical-residual | Cold & hot pixel dT anchor | $T_s$, albedo, NDVI, $R_s$, wind, $T_a$ | Robust, no ground ET needed | Subjective pixel selection |
| **METRIC** (Allen 2007) | Physical-residual | Uses alfalfa $ET_r$ from weather station | Same as SEBAL + measured $ET_r$ | Absolute calibration, sloped terrain | Needs local met data |
| **DisALEXI** | Two-source | Atmospheric constraints | Same | Separates soil/canopy fluxes | Complex |
| **CWSI** (Idso 1981) | Empirical | Theoretical $T_c - T_a$ baselines | $T_c$, $T_a$, VPD | Simple, fast | Empirical, no LE directly |
| **TSEB** (Norman 1995) | Two-source energy | Iterative PT | $T_s$, $T_a$, LAI | Separates soil & canopy | Requires LAI input |
| **STSEB** | Simplified TSEB | Empirical | $T_s$, albedo, NDVI | Operational | Lower accuracy |

---

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 End-to-End SEBAL Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Landsat 8 TIRS Scene] --> B[Convert DN to Radiance]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Correction using ASTER GED]
    C --> D[Retrieve Land Surface Temperature Ts]
    D --> E[Compute Albedo alpha from optical bands]
    E --> F[Compute NDVI from NIR and Red]
    F --> G[Calculate Net Radiation Rn]
    D --> H[Estimate Soil Heat Flux G using NDVI and Ts]
    D --> I[Select Cold Pixel and Hot Pixel]
    I --> J[Anchor dT versus Ts Linear Regression]
    J --> K[Compute Sensible Heat Flux H per pixel]
    G --> L[Close Energy Balance lambdaE = Rn minus G minus H]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Evaporative Fraction EF]
    M --> N[Scale to Daily ET mm per day]
    N --> O[Validate with Field Lysimeter or Eddy Covariance]
    O --> P[Generate Crop Water Requirement Map]
```

## 4.2 Thermal Band → Energy Flux Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Thermal Band 10 10.9 micrometer] --> M1[Radiance Conversion]
    L2[Thermal Band 11 12.0 micrometer] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Atmospheric Correction]
    M2 --> M3[Land Surface Temperature Ts in Kelvin]
    M3 --> N1[Net Radiation Rn]
    M3 --> N2[Soil Heat Flux G]
    M3 --> N3[Sensible Heat Flux H via SEBAL]
    N1 --> R1[Residual lambdaE = Rn minus G minus H]
    N2 --> R1
    N3 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Convert lambdaE to mm per hour ET]
    S1 --> S2[Scale to Daily and Seasonal Crop ET]
```

## 4.3 Energy Partitioning Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Rn[Net Radiation Rn] --> P1{Energy Partition}
    P1 --> P2[Soil Heat Flux G]
    P1 --> P3[Sensible Heat Flux H]
    P1 --> P4[Latent Heat Flux lambdaE equals Water Use]
    P2 --> O1[Heats the soil profile]
    P3 --> O2[Heats the air above canopy]
    P4 --> O3[Evaporates water from soil and leaves]
    O3 --> Q1[High lambdaE equals Well watered field]
    O3 --> Q2[Low lambdaE equals Stressed field]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not photographs?** Mermaid block diagrams give the KTU Board examiner a clear, dependency-aware view of how Landsat thermal bands become a CWR map. This is the most testable representation.

---

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define Crop Water Requirement (CWR). State the FAO-56 equation relating actual crop ET to reference ET.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Crop Water Requirement (CWR) is the total quantity of water a crop needs for its growth, expressed as the depth of water (mm) per unit area per unit time, accounting for evapotranspiration losses under optimal growth conditions. The FAO-56 equation is:

$$\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0$$

where $\text{ET}_c$ = actual crop evapotranspiration, $K_c$ = crop coefficient (dimensionless, varies from 0.3 for bare soil to 1.2 for paddy), and $\text{ET}_0$ = reference evapotranspiration (mm day⁻¹) calculated by Penman-Monteith from a hypothetical grass surface. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI)? State its formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI), developed by Idso et al. (1981), is a normalized indicator of plant water stress derived from canopy-air temperature differences. It ranges from 0 (no stress, well watered) to 1 (maximum stress, no transpiration). The formula is:

$$\text{CWSI} = \dfrac{(T_c - T_a) - (T_c - T_a)_{ll}}{(T_c - T_a)_{ul} - (T_c - T_a)_{ll}}$$

where $(T_c - T_a)_{ll}$ is the lower limit (wet baseline, fully transpiring canopy) and $(T_c - T_a)_{ul}$ is the upper limit (dry baseline, non-transpiring canopy) — both functions of vapour pressure deficit (VPD). (3 marks)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from each module. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Total = 14 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the Surface Energy Balance Algorithm for Land (SEBAL) in detail. With a neat flowchart, describe its major computational steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **Surface Energy Balance Algorithm for Land (SEBAL)**, developed by **W.G.M. Bastiaanssen (1998)**, computes pixel-wise actual evapotranspiration from satellite optical and thermal imagery without requiring ground-based ET measurements. The governing principle is the surface energy balance:

$$R_n = G + H + \lambda E$$

SEBAL solves this for $\lambda E$ as a **residual**:

$$\lambda E = R_n - G - H$$

**Computational steps:**

1. **Image acquisition and pre-processing** — Landsat-8/9 (or MODIS, ECOSTRESS) scenes, conversion of digital numbers to top-of-atmosphere radiance, atmospheric correction.
2. **Land Surface Temperature retrieval** — from TIRS Bands 10 & 11 using split-window or single-channel methods; emissivity derived from NDVI thresholds.
3. **Albedo ($\alpha$)** computed from weighted broadband reflectance of optical bands.
4. **NDVI** computed from NIR and Red bands to derive vegetation fraction, surface roughness, and emissivity.
5. **Net Radiation ($R_n$)** computed using shortwave $(1-\alpha)R_s$ and longwave (atmospheric + surface emission) components.
6. **Soil Heat Flux ($G$)** estimated as $G = c_G \cdot R_n$, where $c_G$ depends on NDVI, $T_s$, and surface type (Bastiaanssen 2000 empirical relation).
7. **Hot and Cold pixel selection** — anchor pixels used to define the linear $dT - T_s$ relationship for sensible heat flux. Hot pixel = dry, bare soil with $\lambda E \approx 0$. Cold pixel = well-irrigated, dense, full-cover crop with $H \approx 0$.
8. **Aerodynamic resistance $r_{ah}$** computed with Monin-Obukhov similarity theory.
9. **Sensible heat flux $H$** computed as $H = \rho_a c_p (a + b T_s) / r_{ah}$ for every pixel.
10. **Latent heat flux $\lambda E$** = residual, converted to ET (mm/hr) and then to daily ET using the Evaporative Fraction method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Landsat-8 TIRS scene over a command area gives the following data:

| Parameter | Cold Pixel | Hot Pixel |
|---|---|---|
| Surface Temperature $T_s$ (K) | 298 | 320 |
| Wind speed $u_2$ (m s⁻¹) | 4.0 | 2.5 |
| Aerodynamic resistance $r_{ah}$ (s m⁻¹) | 50 | 80 |
| $R_n - G$ (W m⁻²) | 400 | 480 |
| Air temperature $T_a$ (K) | 305 | 305 |

Compute the **linear calibration coefficients $a$ and $b$** for SEBAL and the **sensible heat flux** for a third pixel having $T_s$ = 308 K, $r_{ah}$ = 65 s m⁻¹. Take $\rho_a c_p$ = 1230 W s m⁻³ K⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $dT$ for the cold pixel (assumed $H = 0$):**

$$dT_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{cold}} \cdot r_{ah,\text{cold}}}{\rho_a c_p} = \dfrac{0 \cdot 50}{1230} = 0 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $dT$ for the hot pixel (assumed $\lambda E = 0$, so $H = R_n - G$):**

$$H_{\text{hot}} = 480 \text{ W m}^{-2}$$

$$dT_{\text{hot}} = \dfrac{480 \cdot 80}{1230} = \dfrac{38\,400}{1230} = 31.22 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Linear regression $dT = a + b T_s$:**

Two equations:
- Cold: $0 = a + 298 \, b$
- Hot: $31.22 = a + 320 \, b$

Subtract: $31.22 = 22 \, b \Rightarrow b = 1.419 \text{ K/K}$

From cold equation: $a = -298 \times 1.419 = -422.86 \text{ K}$

**Step 4 — Compute $dT$ for the target pixel ($T_s$ = 308 K):**

$$dT = -422.86 + 1.419 \times 308 = -422.86 + 437.05 = 14.19 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Sensible heat flux for target pixel:**

$$H = \dfrac{\rho_a c_p \cdot dT}{r_{ah}} = \dfrac{1230 \times 14.19}{65} = \dfrac{17\,453.7}{65} = 268.5 \text{ W m}^{-2}$$

**Result:** $a = -422.86$ K, $b = 1.419$ K K⁻¹, $H_{\text{target}} \approx 268.5$ W m⁻². (7 marks)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **thermal infrared remote sensing** in estimating crop water requirement. Compare **SEBAL** and **METRIC** models. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Role of thermal remote sensing:**
Thermal infrared (8–14 µm) sensors measure emitted terrestrial radiation governed by Stefan-Boltzmann law, allowing retrieval of land surface temperature ($T_s$). Because $T_s$ is the single most diagnostic variable for crop water status — well-watered canopies are cooler (transpirational cooling), water-stressed canopies are warmer (stomatal closure, reduced $\lambda E$) — thermal data is *indispensable* for spatially distributed ET estimation. Sensors such as **Landsat-8/9 TIRS (100 m)**, **ECOSTRESS (70 m)**, **MODIS (1 km)**, and **Sentinel-3 SLSTR (1 km)** provide this thermal channel globally.

**Role in CWR estimation:**
1. Provides spatial $T_s$ maps for energy balance models.
2. Enables dT calibration (SEBAL) and CWSI computation.
3. Permits diurnal EF extrapolation to daily/monthly ET.

**Comparison SEBAL vs METRIC:**

| Aspect | SEBAL | METRIC |
|---|---|---|
| **Developed by / Year** | Bastiaanssen, 1998 | Allen et al., 2007 |
| **Calibration anchor** | Arbitrary cold/hot pixels | Weather-station based alfalfa $ET_r$ |
| **Absolute ET** | Relative (anchored to extremes) | Absolute (anchored to measured $ET_r$) |
| **Sloped terrain** | Less robust | Includes C-factor for terrain |
| **Inputs** | $T_s$, albedo, NDVI, $R_s$, $u$, $T_a$ | Same + measured $ET_r$ |
| **Accuracy** | ±15% in flat areas | ±10–12% with good met data |
| **Operational use** | Global, less data-hungry | Western US irrigation districts |

Both share the same residual closure of energy balance; METRIC adds absolute calibration, making it preferable for engineering-grade water budgets. (7 marks)

**(b)** A field has the following energy flux measurements at satellite overpass time (11:30 AM IST):

- $R_n$ = 620 W m⁻²
- $G$ = 95 W m⁻²
- $H$ = 180 W m⁻²

Compute: (i) instantaneous latent heat flux $\lambda E$, (ii) instantaneous ET in mm hr⁻¹, (iii) daily ET in mm day⁻¹ assuming daytime average $R_{n,day} - G_{day}$ = 220 W m⁻² and EF is conserved from overpass to sunset. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Instantaneous $\lambda E$:**

$$\lambda E = R_n - G - H = 620 - 95 - 180 = 345 \text{ W m}^{-2}$$

**(ii) Instantaneous ET (mm hr⁻¹):**

$$\text{ET}_{\text{inst}} = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda E}{\lambda \cdot \rho_w}$$

with $\lambda = 2.45 \times 10^6$ J kg⁻¹, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg m⁻³:

$$\text{ET}_{\text{inst}} = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{345}{2.45 \times 10^6 \times 1000} = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{345}{2.45 \times 10^9}$$

$$= 3600 \times 1.408 \times 10^{-7} = 5.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m hr}^{-1} = 0.507 \text{ mm hr}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Daily ET using Evaporative Fraction:**

$$\Lambda = \dfrac{\lambda E}{R_n - G} = \dfrac{345}{620 - 95} = \dfrac{345}{525} = 0.657$$

$$\lambda E_{24} = \Lambda \cdot (R_{n,24} - G_{24}) = 0.657 \times 220 = 144.6 \text{ W m}^{-2}$$

$$\text{ET}_{24} = 86\,400 \cdot \dfrac{144.6}{2.45 \times 10^9} = 86\,400 \times 5.90 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$= 5.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m day}^{-1} = 5.10 \text{ mm day}^{-1}$$

**Result:** $\lambda E$ = 345 W m⁻²; ET_inst ≈ 0.51 mm hr⁻¹; ET_daily ≈ 5.10 mm day⁻¹. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing LST ($T_s$) with aerodynamic temperature ($T_0$)** in the $H$ equation — they differ by 1–3 °C; SEBAL's dT anchor resolves this.
> 2. **Forgetting the 86 400 factor** when scaling to daily ET — students write $\lambda E$ directly as mm, losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing units**: $R_n$ in W m⁻² must be multiplied by 3600 (hourly) or 86 400 (daily) seconds, NOT left as is.
> 4. **Wrong cold pixel assumption**: Many students set $dT_{\text{cold}}$ to air temperature instead of ZERO. Cold pixel means $H = 0$, hence $dT = 0$.
> 5. **Omitting $K_c$ context** — KTU expects you to bridge CWR to FAO $K_c$ framework; pure SEBAL without crop coefficient mention loses a mark.
> 6. **Sign conventions**: In the energy balance, $G$ is positive downward; $\lambda E$ is positive upward — clearly state the convention.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **CWR** = total water crop needs; $\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0$ (FAO-56).
- ✅ **Thermal RS** measures **land surface temperature $T_s$** via Stefan-Boltzmann: $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
- ✅ **Energy balance**: $R_n = G + H + \lambda E$ — closed by **residual**.
- ✅ **SEBAL**: Bastiaanssen, 1998 — uses **cold and hot pixel dT anchor**; relative ET.
- ✅ **METRIC**: Allen, 2007 — uses **alfalfa $ET_r$** for absolute calibration; better for slopes.
- ✅ **CWSI**: Idso, 1981 — empirical; uses VPD-based wet/dry baselines; 0 (no stress) to 1 (max stress).
- ✅ **Evaporative Fraction $\Lambda$** is self-preserved during daytime → enables daily ET extrapolation.
- ✅ **Conversion**: $\text{ET (mm/hr)} = 3600 \cdot \lambda E / (\lambda \rho_w)$; $\text{ET (mm/day)} = 86\,400 \cdot \lambda E_{24} / (\lambda \rho_w)$.
- ✅ **Key sensors**: Landsat-8/9 TIRS (100 m), MODIS (1 km), ECOSTRESS (70 m), Sentinel-3 SLSTR.
- ✅ **Soil heat flux $G$** is 5% of $R_n$ for dense vegetation, up to 30% for bare soil.
- ✅ **Sensible heat $H$** is the *trickiest* term — requires $r_{ah}$ (Monin-Obukhov) and dT anchor.
- ✅ **Albedo $\alpha$** is broadband (visible + NIR + SWIR weighted sum, ≈ 0.20–0.30 for crops).
- ✅ **Validation**: lysimeter, eddy covariance, Bowen ratio stations.
- ✅ **Engineering uses**: precision irrigation, drought insurance (PMFBY), canal water budgeting, groundwater management.
- ✅ **Exam formula to memorize**: $H = \rho_a c_p (a + bT_s) / r_{ah}$ ; $\lambda E = R_n - G - H$ ; $\text{CWSI}$ equation.
- ✅ **Always mention the assumption**: SEBAL/METRIC need **clear-sky**, **near midday** overpass and **adequate wind & temperature data**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crop Condition Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crop Condition Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crop Condition Assessment (CCA)** is the systematic application of multi-temporal, multi-spectral remote sensing data to evaluate the physiological, biochemical, and structural health of agricultural crops over their phenological cycle. It involves the quantitative measurement of biophysical and biochemical parameters—such as leaf area index (LAI), biomass, chlorophyll content, canopy moisture, and photosynthetic efficiency—to detect deviations from normal growth trajectories caused by biotic stress (pests, diseases, weeds) or abiotic stress (drought, salinity, nutrient deficiency, waterlogging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Crop condition assessment forms the operational backbone of **Precision Agriculture** and **Crop Monitoring Systems** under the National Agricultural Drought Assessment and Monitoring Programme. It is the direct application of **Vegetation Indices (VIs)** derived from **Red (R), Near-Infrared (NIR)**, and **Shortwave Infrared (SWIR)** spectral bands.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, CCA is fundamentally an **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** integration of:
- **Electromagnetic Radiation (EMR) – Crop Canopy Interaction** (from Module 2)
- **Digital Image Processing & Indices** (from Module 3)
- **GIS-based spatial decision support** (from Module 5)
- **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) and Satellite platforms** (from Module 4)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Plant Doctor's Check-up"**
> Imagine a human doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor does not just "look" at the patient; they measure vital signs: temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, and oxygen saturation. Each vital sign is a *biophysical proxy* for health.
> 
> A remote sensing scientist performs the **same job on a field of crops**, but instead of a stethoscope, they use a **spectroradiometer or a satellite sensor**. The "vital signs" of a crop are its **spectral reflectance values** in specific wavelength bands. A healthy, photosynthesizing plant reflects strongly in the NIR (like "oxygen saturation") and absorbs in the red (like a "fever" if absorption drops). By computing mathematical combinations of these reflectances, the scientist creates a **Vegetation Index**—the crop's "health report card."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following canonical metrics anchor all crop condition assessment procedures:

- **NDVI range:** $\mathbf{-1.0 \leq NDVI \leq +1.0}$ (Healthy vegetation typically lies between $\mathbf{0.2}$ and $\mathbf{0.9}$)
- **Healthy green leaf NIR reflectance:** $\mathbf{40\% \text{ to } 60\%}$
- **Healthy green leaf Red reflectance:** $\mathbf{5\% \text{ to } 8\%}$
- **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR) band:** $\mathbf{400 \text{ nm to } 700 \text{ nm}}$
- **SWIR absorption trough (water):** centered near **$\mathbf{1450 \text{ nm}}$, $\mathbf{1950 \text{ nm}$, and $\mathbf{2500 \text{ nm}}$**
- **Standard satellite reference:** Landsat 8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, MODIS Terra/Aqua, Resourcesat-2 AWiFS

## 1.4 Why Crop Condition Assessment is Critical in KTU Curriculum

| Aspect | Engineering / Real-World Utility |
|---|---|
| **Food Security** | Pre-emptive drought / famine warning for national policy |
| **Crop Insurance** | Transparent, satellite-based yield loss assessment (PMFBY) |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Variable rate fertilizer & irrigation prescription maps |
| **Climate Change Monitoring** | Detecting phenological shifts and growing season anomalies |
| **Disaster Management** | Rapid mapping of flood-, cyclone-, or pest-affected cropland |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Five Pillars of Crop Condition Assessment

### Pillar 1: Spectral Signature of Healthy vs. Stressed Crops
A **green, healthy, mesophyll-rich leaf** has a very distinctive spectral reflectance curve (often called the "red edge" signature). It is governed by three biophysical components:

1. **Pigments (Chlorophyll a & b, Carotenoids):** Strong absorption in Blue (~450 nm) and Red (~670 nm); high reflectance in Green (~550 nm) — this is why healthy leaves appear green.
2. **Mesophyll Cell Structure (spongy parenchyma):** Causes very high reflectance in the Near-Infrared (NIR) plateau (700–1300 nm) due to internal light scattering.
3. **Water Content:** Produces deep absorption troughs in SWIR (1450 nm, 1950 nm).

> When a crop experiences **stress**, the red reflectance **increases** (chlorophyll breaks down), the NIR reflectance **decreases** (mesophyll collapses), and the SWIR reflectance shifts. This triple change is the *fingerprint* of crop stress.

### Pillar 2: Vegetation Indices (VIs) — The Mathematical Vital Signs
VIs are algebraic combinations of two or more spectral bands designed to enhance the vegetation signal and suppress confounding factors (soil background, atmosphere, sun angle).

### Pillar 3: Crop Phenology Mapping
Phenology is the timing of biological events (sowing → emergence → tillering → heading → maturity). Remote sensing reconstructs **time-series NDVI curves** to identify each stage.

### Pillar 4: Stress Detection (Biotic & Abiotic)
- **Abiotic stress:** Drought, salinity, heat, nutrient deficiency, waterlogging.
- **Biotic stress:** Insect pests, fungal/bacterial/viral diseases, weed infestation.

### Pillar 5: Yield Modeling & Crop Acreage Estimation
Empirical and process-based models link seasonal maximum NDVI (or integrated NDVI over the season) to final grain yield.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Exams** — All formulas below are tested regularly.

| # | Index / Formula | Equation | Range / Interpretation | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NDVI** (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | General greenness, biomass proxy |
| 2 | **EVI** (Enhanced Vegetation Index) | $G \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + C_1 \rho_{Red} - C_2 \rho_{Blue} + L}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | High-LAI regions, reduces soil/atmosphere noise |
| 3 | **SAVI** (Soil-Adjusted VI) | $\dfrac{(\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red})}{(\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red} + L)} \cdot (1+L)$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Sparse vegetation, exposed soil background |
| 4 | **MSAVI** (Modified SAVI) | $\dfrac{2\rho_{NIR} + 1 - \sqrt{(2\rho_{NIR}+1)^2 - 8(\rho_{NIR}-\rho_{Red})}}{2}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Self-adjusting for soil |
| 5 | **NDWI** (Normalized Difference Water Index) | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR}}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Leaf/canopy water content, irrigation stress |
| 6 | **NDMI** (Normalized Difference Moisture Index) | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{SWIR1}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR1}}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Canopy moisture stress |
| 7 | **GNDVI** (Green NDVI) | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Green}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Green}}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Chlorophyll concentration |
| 8 | **CVI** (Chlorophyll Vegetation Index) | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{Green}} - 1$ | $\geq 0$ | Chlorophyll, nitrogen status |
| 9 | **LAI Estimation (Beer-Lambert)** | $LAI = -\dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{PAR_{transmitted}}{PAR_{incident}}\right)$ | $0 \to 6+$ | Leaf area per unit ground |
| 10 | **Simple Ratio (SR)** | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{Red}}$ | $0 \to \infty$ | Linear biomass proxy |
| 11 | **EVI Coefficients (MODIS)** | $G = 2.5,\ C_1 = 6,\ C_2 = 7.5,\ L = 1$ | — | Atmospheric/soil correction |
| 12 | **SAVI L-factor** | $L = 0.5$ (intermediate vegetation) | $0$ (dense) $\to 1$ (sparse) | Soil brightness correction |
| 13 | **Yield – NDVI empirical** | $Y = a + b \cdot NDVI_{max}$ | — | Crop yield prediction |
| 14 | **TCARI / OSAVI** (Chlorophyll) | $\dfrac{3[(\rho_{700}-\rho_{670}) - 0.2(\rho_{700}-\rho_{550})(\rho_{700}/\rho_{670})]}{(1+0.16)(\rho_{700}-\rho_{670})}$ | — | Leaf chlorophyll content |

> **Note on notation:** $\rho$ denotes surface reflectance (dimensionless, $0 \text{ to } 1$); $\rho_{NIR}$ is the **Near-Infrared band reflectance**; $\rho_{Red}$, $\rho_{Blue}$, $\rho_{Green}$, $\rho_{SWIR}$ are the respective band reflectances.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NDVI Time-Series Anomaly Maps** are used by **ISRO's Mahalanobis National Crop Forecast Centre (MNCFC)** for district-level crop production forecasts.
- **NDWI-based irrigation scheduling** in **drip and sprinkler systems** reduces water use by up to **30%**.
- **UAV-mounted multispectral cameras** (e.g., MicaSense RedEdge) generate **plot-level prescription maps** for **variable rate fertilizer applicators**.
- **NDVI stress maps** trigger **crop insurance payouts** under India's **Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY)**.
- **LAI retrieval from Sentinel-2** supports **carbon flux modeling** under UNFCCC reporting obligations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: NDVI — From Spectral Reflectance to Vegetation Index

### Goal
Derive the NDVI formula, explain why it normalizes the difference, and prove its boundedness $-1 \le NDVI \le +1$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define raw band reflectances.**
Let:
- $\rho_{NIR}$ = reflectance in the Near-Infrared band
- $\rho_{Red}$ = reflectance in the Red band

Both lie in the physical domain $\rho \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 2 — Form the difference numerator.**
Vegetation contrasts most strongly in the (NIR − Red) difference. For dense green vegetation, $\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_{Red}$, so the difference is large and positive.

$$
D = \rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the sum denominator.**
To make the index **scale-independent** (independent of overall scene brightness), we normalize by the sum:

$$
S = \rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio (NDVI).**

$$
\boxed{NDVI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Prove the boundedness $-1 \le NDVI \le +1$.**

Let $x = \rho_{NIR}$ and $y = \rho_{Red}$, where $x, y \ge 0$.

*Upper bound:*
When $y = 0$ (pure NIR reflector, e.g., snow), $NDVI = \dfrac{x}{x} = +1$.

*Lower bound:*
When $x = 0$ (no NIR response, e.g., water or bare rock in some cases), $NDVI = \dfrac{-y}{y} = -1$.

*Monotonicity:*
For fixed $S > 0$, $NDVI$ is a strictly increasing function of $D$ (and therefore of $\rho_{NIR}$). Hence the maximum is $+1$ and the minimum is $-1$.

**Step 6 — Interpret the cases.**

| NDVI Value | Surface Type |
|---|---|
| $\le 0.1$ | Bare soil, rock, sand, snow, water |
| $0.2$ – $0.3$ | Sparse vegetation, stubble, senescing crops |
| $0.4$ – $0.6$ | Moderate vegetation, mid-growth crops |
| $0.7$ – $0.9$ | Dense, vigorous vegetation, peak growth |
| $>0.9$ | Tropical rainforests, peak chlorophyll saturation |

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SAVI — Correcting NDVI for Soil Background

### Why a correction is needed
In **sparse vegetation** (LAI < 1), the NDVI value is contaminated by the underlying soil signature. The red reflectance of a wet dark soil can be *lower* than the crop's red reflectance, paradoxically inflating NDVI.

**Step 1 — Introduce a soil-brightness correction factor $L$.**
The objective is to minimize soil-induced variance in the vegetation isoline.

**Step 2 — Write SAVI as a modified NDVI.**

$$
\boxed{SAVI = \frac{(\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red})}{(\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red} + L)} \cdot (1+L)}
$$

**Step 3 — Select $L$.**
- $L = 0 \Rightarrow SAVI \equiv NDVI$ (no correction)
- $L = 1 \Rightarrow$ very sparse vegetation, maximum soil correction
- $L = 0.5 \Rightarrow$ **default** for intermediate / unknown canopy density

**Step 4 — Special case $L = 0.5$.**

$$
SAVI = \frac{1.5 \cdot (\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red})}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red} + 0.5}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify behaviour at the two limits.**

*Case A — Bare soil (no vegetation signal):* The soil line approximation gives $\rho_{NIR} \approx a \cdot \rho_{Red} + b$ where the line passes through the "soil isoline." A properly chosen $L$ collapses the variability along this line, giving constant SAVI for a given vegetation cover, regardless of soil brightness.

*Case B — Dense vegetation:* $\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_{Red}$ and $L = 0.5$ is negligible compared to $\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}$. SAVI $\to$ NDVI asymptotically.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EVI — Adding Atmospheric Resistance and a Blue Band

**Step 1 — Atmospheric motivation.**
Red band reflectance is corrupted by aerosol scattering, especially in tropical atmospheres. A Blue band reference is used to correct this contamination.

**Step 2 — EVI equation.**

$$
\boxed{EVI = G \cdot \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + C_1 \rho_{Red} - C_2 \rho_{Blue} + L}}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard MODIS coefficients.** $G = 2.5$, $C_1 = 6$, $C_2 = 7.5$, $L = 1$.

$$
EVI = 2.5 \cdot \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + 6\rho_{Red} - 7.5\rho_{Blue} + 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical meaning of each term.**
- Numerator $\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}$: vegetation signal.
- Coefficient $G = 2.5$: gain factor to bring EVI into a range comparable to NDVI.
- $C_1 \rho_{Red}$: corrects Red band aerosol (mainly *Rayleigh* and *Mie* scattering).
- $C_2 \rho_{Blue}$: corrects aerosol influence using the Blue band as a reference.
- $L$: soil background adjustment.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Compute NDVI for a Paddy Field

**Given:** Sentinel-2 surface reflectance values for a Kerala paddy pixel during peak vegetative stage.

| Band | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Red (B4) | $\rho_{Red}$ | 0.06 |
| NIR (B8) | $\rho_{NIR}$ | 0.48 |
| SWIR (B11) | $\rho_{SWIR}$ | 0.18 |

**Task:** Compute NDVI, NDWI, and classify the crop condition.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute NDVI.

$$
NDVI = \frac{0.48 - 0.06}{0.48 + 0.06} = \frac{0.42}{0.54} \approx 0.778
$$

Step 2 — Interpret. $NDVI = 0.778$ indicates **dense, vigorous vegetation** (peak growth stage).

Step 3 — Compute NDWI (for water status).

$$
NDWI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR}} = \frac{0.48 - 0.18}{0.48 + 0.18} = \frac{0.30}{0.66} \approx 0.455
$$

Step 4 — Interpret. $NDWI = +0.455$ indicates **high canopy water content** (well-hydrated, no drought stress).

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The pixel represents a **healthy, well-irrigated paddy crop** at peak tillering/heading stage.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Which Index for Which Stress?

| Stress Type | Best Primary Index | Supporting Index | Spectral Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drought / Water stress** | NDWI / NDMI | NDVI, Thermal | SWIR absorption drops as leaves dehydrate |
| **Nitrogen deficiency** | GNDVI, CVI, TCARI/OSAVI | NDVI | Chlorophyll drops → Red reflectance rises |
| **Disease / Pest attack** | NDVI anomaly + Red Edge | EVI, Chlorophyll indices | Loss of mesophyll structure |
| **Weed infestation** | NDVI (crop vs weed contrast) | Texture analysis | Weed patches differ in phenology |
| **Salinity stress** | NDVI + SWIR bands | Salinity Index (SI) | SWIR detects salt crust reflectance |
| **Heat stress** | Thermal bands (LST) + NDVI | NDWI | High canopy temperature |
| **Flood / Waterlogging** | NDWI (> 0.3) | NDVI drop | Standing water dramatically raises SWIR/NIR contrast |
