# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI). State its mathematical formula and explain the physical significance of using both Red and Near-Infrared bands.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

NDVI is a normalized, dimensionless index that quantifies the presence and vigour of green vegetation using satellite multispectral data.

**Mathematical Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
NDVI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}
$$

**Physical Significance (2 Marks):**
- **Red band ($\sim 670$ nm):** Chlorophyll pigments in healthy leaves strongly absorb red light for photosynthesis. Low red reflectance therefore indicates a healthy, photosynthesizing canopy.
- **NIR band ($\sim 840$ nm):** The spongy mesophyll cell structure inside healthy leaves scatters NIR radiation, producing very high NIR reflectance (40–60%).
- The **ratio form** normalizes the difference by the sum, cancelling out multiplicative illumination effects and producing a bounded value in $[-1, +1]$. The combination of "low Red + high NIR" generates a large positive NDVI, which serves as a robust proxy for vegetation health, biomass, and leaf area.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between NDVI and EVI. Why is EVI preferred in high-biomass regions?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | NDVI | EVI |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}$ | $G \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + C_1\rho_{Red} - C_2\rho_{Blue} + L}$ |
| Bands used | NIR, Red | NIR, Red, Blue |
| Saturation in dense canopy | Yes (signal saturates above LAI $\sim 4$) | No — remains sensitive at high LAI |
| Atmospheric correction | Implicit only | Explicit, via Blue band |
| Soil background | Not corrected | Corrected via $L$ factor |
| Range | $-1$ to $+1$ | $-1$ to $+1$ (typically 0 to 1) |

**Why EVI is preferred in high-biomass regions (1 Mark):**
NDVI suffers from **signal saturation** when LAI exceeds $\sim 3$–$4$, as the Red band absorption reaches an asymptote and the NIR plateau also flattens. EVI incorporates the **Blue band** for atmospheric correction and a **gain factor $G = 2.5$** to maintain sensitivity in dense canopies (tropical forests, irrigated paddy at peak growth). Hence, EVI discriminates more effectively between LAI levels above 4, which is critical in Kerala's dense cropping systems.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the spectral reflectance curve of a healthy green vegetation. Describe how the curve changes when the crop undergoes (i) chlorophyll degradation and (ii) moisture stress.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the mathematical form of NDVI and SAVI. Discuss the role of the soil-brightness correction factor $L$ in SAVI. Show that NDVI is bounded by $-1$ and $+1$.

#### Model Solution (a)

**Spectral Reflectance Curve of Healthy Green Vegetation:**

```
Reflectance (%)
 60 |                                       *****  NIR plateau
 50 |                                  *****
 40 |                              *****
 30 |                          *****                SWIR trough
 20 |                      *****              *    *
 10 |        **          *****             *    *
  5 |   *  *  *  *    *****         *    *    *
  0 |________________________________________________
    400   500   600   700   800  1000  1400  1900  2500
              Wavelength (nm)
       Blue   Green   Red       NIR         SWIR
```

**Characteristic features (3 Marks):**
1. **Blue absorption trough ($\sim 450$ nm):** Chlorophyll a and b absorb blue light for photosynthesis. Reflectance is typically 2–5%.
2. **Green peak ($\sim 550$ nm):** A small local maximum (~10–20%) — this is why leaves appear green.
3. **Red absorption trough ($\sim 670$ nm):** Deep minimum (5–8%) due to chlorophyll absorption.
4. **Red Edge ($\sim 700$–$750$ nm):** A steep rise in reflectance marking the onset of mesophyll scattering.
5. **NIR plateau ($\sim 750$–$1300$ nm):** Very high reflectance (40–60%) due to multiple scattering in the spongy mesophyll.
6. **SWIR water absorption troughs ($\sim 1450$ nm, $\sim 1950$ nm):** Liquid water in the leaf absorbs strongly, producing two diagnostic dips.

**Changes under stress (4 Marks):**

**(i) Chlorophyll Degradation (e.g., senescence, nitrogen deficiency, disease):**
- The **Red absorption trough weakens**, and Red reflectance rises from ~5% to 10–15%.
- The **Green peak diminishes**, and the leaf appears yellow.
- The **Blue absorption is also reduced**, raising Blue reflectance.
- The **NIR plateau may decrease slightly** as chloroplast disintegration begins to affect cell walls.
- Net effect: **NDVI drops sharply** because the numerator $(\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red})$ shrinks.

**(ii) Moisture Stress (Drought / Dehydration):**
- The **SWIR troughs at 1450 nm and 1950 nm become shallower** because free water content falls.
- The **NIR plateau drops** as leaves wilt and the mesophyll air-space geometry collapses.
- Red reflectance may initially increase as stomata close and leaf temperature rises.
- **NDWI (using SWIR) drops dramatically**, often the earliest indicator of water stress.
- NDVI also decreases, but with a **time lag** relative to NDWI.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Derivation of NDVI (3 Marks):**
Define $\rho_{NIR}$ and $\rho_{Red}$ as the surface reflectances. The vegetation contrast is the difference:

$$
D = \rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}
$$

To remove multiplicative illumination effects, normalize by the sum $S = \rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}$:

$$
NDVI = \frac{D}{S} = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}
$$

**Step 2 — Boundedness Proof (2 Marks):**
- $\rho_{NIR}, \rho_{Red} \in [0,1]$
- Maximum NDVI occurs when $\rho_{Red} = 0$: $NDVI_{max} = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{NIR}} = +1$
- Minimum NDVI occurs when $\rho_{NIR} = 0$: $NDVI_{min} = \dfrac{-\rho_{Red}}{\rho_{Red}} = -1$
- Therefore $-1 \le NDVI \le +1$ (proved).

**Step 3 — Derivation of SAVI (1 Mark):**

$$
SAVI = \frac{(\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red})}{(\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red} + L)} \cdot (1+L)
$$

**Step 4 — Role of $L$ (1 Mark):**
The $L$ factor in the denominator adds a constant that compensates for soil background reflectance variability. Without this term, NDVI computed over sparse vegetation is biased by soil brightness — a bright dry soil may produce a spuriously high NDVI. By tuning $L$:
- $L = 0$ → SAVI ≡ NDVI (no correction)
- $L = 0.5$ → intermediate canopies (default)
- $L = 1$ → extremely sparse canopies (maximum soil suppression)

The factor $(1+L)$ is a gain multiplier that keeps SAVI on a comparable numerical scale to NDVI.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a) [7 Marks)** Explain the operational methodology of crop condition assessment using multi-temporal satellite data. List the major vegetation indices and explain the role of NDWI in irrigation management.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A wheat field pixel shows the following Sentinel-2 surface reflectance values: $\rho_{NIR} = 0.42$, $\rho_{Red} = 0.09$, $\rho_{SWIR} = 0.22$. Compute NDVI, NDWI, and classify the crop condition. If the historical average peak NDVI for this region during the heading stage is 0.85, comment on the current condition.

#### Model Solution (a)

**Operational Methodology of Crop Condition Assessment (4 Marks):**

1. **Task Definition and Crop Calendar Study:** Identify the crop, the growing season, the phenological windows, and the expected NDVI envelope.
2. **Multi-temporal Data Acquisition:** Acquire cloud-free, geometrically and radiometrically corrected imagery (Landsat 8, Sentinel-2, MODIS, Resourcesat) at intervals of 5–16 days.
3. **Pre-processing:** Atmospheric correction (e.g., Sen2Cor, 6S, FLAASH), cloud masking (Fmask, SCL), BRDF normalization, and resampling to a common grid.
4. **Index Computation:** Compute NDVI, EVI, NDWI, and other relevant indices from surface reflectance bands.
5. **Time-Series Construction:** Generate temporal NDVI/EVI profiles and smooth them using Savitzky–Golay filters or harmonic (HANTS) analysis.
6. **Phenology Extraction:** Derive Start of Season (SOS), Peak of Season (POS), End of Season (EOS), and seasonal integral.
7. **Anomaly Detection:** Compare current profile with the historical mean ± 1 standard deviation envelope.
8. **Stress Classification:** Use spectral rule-based logic (e.g., low NDVI + low NDWI → drought; low NDVI + high NDWI → disease) to identify the stress type.
9. **Yield Modeling:** Regress seasonal integrated NDVI against historical yield statistics to forecast production.
10. **GIS Integration & Dissemination:** Overlay with cadastral, soil, and weather data and publish a prescription/decision-support map.

**Major Vegetation Indices (2 Marks):**

- **NDVI** — generic greenness
- **EVI** — high-biomass, atmosphere-corrected
- **SAVI / MSAVI** — sparse vegetation, soil-corrected
- **GNDVI / CVI / TCARI** — chlorophyll and nitrogen
- **NDWI / NDMI** — canopy water content

**Role of NDWI in Irrigation Management (1 Mark):**
The Normalized Difference Water Index uses the NIR and SWIR bands to track canopy water content directly. As irrigation is withdrawn or drought sets in, leaf water potential drops, SWIR absorption weakens, and NDWI declines. By ingesting **near-real-time NDWI maps** into a GIS-based irrigation decision support system, agronomists can schedule irrigation only on stressed zones, achieving water savings of 20–30% compared to uniform flood irrigation.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $\rho_{NIR} = 0.42$
- $\rho_{Red} = 0.09$
- $\rho_{SWIR} = 0.22$
- Historical peak NDVI (heading stage) = 0.85

**Step 1 — Compute NDVI (1 Mark):**

$$
NDVI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}} = \frac{0.42 - 0.09}{0.42 + 0.09} = \frac{0.33}{0.51} \approx 0.647
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NDWI (1 Mark):**

$$
NDWI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR}} = \frac{0.42 - 0.22}{0.42 + 0.22} = \frac{0.20}{0.64} \approx 0.313
$$

**Step 3 — Classification (2 Marks):**

| Metric | Value | Class |
|---|---|---|
| NDVI = 0.647 | Moderate vegetation | Mid-vegetative to booting stage |
| NDWI = 0.313 | Moderate water content | Adequate but not luxuriant hydration |

**Step 4 — Comparison with Historical Peak (2 Marks):**

The current NDVI is 0.647, while the historical peak at heading is 0.85. The deviation is:

$$
\Delta NDVI = 0.85 - 0.647 = 0.203
$$

As a relative deficit:

$$
\frac{\Delta NDVI}{NDVI_{historical}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.203}{0.85} \times 100\% \approx 23.9\%
$$

A 24% deficit from the historical peak is **moderately significant**. The crop is currently in mid-growth and is **underperforming** the historical envelope. Possible reasons include:
- Sub-optimal nitrogen availability (consider top-dressing urea).
- Mild water stress (NDWI = 0.313 is on the lower side of "well-watered").
- Early-stage pest or disease pressure.

**Recommendation (1 Mark):** A field-level visit and ground-truth sampling is recommended; if the stress is confirmed, deploy a precision nitrogen top-dressing and supplemental irrigation prescription map using UAV-based high-resolution data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse NDWI (using SWIR for canopy water) with MNDWI (Modified NDWI, used for open water body detection).** Many students interchange them — the band combination is different.
> 2. **NDVI saturation in high-LAI canopies** is a guaranteed 2-mark trap. Always mention that NDVI loses sensitivity above LAI $\approx 4$ and suggest EVI.
> 3. **In NDVI/SAVI derivations, students often forget to prove boundedness.** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the inequality proof.
> 4. **Mentioning the band wavelengths** (e.g., Red = 670 nm, NIR = 840 nm) carries full credit; omitting them costs 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Writing only the index name without the formula** in numerical problems results in zero marks for the calculation step. Always write the equation first, substitute, then evaluate.
> 6. **Avoid generic "NDVI measures vegetation" answers.** Specify that NDVI measures **photosynthetically active biomass, canopy greenness, and leaf chlorophyll density** to score full conceptual marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Crop Condition Assessment**

- **Definition:** CCA = quantitative evaluation of crop physiological & biochemical health using multispectral remote sensing.
- **Spectral Trifecta:** Three diagnostic regions = **Red (chlorophyll absorption)**, **NIR (mesophyll scattering)**, **SWIR (water absorption)**.
- **NDVI Formula:** $\dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$, range $[-1, +1]$.
- **NDVI Saturation:** Above LAI $\approx 4$ in high-biomass canopies; use **EVI** instead.
- **EVI Adds:** Blue band (atmospheric correction) + gain factor $G=2.5$ + soil factor $L=1$.
- **SAVI:** Modified NDVI with $L$-factor for soil; default $L = 0.5$.
- **NDWI / NDMI:** Use NIR + SWIR; primary diagnostic of **canopy moisture & irrigation stress**.
- **GNDVI, CVI, TCARI:** Chlorophyll- and nitrogen-specific indices.
- **Phenology Metrics:** SOS, POS, EOS derived from smoothed NDVI time-series.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Compare current NDVI with multi-year mean ± 1σ envelope.
- **Two Stress Classes:** **Abiotic** (drought, salinity, heat, nutrients) vs. **Biotic** (pests, diseases, weeds).
- **Yield Model:** Empirical regression $Y = a + b \cdot NDVI_{seasonal\ max\ or\ integrated}$.
- **Operational Pipeline:** Acquisition → Atmospheric Correction → Index Calculation → Time-Series → Anomaly Detection → GIS Overlay → Prescription.
- **KTU Standard Satellites:** Landsat 8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, MODIS, Resourcesat-2/2A, Cartosat.
- **Numerical Trick:** Whenever you see a numerical problem, **state the formula, then substitute, then evaluate** — partial credit is preserved this way.
- **Key Wavelengths to Memorize:** Blue ~450 nm, Green ~550 nm, Red ~670 nm, NIR plateau 700–1300 nm, SWIR water troughs 1450 nm and 1950 nm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soil Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Soil Mapping** is the systematic process of identifying, delineating, classifying, and representing the spatial distribution of different soil types and their physical, chemical, and biological properties across a geographic area. In the context of Remote Sensing (RS) and Geographic Information System (GIS), soil mapping refers to the integration of **multispectral/hyperspectral satellite imagery**, **digital elevation models (DEM)**, **ancillary ground truth data**, and **geostatistical algorithms** to produce thematic soil maps at various scales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Soil mapping is recognized as a sub-discipline of **Digital Soil Mapping (DSM)** under the *GlobalSoilMap* and *SoilGrids* frameworks, which use **scorpan** (an acronym: **s**oil, **c**limate, **o**rganisms, **r**elief, **p**arent material, **a**ge, **n**eighbouring space) environmental covariates for predictive mapping.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor examining a patient. You cannot directly look inside the body, but you use tools like **X-rays, MRI, and blood tests** to infer the internal condition. **Soil mapping works exactly the same way** — the Earth's surface is covered by a "skin" of vegetation, rocks, or sand, and we cannot directly "see" the soil underneath from a satellite. However, by analyzing the **spectral reflectance** (the colors of light reflected from the surface), the **topography** (slope, curvature), and the **climate signatures**, we can infer what type of soil lies beneath — much like a doctor infers a disease from symptoms.

Think of soil mapping as **building a "skin map" of the Earth** — where every pixel on the map is labeled with a soil class (e.g., *Vertisol*, *Alfisol*, *Entisol*) based on indirect but measurable evidence.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics in Soil Mapping:**
> - **Mapping Scale:** 1:250,000 (Reconnaissance) → 1:5,000 (Detailed/Farm-level). 
> - **Minimum Mapping Unit (MMU):** Typically **2 ha to 25 ha** depending on scale.
> - **Soil Map Accuracy (Kappa Coefficient, $\kappa$):** Standard threshold $\kappa \geq 0.80$ for publication-grade maps.
> - **Spectral Resolution Required:** Hyperspectral (**10 nm** bandwidth) for detailed mineralogy; Multispectral (e.g., Landsat — 60–120 nm) for general texture.
> - **Reference Datum:** WGS-84 (World Geodetic System, 1984) with UTM projection for GIS storage.

## 1.4 Significance in the KTU 2024 Scheme

In the **PEAGT521** syllabus, soil mapping bridges **Module 3 (Image Classification)** and **Module 4 (Thematic Applications)**. It is a flagship *application domain* where students are expected to demonstrate the **fusion of RS + GIS + ground validation** — a direct test of **Course Outcome CO3** (Apply RS & GIS to real-world problems) and **CO4** (Analyze thematic outputs).



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Remote Sensing Detects Soil

Soils are detected by remote sensors based on three fundamental physical principles:

1. **Spectral Reflectance ($\rho_\lambda$):** Different soil minerals reflect light differently in the **Visible (VIS: 0.4–0.7 µm)**, **Near-Infrared (NIR: 0.7–1.3 µm)**, and **Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR: 1.3–2.5 µm)** bands. For example:
   - **Iron-rich soils (Oxisols, Laterites):** Show strong absorption in SWIR around **2.2 µm** due to Fe-O-H bending.
   - **Organic-rich soils (Histosols):** Low reflectance in VIS, gentle slope in NIR.
   - **Saline/Sodic soils:** High reflectance in SWIR due to salt crusts.
   - **Sandy soils (Entisols/Aridisols):** Highest overall reflectance, monotonically increasing curve.

2. **Thermal Inertia ($\mathcal{P}$):** A measure of the soil's resistance to temperature change. Computed from diurnal temperature difference ($\Delta T$):

$$ \mathcal{P} = \sqrt{k \cdot \rho_s \cdot c_s} $$

   where $k$ is thermal conductivity, $\rho_s$ is soil bulk density, and $c_s$ is specific heat capacity. Soils with high moisture/clay content have **higher thermal inertia** and appear cooler in daytime thermal imagery.

3. **Emissivity ($\varepsilon_\lambda$):** Measured in the **Thermal Infrared (TIR: 8–14 µm)** band using sensors like Landsat TIRS or MODIS. Mineral composition (quartz vs. carbonates) shifts emissivity peaks.

## 2.2 The SCORPAN Framework (McBratney et al., 2003)

Digital Soil Mapping is mathematically expressed as:

$$ S_c = f(s, c, o, r, p, a, n) + e $$

where:
- $S_c$ = soil class or property (e.g., clay %, pH, depth)
- $s$ = soil (other properties of soil at a point)
- $c$ = climate (precipitation, temperature)
- $o$ = organisms (vegetation, fauna)
- $r$ = relief (DEM derivatives: slope, aspect, curvature)
- $p$ = parent material (lithology)
- $a$ = age (time factor)
- $n$ = spatial position (neighbourhood)
- $e$ = spatially correlated error

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The SCORPAN model is often implemented using **Machine Learning algorithms** like **Random Forest (RF)**, **Cubist**, or **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** trained on point soil observations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Index** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Application in Soil Mapping** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normalized Soil Moisture Index (NSMI) | $\text{NSMI} = \dfrac{(\text{NIR} - 0.5) - (\text{SWIR} + 0.5)}{(\text{NIR} + 0.5) + (\text{SWIR} + 0.5)}$ | NIR, SWIR = Top-of-Atmosphere reflectance | Detect wet vs. dry bare soils |
| Salinity Index (SI) | $\text{SI} = \dfrac{\text{Green} \times \text{Red}}{\text{NIR}}$ | Green, Red, NIR band reflectances | Map salt-affected soils |
| Iron Oxide Index | $\text{IOI} = \dfrac{\text{Red}}{\text{Blue}}$ | Red and Blue band reflectances | Detect lateritic/iron-rich soils |
| Brightness Index | $\text{BI} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \rho_i$ | $\rho_i$ = reflectance of band $i$, $N$ = number of bands | Distinguish light (sandy) from dark (organic) soils |
| Thermal Inertia | $\mathcal{P} = \sqrt{k \cdot \rho_s \cdot c_s}$ | $k$ [W·m$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$], $\rho_s$ [kg·m$^{-3}$], $c_s$ [J·kg$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$] | Infer soil texture and moisture |
| Overall Classification Accuracy | $\text{OA} = \dfrac{\text{Correctly Classified Pixels}}{\text{Total Pixels}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage [%] | Validate soil class map |
| Kappa Coefficient | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $p_o$ = observed agreement, $p_e$ = expected agreement | Account for chance agreement in soil map accuracy |
| SCORPAN Digital Soil Model | $S_c = f(s,c,o,r,p,a,n) + e$ | Environmental covariates and error term | Predictive soil mapping |
| Soil Line Equation (bare soil) | $\rho_{\text{NIR}} = a \cdot \rho_{\text{Red}} + b$ | $a$ = slope, $b$ = intercept; dimensionless | Separates bare soil pixels from vegetation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In civil and agricultural engineering, soil maps from RS & GIS are used for:
- **Site suitability analysis** for infrastructure (roads, dams, buildings) — avoiding expansive *Vertisols* or soft *Histosols*.
- **Precision agriculture:** Variable rate fertilizer application based on soil nutrient maps.
- **Watershed management:** Identifying erosion-prone *Inceptisols* on steep slopes.
- **Climate change studies:** Monitoring soil organic carbon (SOC) stocks using multispectral indices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** When a civil engineer is planning a highway alignment through Kerala's Western Ghats, the **soil map (combined with slope and lithology)** directly decides the cut-and-fill volumes, the type of pavement subgrade, and the drainage design. A **1:50,000 scale soil map from ISRO's Bhuvan** is often the first layer loaded into the GIS for such projects.



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Soil Line and its Role in Bare Soil Extraction

The **Soil Line** is a fundamental concept in remote sensing of soils. It is the linear regression of **NIR reflectance on Red reflectance** for a population of bare soil pixels.

**Step 1 — Define reflectance pairs for bare soil pixels:**

Let $n$ bare soil pixels be sampled from a satellite image. For each pixel $i$, we measure $(\rho_{R,i}, \rho_{\text{NIR},i})$.

**Step 2 — Compute the linear regression coefficients:**

The slope $a$ of the soil line is given by:

$$ a = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\rho_{R,i} - \bar{\rho}_R)(\rho_{\text{NIR},i} - \bar{\rho}_{\text{NIR}})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\rho_{R,i} - \bar{\rho}_R)^2} $$

The intercept $b$ is:

$$ b = \bar{\rho}_{\text{NIR}} - a \cdot \bar{\rho}_R $$

**Step 3 — Formulate the soil line equation:**

$$ \rho_{\text{NIR}} = a \cdot \rho_{\text{Red}} + b $$

**Step 4 — Compute the Perpendicular Vegetation Index (PVI) to isolate bare soil:**

The distance of a pixel from the soil line in the Red–NIR feature space is:

$$ \text{PVI} = \dfrac{\vert a \cdot \rho_{\text{Red}} - \rho_{\text{NIR}} + b \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + 1}} $$

**Conversion Logic:** A pixel with $\text{PVI} \approx 0$ lies on the soil line and is **bare soil**. A pixel with large positive PVI lies *above* the line and is **vegetation**. A pixel with large negative PVI lies *below* (water bodies or wet/shadowed soils).

**Step 5 — Apply a threshold to mask non-soil pixels:**

Define a threshold $T$ (typically $T = 0.05$ for Landsat-8 OLI). Bare soil mask $\mathcal{M}$ is:

$$ \mathcal{M}(i,j) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \text{PVI}(i,j) \le T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

where $\mathcal{M}(i,j) = 1$ means the pixel $(i,j)$ is classified as bare soil.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Computing Map Accuracy using the Kappa Coefficient

Suppose a soil map has 3 classes: **Clay, Loam, Sandy**. The validation uses 100 ground truth points.

**Step 1 — Build the confusion matrix:**

| | Pred. Clay | Pred. Loam | Pred. Sandy | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Act. Clay** | 28 | 4 | 2 | 34 |
| **Act. Loam** | 3 | 32 | 5 | 40 |
| **Act. Sandy** | 1 | 5 | 20 | 26 |
| **Col Total** | 32 | 41 | 27 | 100 |

**Step 2 — Compute overall accuracy ($p_o$):**

$$ p_o = \dfrac{28 + 32 + 20}{100} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.80 $$

**Step 3 — Compute expected agreement $p_e$:**

$$ p_e = \sum_{k=1}^{3} \dfrac{(\text{row total}_k)(\text{column total}_k)}{N^2} $$

$$ p_e = \dfrac{34 \times 32}{100^2} + \dfrac{40 \times 41}{100^2} + \dfrac{26 \times 27}{100^2} $$

$$ p_e = \dfrac{1088 + 1640 + 702}{10000} = \dfrac{3430}{10000} = 0.343 $$

**Step 4 — Compute Kappa ($\kappa$):**

$$ \kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \dfrac{0.80 - 0.343}{1 - 0.343} = \dfrac{0.457}{0.657} \approx 0.696 $$

**Interpretation:** A $\kappa$ of 0.696 corresponds to **"Substantial Agreement"** on the Landis & Koch scale — the map is acceptable for reconnaissance planning but not for farm-level precision.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Conventional vs. RS-GIS Soil Mapping

| **Parameter** | **Conventional Pedon Mapping** | **RS-GIS Digital Soil Mapping (DSM)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Time for 1:50,000 map (1 district)** | 3–5 years | 6–12 months |
| **Cost (approx.)** | High (₹50–80 lakh) | Moderate (₹10–20 lakh) |
| **Spatial coverage** | Limited by foot survey | Wall-to-wall from satellite |
| **Objectivity** | Subjective (surveyor-dependent) | Quantitative (ML-based) |
| **Updatability** | Static (decades) | Dynamic (can be re-run with new imagery) |
| **Resolution** | Up to farm scale | Limited by sensor spatial resolution (e.g., 10 m for Sentinel-2) |
| **Validation** | Field profile description | Confusion matrix + $\kappa$ statistic |
| **Key data input** | Soil profile + auger samples | Image spectra + DEM + climate grids |
| **Output format** | Hardcopy map + legend | GeoDatabase (.shp, .tif, GeoJSON) |



<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow Diagram: Soil Mapping using RS & GIS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: DefineMappingObjective] --> B[Step2: AcquireSatelliteImagery]
    B --> C[Step3: PreprocessImagery]
    C --> D[Step4: ExtractBareSoilPixels]
    D --> E[Step5: ComputeSoilIndices]
    E --> F[Step6: CollectGroundTruthSamples]
    F --> G[Step7: TrainClassifierModel]
    G --> H[Step8: ClassifyandProduceSoilMap]
    H --> I[Step9: AccuracyAssessment]
    I --> J{AccuracyAcceptable}
    J -->|No| K[Step10: RefineTrainingData]
    K --> G
    J -->|Yes| L[Step11: PublishFinalSoilMapGIS]
    L --> M[Step12: ApplicationsAgricultureCivilEng]
```

**Diagram Interpretation (Block-Level Architecture):**
- **Step 1–3** handle the planning and geometric/radiometric correction of the imagery.
- **Step 4–5** are the **feature engineering** phase (spectral indices).
- **Step 6–7** constitute the **supervised learning** core.
- **Step 8** produces the thematic map; **Step 9–10** is the iterative quality control loop.
- **Step 11–12** move the validated map into a production GIS for end-users.

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: SCORPAN Inputs and SCORPAN Output

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[SCORPAN_s_Soil] --> M[DSMModel]
    C[SCORPAN_c_Climate] --> M
    O[SCORPAN_o_Organisms] --> M
    R[SCORPAN_r_Relief] --> M
    P[SCORPAN_p_ParentMaterial] --> M
    A[SCORPAN_a_Age] --> M
    N[SCORPAN_n_Neighbourhood] --> M
    M --> Sc[Output_SoilProperty_Sc]
    M --> Err[ErrorTerm_e]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
Each SCORPAN covariate layer (s, c, o, r, p, a, n) is fed as a **raster or vector input** to the digital soil model $f(\cdot)$. The model produces a continuous or categorical map of soil class/property ($S_c$) along with a spatially correlated error map ($e$).

## 4.3 Architecture Flow: Soil Spectral Response vs. Soil Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[LateriteSoil] --> A2[Strong_FeOxide_Absorption_2p2um]
    B1[Vertisol_Clay] --> B2[High_Moisture_Deep_SWIR_Absorption]
    C1[Entisol_Sandy] --> C2[High_Broadband_Reflectance]
    D1[Histosol_Organic] --> D2[Low_Reflectance_Gentle_Slope]
    E1[Aridisol_Saline] --> E2[SWIR_2p1um_Salt_Crest_Peak]
    A2 --> F1[Spectral_Class_Laterite]
    B2 --> F2[Spectral_Class_Vertisol]
    C2 --> F3[Spectral_Class_Entisol]
    D2 --> F4[Spectral_Class_Histosol]
    E2 --> F5[Spectral_Class_Aridisol]
    F1 --> G[FinalThematicSoilMap]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
This flow illustrates how different soil types produce **diagnostic spectral signatures** in specific wavelength regions, which the classifier uses to assign each pixel to a soil class. The result is the final thematic soil map.



<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *Define Digital Soil Mapping. List any two environmental covariates used in the SCORPAN model.*

**Model Answer:**
Digital Soil Mapping (DSM) is the creation and population of spatial soil information systems using **field observations, laboratory analyses, and computational environmental covariates** through quantitative (statistical/ML) models. It is formally expressed as:

$$ S_c = f(s, c, o, r, p, a, n) + e $$

**Two SCORPAN covariates:**
1. **Relief (r):** DEM-derived parameters such as slope, aspect, and curvature that control water runoff and soil formation.
2. **Climate (c):** Long-term mean precipitation and temperature grids (e.g., WorldClim) that influence weathering and organic matter accumulation.

---

> **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *What is the Normalized Difference Soil Index (NDSI)? State its band combination and one limitation.*

**Model Answer:**
NDSI is a spectral index designed to differentiate **bare soil moisture conditions**:

$$ \text{NDSI} = \dfrac{(\text{SWIR} - \text{NIR})}{(\text{SWIR} + \text{NIR})} $$

**Band combination:** Near-Infrared (NIR) and Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR).
**Limitation:** It cannot distinguish **moisture due to soil texture from moisture due to irrigation**, and it is **ineffective under dense vegetation cover** (the signal becomes vegetation-dominated).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> *(a) Explain the step-by-step methodology of preparing a digital soil map using Remote Sensing and GIS. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Describe the spectral characteristics of the five major soil orders (Entisol, Inceptisol, Vertisol, Alfisol, Histosol). (7 marks)*

### Model Solution:

**(a) Step-by-Step Methodology of Digital Soil Map Preparation (7 marks):**

1. **Defining the Objective and Scale:** Decide the mapping scale (e.g., 1:50,000), MMU, and target soil classes (USDA Soil Taxonomy / WRB).

2. **Data Acquisition:**
   - **Multispectral imagery:** Landsat-8/9 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, or Resourcesat LISS-IV.
   - **DEM:** SRTM (30 m), ALOS PALSAR (12.5 m), or Cartosat DEM (10 m).
   - **Ancillary data:** Geological map, climate grids, land use/land cover (LULC), and existing legacy soil maps.

3. **Image Preprocessing:**
   - **Geometric correction:** Orthorectification using ground control points (GCPs).
   - **Radiometric correction:** Conversion of DN to Top-of-Atmosphere (ToA) reflectance, then to Surface Reflectance (SR) using **DOS1, 6S, or LaSRC** models.
   - **Atmospheric correction:** Removal of haze and aerosol effects.

4. **Bare Soil Composite Generation:**
   - **Temporal compositing** of multi-date images to maximize bare soil exposure.
   - Apply **soil line + PVI threshold** to mask vegetation, water, and built-up.

5. **Feature Engineering (Spectral Indices):**
   - Compute NDSI, SI, BI, Clay Mineral Index (CMI), Iron Oxide Index, etc.
   - Stack all indices as bands in a multi-layer raster.

6. **Ground Truth Collection:**
   - Use **auger samples, profile pits, and laboratory analysis** to create a reference dataset (minimum 30 samples per class for statistical robustness).

7. **Classification:**
   - **Supervised classification** (Maximum Likelihood, Random Forest, SVM) using the multi-layer stack and ground truth.

8. **Post-Classification:**
   - **Majority filter, clump/sieve,** and reclassification to remove salt-and-pepper noise.
   - **Recoding** to USDA/WRB classes.

9. **Accuracy Assessment:**
   - **Confusion matrix + Kappa coefficient**; target $\kappa \geq 0.80$.

10. **GIS Integration and Dissemination:** Export as **GeoTIFF, Shapefile, KML** for use in QGIS/ArcGIS.

**(b) Spectral Characteristics of Five Major Soil Orders (7 marks):**

| **Soil Order** | **Key Properties** | **VIS Reflectance** | **NIR Reflectance** | **SWIR Features** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Entisol** | Sandy, weakly developed | High (bright) | Very high, steep slope | Flat, featureless |
| **Inceptisol** | Moderately weathered | Moderate | Moderate, gentle | Slight absorption at 2.2 µm |
| **Vertisol** | Expansive clay (smectite) | Moderate, dark | Moderate | Strong absorption at **2.2 µm** (clay lattice), high moisture dip at **1.4 µm & 1.9 µm** |
| **Alfisol** | Clay-enriched B-horizon | Moderate | Moderate-high | Clay absorption at 2.2 µm, but shallower than Vertisol |
| **Histosol** | Organic-rich (peat) | Very low (dark) | Low, gentle slope | Minimal mineral features, organic absorption at 1.7 µm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> - **Do NOT confuse Vertisol spectral signature with Alfisol.** Vertisols have *deep* SWIR absorption at 2.2 µm due to **smectite clays**, while Alfisols (kaolinite-dominated) have a *shallower* absorption.
> - **Always mention the specific wavelength** (e.g., 2.2 µm) when describing clay minerals — vague statements like "clay absorbs in SWIR" lose 2 marks.
> - **Do not skip the accuracy assessment step** in (a). Examiners allocate 1.5 marks specifically for the confusion matrix and Kappa.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
> *(a) What is the SCORPAN model? Explain any five of its covariates with their role in digital soil mapping. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Discuss the role of hyperspectral remote sensing in soil property estimation (organic carbon, clay content, moisture). (7 marks)*

### Model Solution:

**(a) The SCORPAN Model — Definition and Five Covariates (7 marks):**

The SCORPAN model is an empirical-digital framework proposed by McBratney et al. (2003) to predict soil classes or properties from spatially explicit environmental data:

$$ S_c = f(s, c, o, r, p, a, n) + e $$

**Five Covariates:**

1. **Climate ($c$):** Precipitation, temperature, evapotranspiration. Influences weathering rate, organic matter decomposition, and leaching.
2. **Organisms ($o$):** Vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI), LULC, fauna activity. Vegetation indicates biological productivity and root zone influence.
3. **Relief ($r$):** DEM-derived layers — slope, aspect, curvature, TWI (Topographic Wetness Index), LS-factor. Controls water flow, erosion, and deposition.
4. **Parent Material ($p$):** Geological map, lithology raster. Determines the mineral composition of the soil (e.g., basalt → Vertisol; granite → Alfisol/Entisol).
5. **Spatial Position ($n$):** Latitude/longitude, distance to nearest profile, multi-resolution rasters. Captures spatial autocorrelation.

**(b) Hyperspectral Remote Sensing for Soil Property Estimation (7 marks):**

Hyperspectral sensors (e.g., **PRISMA, EnMAP, Hyperion, AVIRIS**) acquire data in **200+ narrow contiguous bands** (bandwidth ~10 nm) across VIS-NIR-SWIR (0.4–2.5 µm). This fine spectral resolution enables quantitative retrieval of soil properties:

1. **Soil Organic Carbon (SOC):**
   - Organic matter has diagnostic absorption features at **~460, 670, and 860 nm** (overtones of C-H bonds).
   - **PLSR (Partial Least Squares Regression)** or **Cubist models** trained on lab spectra are inverted onto image spectra.
   - Typical accuracy: $R^2 = 0.65$–$0.85$ for topsoil SOC.

2. **Clay Content:**
   - Clay minerals (kaolinite, smectite, illite) show absorption at **2200 nm (2.2 µm)**, **1900 nm**, and **1400 nm**.
   - **Clay Mineral Index (CMI):** $\text{CMI} = \rho_{2200} / \rho_{2300}$.
   - Hyperspectral allows separation of clay *mineralogy*, not just total clay %.

3. **Soil Moisture:**
   - Water absorption bands at **1450 nm** and **1940 nm**.
   - **Short-Wave Infrared Soil Moisture Index (SMI):** $\text{SMI} = \rho_{2130} - \rho_{1935}$.
   - Critical for **drought monitoring and irrigation scheduling** in precision agriculture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss (Question B):**
> - **Do NOT list hyperspectral sensors without their spectral range.** Always specify **0.4–2.5 µm** for the optical range.
> - **Do not confuse PLSC (Partial Least Squares Classification) with PLSR (Regression).** SOC is a *continuous* variable, so PLSR is correct.
> - **For SCORPAN:** Writing all 7 covariates is unnecessary; the question asks for *any five* — but examiners penalize if the chosen five are **generic** (e.g., "vegetation"). Be specific: write *NDVI*, *TWI*, *LULC* — not just words.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets for Last-Minute Reading:**

- **Soil Mapping** = identification, classification, and spatial representation of soils using RS + GIS.
- **SCORPAN** is the *backbone* formula: $S_c = f(s,c,o,r,p,a,n) + e$. Remember all 7 letters.
- **Soil Line:** $\rho_{\text{NIR}} = a \cdot \rho_{\text{Red}} + b$ — used to separate bare soil from vegetation via PVI.
- **Key Spectral Bands:** VIS (0.4–0.7 µm) — color; NIR (0.7–1.3 µm) — texture; SWIR (1.3–2.5 µm) — **minerals & moisture**; TIR (8–14 µm) — **thermal inertia**.
- **Diagnostic absorption features:** Iron oxide at **0.9 µm & 2.2 µm**; Clay lattice at **2.2 µm**; Organic C-H at **460, 670, 860 nm**; Water at **1.45 & 1.94 µm**; Salts at **2.1 µm**.
- **Indices to remember:** **NDSI** (NIR-SWIR), **SI** (Salinity), **BI** (Brightness), **IOI** (Iron Oxide), **CMI** (Clay).
- **Accuracy validation:** Always mention **Confusion Matrix + Kappa coefficient**; threshold $\kappa \geq 0.80$.
- **Sensors:** Multispectral — Landsat, Sentinel-2, LISS-IV. Hyperspectral — PRISMA, EnMAP, Hyperion.
- **Common soil orders & their spectral traits:** Entisol (bright), Histosol (dark), Vertisol (SWIR absorption), Alfisol (moderate SWIR), Aridisol (salt crest).
- **DSM advantages:** Faster, cheaper, objective, updatable. Limitation: depends on training data quality.
- **GIS formats to remember:** GeoTIFF (raster), Shapefile (vector), KML/GeoJSON (web).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "*Spectra give you the **what** (mineralogy), DEM gives you the **where** (topographic position), ground truth gives you the **certainty** (validation).*"



<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Soil with Digital Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Soil with Digital Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Number (DN)** is the quantized, integer-encoded brightness value recorded by a remote sensing detector for each pixel, representing the intensity of upwelling spectral radiance (L) reflected or emitted from the Earth's surface. In the context of soil classification, DNs form the **fundamental input variable** to which statistical, spectral, and geostatistical classifiers are applied to map discrete soil taxonomic units (e.g., *Sandy, Loamy, Clayey, Saline, Calcareous, Organic*) on a per-pixel basis.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus (PEAGT521 - Module 4), the classification of soils using DNs involves the following established framework:

$$DN = \text{round}\left[\left(\frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{2^{n}-1}\right) \cdot L + Q_{offset}\right]$$

where $L$ is the spectral radiance at the sensor, $L_{min}$ and $L_{max}$ are the detector's minimum and maximum calibration radiances, and $n$ is the radiometric resolution (typically 8, 11, 12, or 16 bits).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Piano Keyboard of Earth"**

Imagine the Earth's surface is playing a giant piano, and every object (sand, clay, water, vegetation) strikes a different *chord* of electromagnetic waves. The satellite sensor is a **tape recorder** that quantizes these chords into numbers from 0 to 255 (for 8-bit) or 0 to 65,535 (for 16-bit). 

Each soil type, like each musical note, has a unique chord:
- 🏖️ **Dry Sand** → Bright, high-pitched chord (high DNs across all bands)
- 🟤 **Clay** → Dull, mid-tone chord (low DNs, especially in SWIR)
- 🧂 **Saline Soil** → Muted chord with characteristic SWIR drop
- 🌱 **Organic/Peat** → Almost silent chord (very low DNs)

Classification is simply the process of **assigning each pixel's "chord signature" to a known soil class** — like a music teacher recognizing instruments by their timbre.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Radiometric Resolutions (KTU Reference)**
> - **8-bit**: $2^8 = 256$ levels (Landsat-1 to 5 MSS, ASTER VNIR)
> - **10-bit**: $2^{10} = 1024$ levels (Sentinel-2 reduced resolution)
> - **12-bit**: $2^{12} = 4096$ levels (Landsat-8/9 OLI, Sentinel-2)
> - **16-bit**: $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ levels (Hyperion, AVIRIS, MODIS)
> - **Spectral Range for Soil Studies**: **0.4 µm – 2.5 µm** (VNIR + SWIR)
> - **Standard Band Centres for Soil Discrimination**: 
>   - **Blue**: 0.48 µm
>   - **Green**: 0.56 µm
>   - **Red**: 0.66 µm
>   - **NIR**: 0.86 µm
>   - **SWIR1**: 1.6 µm
>   - **SWIR2**: 2.2 µm

## 1.4 Why Classify Soil with DNs?

Traditional soil survey is **slow, expensive, and point-based** (one profile per 5–10 km²). Remote sensing-based soil classification using DNs offers:

| Parameter | Conventional Survey | DN-based Remote Sensing |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial Coverage** | Point/Polygonal | Pixel-wise (raster grid) |
| **Cost per km²** | High (₹2,000–10,000) | Low (₹50–500) |
| **Repeatability** | Years to decades | Days to weeks |
| **Objectivity** | Subjective field judgment | Statistically reproducible |
| **Spectral Resolution** | N/A | Hyperspectral (5–10 nm) |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Variable | 10 m – 1000 m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟡 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow — "DN → Reflectance → Index → Class"

Soil classification from DNs proceeds in **four ordered stages**:

### **Stage 1 — DN to Top-of-Atmosphere (TOA) Radiance**

The raw DN is converted to spectral radiance using the sensor's calibration gain and bias:

$$L_{\lambda} = \text{Gain}_{\lambda} \cdot DN + \text{Bias}_{\lambda}$$

- $\text{Gain}_{\lambda}$ = slope of the radiometric calibration curve (W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·µm⁻¹ per DN)
- $\text{Bias}_{\lambda}$ = intercept (offset) of the calibration curve

> [!NOTE]
> For Landsat-8 OLI, the gain and bias are stored in the metadata file (`.MTL.txt`) as `RADIANCE_MULT_BAND_x` and `RADIANCE_ADD_BAND_x`.

### **Stage 2 — TOA Radiance to TOA Reflectance**

$${\rho}_{TOA} = \frac{\pi \cdot L_{\lambda} \cdot d^2}{ESUN_{\lambda} \cdot \cos\theta_s}$$

where:
- $d$ = Earth–Sun distance in Astronomical Units (AU)
- $ESUN_{\lambda}$ = Exo-atmospheric solar irradiance
- $\theta_s$ = Solar zenith angle

### **Stage 3 — Atmospheric Correction (TOA → Surface Reflectance)**

Atmospheric scattering and absorption distort the signal. Common correction models:

| Model | Input Requirement | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Dark Object Subtraction (DOS)** | Histogram minimum (1%) | Approximate surface ρ |
| **FLAASH** | Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD) | Refined surface ρ |
| **6S / 6SV** | AOD, water vapour, ozone | Radiative transfer-corrected ρ |
| **LaSRC (Landsat)** | Built-in look-up tables | Operational surface ρ |

### **Stage 4 — Spectral Index Computation & Classification**

Surface reflectance values are then fed into **spectral indices** designed to enhance soil-property contrast:

## 2.2 Key Spectral Indices for Soil Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Spectral Indices for Soil Mapping**

| Index Name | Formula | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bare Soil Index (BSI)** | $\dfrac{(SWIR + RED) - (NIR + BLUE)}{(SWIR + RED) + (NIR + BLUE)}$ | Distinguishing bare soil from vegetation |
| **Normalized Difference Soil Index (NDSI)** | $\dfrac{SWIR - NIR}{SWIR + NIR}$ | Soil moisture and mineralogy |
| **Salinity Index (SI)** | $\sqrt{GREEN \cdot RED}$ | Detecting salt crusts |
| **Salinity Index 2 (SI2)** | $\sqrt{RED^2 + NIR^2}$ | Saline-alkaline soil mapping |
| **Normalized Difference Salinity Index (NDSaI)** | $\dfrac{RED - NIR}{RED + NIR}$ | Salt-affected soil delineation |
| **Soil Adjusted Vegetation Index (SAVI)** | $\dfrac{(NIR - RED)}{(NIR + RED + L)}(1 + L)$, L = 0.5 | Minimizing soil brightness in mixed pixels |
| **Clay Mineral Ratio (CMR)** | $\dfrac{SWIR1}{SWIR2}$ | Clay mineral content |
| **Iron Oxide Ratio (IOR)** | $\dfrac{RED}{BLUE}$ | Iron-rich (lateritic/red) soils |
| **Carbonate Index (CI)** | $\dfrac{RED - GREEN}{RED + GREEN}$ | Calcareous soil detection |
| **Moisture Stress Index (MSI)** | $\dfrac{SWIR}{NIR}$ | Soil moisture status |

## 2.3 Classification Algorithms Applied to DN-Space

Once indices are computed, the DN (or index) vectors per pixel are assigned to soil classes using:

### **A. Unsupervised Classification**
- **K-Means Clustering**: Partitions the DN-space into *K* cluster centroids, each representing a soil class. The algorithm minimizes:
$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$$
- **ISODATA**: Iterative Self-Organizing Data Analysis Technique — adds *merge* and *split* operations to K-Means.
- **Fuzzy C-Means**: Each pixel has a *membership degree* in every class (continuous, not binary).

### **B. Supervised Classification**
- **Minimum Distance**: Euclidean distance from pixel to class mean:
$$D_i = \sqrt{\sum_{b=1}^{B} (DN_{b} - \mu_{i,b})^2}$$
- **Maximum Likelihood (Gaussian)**: Assumes each soil class has a multivariate normal distribution:
$$P(x \mid C_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{B/2} \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x-\mu_i)\right]$$
- **Support Vector Machine (SVM)**: Finds optimal hyperplane in multi-band DN-space.
- **Random Forest / XGBoost**: Ensemble tree-based classifiers on feature vectors.

### **C. Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM)**
Treats each pixel as a vector in *B*-dimensional space and measures the **angular distance** to reference soil spectra:
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\sum_{b=1}^{B} DN_b \cdot R_b}{\sqrt{\sum_{b=1}^{B} DN_b^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{b=1}^{B} R_b^2}}\right)$$
where $R_b$ is the reference endmember reflectance for band $b$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where This System is Used in Practice**
> - **Highway & Railway Embankment Design** — Pre-construction subgrade soil mapping
> - **Precision Agriculture** — Variable-rate fertilizer prescription maps
> - **Landslide Susceptibility Zonation** — Clay content and moisture mapping
> - **Dam Site Investigation** — Impervious clay-liner detection
> - **Smart City Planning** — Bearing capacity zoning
> - **Climate Modelling** — Soil moisture and carbon stock estimation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔵 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DN → TOA Radiance → Reflectance Conversion

This is the most fundamental step in soil classification. We shall work it out completely, end-to-end, using a worked numerical example to anchor the mathematics.

### **Step 1 — Write the sensor calibration equation**

The manufacturer provides, for each band $\lambda$, the multiplicative gain $G_\lambda$ and additive bias $B_\lambda$:

$$L_{\lambda} = G_{\lambda} \cdot DN + B_{\lambda}$$

**Assumed Landsat-8 OLI Calibration Coefficients (Band 4 = Red):**
- $G_{4} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·µm⁻¹ per DN
- $B_{4} = 0.0$ W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·µm⁻¹

### **Step 2 — Substitute a hypothetical observed DN value**

Let the observed DN for a pixel (suspected to be **sandy soil**) in Band 4 be:
$$DN_4 = 8{,}500 \text{ (out of 16-bit maximum } 65{,}535\text{)}$$

Compute $L_4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_4 &= G_4 \cdot DN_4 + B_4 \\[4pt]
    &= (2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (8{,}500) + 0.0 \\[4pt]
    &= 17.0 \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·sr}^{-1}\text{·µm}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The raw digital count is multiplied by the gain (units of radiance per DN) to convert to physical radiance units, then bias (offset) corrects for any detector dark current.

### **Step 3 — Compute Earth–Sun distance factor**

The Earth–Sun distance varies due to the elliptical orbit. We use the **inverse square correction**:

$$d^2 = \left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.0167 \cdot \cos\left[0.9856 \cdot (DOY - 4)\right]}\right)^2$$

For Day-of-Year $DOY = 172$ (typical mid-June acquisition):

$$\begin{aligned}
0.9856 \cdot (172 - 4) &= 0.9856 \cdot 168 = 165.58 \text{ degrees} \\[4pt]
\cos(165.58^\circ) &= -0.9682 \\[4pt]
1 - 0.0167 \cdot (-0.9682) &= 1 + 0.01617 = 1.01617 \\[4pt]
d^2 &= \left(\frac{1}{1.01617}\right)^2 = (0.9840)^2 = 0.9683 \text{ AU}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 4 — Retrieve exo-atmospheric solar irradiance**

For OLI Band 4 (Red, ~0.655 µm), the standard value from the WMO solar spectrum is:
$$ESUN_4 = 1{,}547.39 \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·µm}^{-1}$$

### **Step 5 — Compute solar zenith angle**

Solar zenith angle $\theta_s$ at 10:30 AM local time, mid-latitude 10°N, June 21:
$$\cos\theta_s = \sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos h$$

where $\phi = 10°$ (latitude), $\delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.43°$ (declination), and $h = 15°$ (hour angle at 10:30 AM).

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_s &= \sin(10°)\sin(23.43°) + \cos(10°)\cos(23.43°)\cos(15°) \\[4pt]
             &= (0.1736)(0.3979) + (0.9848)(0.9174)(0.9659) \\[4pt]
             &= 0.0691 + 0.8730 \\[4pt]
             &= 0.9421 \\[4pt]
\theta_s &= 19.65°
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 6 — Substitute into TOA reflectance equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
{\rho}_{TOA} &= \frac{\pi \cdot L_4 \cdot d^2}{ESUN_4 \cdot \cos\theta_s} \\[4pt]
              &= \frac{\pi \cdot 17.0 \cdot 0.9683}{1547.39 \cdot 0.9421} \\[4pt]
              &= \frac{51.72}{1457.79} \\[4pt]
              &= 0.0355
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator is the upwelling radiance scaled by geometry ($\pi$ for Lambertian, $d^2$ for orbital distance). The denominator is the downwelling irradiance at the ground (solar constant × cosine of solar zenith). The result is a unitless reflectance, typically in the range [0, 1].

### **Step 7 — Atmospheric correction (Dark Object Subtraction)**

Assume the histogram minimum DN in Band 4 for the dark water body is 250. The DOS-estimated additive contribution is:

$$\Delta L_{DOS} = G_4 \cdot DN_{dark} = (2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 250 = 0.5 \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·sr}^{-1}\text{·µm}^{-1}$$

Corrected surface radiance:
$$L_{surf,4} = 17.0 - 0.5 = 16.5 \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·sr}^{-1}\text{·µm}^{-1}$$

Surface reflectance (re-using same denominator):
$$\rho_{surf,4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 16.5 \cdot 0.9683}{1457.79} = 0.0344$$

### **Step 8 — Compute the BSI for soil classification**

Assume the same pixel yields the following surface reflectances (all bands, corrected):

| Band | Wavelength (µm) | Surface Reflectance ($\rho$) |
|---|---|---|
| Blue | 0.48 | 0.0541 |
| Red | 0.66 | 0.0344 |
| NIR | 0.86 | 0.1210 |
| SWIR1 | 1.61 | 0.1490 |

$$\begin{aligned}
BSI &= \frac{(SWIR + RED) - (NIR + BLUE)}{(SWIR + RED) + (NIR + BLUE)} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{(0.1490 + 0.0344) - (0.1210 + 0.0541)}{(0.1490 + 0.0344) + (0.1210 + 0.0541)} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{0.1834 - 0.1751}{0.1834 + 0.1751} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{0.0083}{0.3585} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.0232
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** A BSI value close to **+0.1 to +0.2** is characteristic of bare sandy soil; **near 0** is mixed pixel; **negative values** indicate vegetation or water. Our computed BSI of **0.0232** is consistent with **sandy soil with sparse vegetation cover** (slightly higher than pure bare soil threshold of ~0.05).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Maximum Likelihood Soil Classification

Given the training statistics for two soil classes, classify an unknown pixel $X = (DN_1, DN_2, DN_3) = (150, 220, 310)$:

| Class | Mean $\mu$ | Covariance $\Sigma$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Clay** | $\mu_C = (120, 200, 300)$ | $\Sigma_C = \begin{pmatrix} 100 & 50 & 30 \\ 50 & 200 & 80 \\ 30 & 80 & 150 \end{pmatrix}$ |
| **Sand** | $\mu_S = (180, 250, 400)$ | $\Sigma_S = \begin{pmatrix} 150 & 70 & 40 \\ 70 & 250 & 100 \\ 40 & 100 & 180 \end{pmatrix}$ |

### **Step 1 — Equal priors assumption**
$$P(C) = P(S) = 0.5$$

### **Step 2 — Compute Mahalanobis distance to Clay**

$$d_M^2(X, C) = (X - \mu_C)^T \Sigma_C^{-1} (X - \mu_C)$$

We need $\Sigma_C^{-1}$. Let us compute its determinant first:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert\Sigma_C\vert &= 100(200 \cdot 150 - 80 \cdot 80) - 50(50 \cdot 150 - 80 \cdot 30) + 30(50 \cdot 80 - 200 \cdot 30) \\
&= 100(30000 - 6400) - 50(7500 - 2400) + 30(4000 - 6000) \\
&= 100(23600) - 50(5100) + 30(-2000) \\
&= 2{,}360{,}000 - 255{,}000 - 60{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}045{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

This is algebraically tedious, so for KTU examination purposes, students should use the **discriminant function formulation**:

$$g_i(X) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(X-\mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (X-\mu_i) + \ln P(i)$$

**Simplified case (equal covariance)**: If we assume both classes share a pooled covariance, the decision boundary becomes linear:

$$g_i(X) = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} X + \ln P(i)$$

**For the KTU exam (KTU-typical style)**: Use the **minimum distance to mean** approach (simplified). Compute Euclidean distance from $X$ to each class mean:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_C &= \sqrt{(150-120)^2 + (220-200)^2 + (310-300)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{900 + 400 + 100} \\
    &= \sqrt{1400} \\
    &= 37.42
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D_S &= \sqrt{(150-180)^2 + (220-250)^2 + (310-400)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{900 + 900 + 8100} \\
    &= \sqrt{9900} \\
    &= 99.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision**: $D_C < D_S$, so the pixel is classified as **Clay**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DN Signatures of Common Soil Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Table — Typical Multi-Band DN Ranges (8-bit, Landsat-class)**
> *(After atmospheric correction; values are for visual reference)*

| Soil Class | Band 2 (Blue) | Band 3 (Green) | Band 4 (Red) | Band 5 (NIR) | Band 6 (SWIR1) | BSI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Sand** | 110–140 | 130–160 | 150–190 | 130–170 | 180–220 | +0.10 to +0.20 |
| **Clay (dry)** | 70–95 | 80–110 | 95–125 | 105–140 | 100–135 | +0.04 to +0.08 |
| **Loam** | 95–115 | 110–130 | 125–150 | 140–170 | 150–180 | +0.05 to +0.10 |
| **Organic/Peat** | 40–60 | 45–65 | 55–80 | 60–85 | 50–75 | -0.05 to 0.00 |
| **Saline (crust)** | 120–150 | 130–160 | 140–170 | 115–145 | 100–130 | +0.08 to +0.15 |
| **Laterite (red)** | 75–95 | 90–110 | 130–160 | 95–125 | 90–120 | +0.05 to +0.10 |
| **Calcareous** | 130–155 | 140–165 | 145–170 | 150–180 | 175–210 | +0.10 to +0.18 |
| **Volcanic (Andisol)** | 50–70 | 55–75 | 65–85 | 80–105 | 70–90 | +0.00 to +0.04 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🟣 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Soil Classification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite Sensor Capture] --> B[Raw Digital Numbers DN]
    B --> C{Radiometric Calibration}
    C --> C1[Convert DN to TOA Radiance L]
    C1 --> D{Atmospheric Correction}
    D --> D1[Dark Object Subtraction]
    D --> D2[FLAASH Model]
    D --> D3[6S Radiative Transfer]
    D1 --> E[Surface Reflectance rho]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Spectral Index Computation]
    F --> F1[BSI Bare Soil Index]
    F --> F2[NDSI Normalized Soil Index]
    F --> F3[SI Salinity Index]
    F --> F4[IOR Iron Oxide Ratio]
    F1 --> G{Classification Algorithm}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[Supervised MLC SVM]
    G --> G2[Unsupervised K Means]
    G --> G3[Spectral Angle Mapper]
    G1 --> H[Thematic Soil Map]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Post Classification Accuracy]
    I --> J[Confusion Matrix]
    J --> K[Kappa Coefficient]
    J --> L[Overall Accuracy percent]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        nodeA1[Multi Band Raster Stack]
        nodeA2[Training Polygon Shapefile]
        nodeA3[Reference Soil Spectra]
    end

    subgraph PREPROC[Preprocessing Module]
        nodeB1[Radiometric Calibration]
        nodeB2[Atmospheric Correction]
        nodeB3[Cloud and Shadow Masking]
    end

    subgraph FEATENG[Feature Engineering Module]
        nodeC1[Band Algebra and Index]
        nodeC2[Principal Component Analysis]
        nodeC3[Texture Filters GLCM]
    end

    subgraph CLASSIFY[Classification Engine]
        nodeD1[Train Class Statistics]
        nodeD2[Decision Rule Application]
        nodeD3[Output Class Labels]
    end
```
