# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define the term **Trap Efficiency** of a reservoir. State Brune's empirical relationship between Trap Efficiency and the **Capacity-Inflow Ratio**, and explain its significance in reservoir sedimentation studies.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Trap Efficiency (TE) is defined as the ratio of the total quantity of sediment deposited in a reservoir to the total quantity of sediment entering the reservoir, expressed as a percentage. It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 % to 100 %.
- **Brune's Relationship (1 mark):** G.M. Brune (1953) developed an empirical curve showing that TE increases with the **Capacity-Inflow Ratio (C/I)**. For medium-to-large reservoirs, an analytical fit is given by:

$$TE = -0.0301 \left( \frac{C}{I} \right)^2 + 0.1791 \left( \frac{C}{I} \right) + 0.4873$$

- **Significance (1 mark):** Brune's relationship allows engineers to **estimate the fraction of incoming sediment** that will be retained by the reservoir without any in-situ measurement. It forms the basis for predicting the **useful life of the reservoir** and is therefore critical for the design of dead storage allocation, sediment-flushing operations, and long-term dam safety assessments.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
List and briefly explain **any three** spectral indices used in remote sensing for water body and suspended sediment analysis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI) (1 mark):**

$$NDWI = \frac{G - NIR}{G + NIR}$$

This index maximizes water reflectance (Green band) while suppressing vegetation and land features (NIR). It is used to delineate the water-spread area of the reservoir for area-based sedimentation analysis.

2. **Normalized Difference Suspended Sediment Index (NDSSI) (1 mark):**

$$NDSSI = \frac{SWIR - NIR}{SWIR + NIR}$$

This index exploits the differential reflectance of suspended sediments in the SWIR and NIR bands. It is effective for concentrations exceeding 50 mg/L and reduces the influence of chlorophyll and dissolved organic matter.

3. **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) (1 mark):**

$$NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$$

While not a sediment index, NDVI is used to **mask aquatic vegetation and floating macrophytes** that would otherwise contaminate the sediment signal in shallow near-shore regions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **complete methodology** for estimating reservoir sedimentation using **multispectral satellite image processing**. Discuss in detail the pre-processing, water body extraction, and suspended sediment concentration estimation steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data are obtained from a Landsat-8 OLI image of a reservoir. Using the empirical model $SSC = 1840 \cdot (R_{NIR}/R_{Red})^{2.3}$, compute the SSC for the three pixels listed below. Comment on the spatial pattern. **(7 marks)**

| Pixel ID | $R_{Red}$ | $R_{NIR}$ | Approximate Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0.05 | 0.06 | Deep central reservoir |
| P2 | 0.08 | 0.12 | Near river inflow (monsoon) |
| P3 | 0.15 | 0.18 | Shallow near-dam zone |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Flowchart (2 marks):** A well-labeled diagram showing the following sequential stages:
1. **Data Acquisition** — Landsat/Sentinel imagery + ground truth.
2. **Pre-Processing** — Radiometric calibration, geometric correction.
3. **Atmospheric Correction** — DOS / 6S / Flaash.
4. **Water Body Delineation** — NDWI thresholding.
5. **SSC Estimation** — Empirical / band-ratio / multi-linear models.
6. **Validation** — Ground truth comparison, RMSE, R².
7. **Capacity Loss Computation** — Brune's TE + Areal Reduction Method.
8. **Reporting** — Maps, tables, decadal trend.

**Pre-processing (2 marks):**
- **Radiometric calibration:** Convert raw DN values to spectral radiance using sensor-specific gain and bias coefficients from the metadata file.
- **Geometric correction:** Register the image to a standard UTM projection using Ground Control Points (GCPs) and a suitable resampling method (nearest-neighbour for classification, bilinear for reflectance).
- **Atmospheric correction:** Remove the effects of Rayleigh scattering, aerosol scattering, and gaseous absorption. The **Dark Object Subtraction (DOS)** method estimates path radiance from dark pixels (deep water, shadows).

**Water body extraction (1 mark):** Compute NDWI and apply a threshold (typically NDWI $\geq 0.2$) to create a binary water mask.

**SSC estimation (1 mark):** Apply the calibrated empirical model $SSC = a(R_{NIR}/R_{Red})^b$ on a per-pixel basis to generate a continuous SSC map in mg/L.

**Validation (1 mark):** Compare predicted SSC with in-situ samples using **R², RMSE, and bias statistics**. A minimum R² of 0.7 is generally required for operational use.

**Part (b) — 7 marks (2 marks per calculation, 1 mark for comment):**

**Pixel P1:**

$$X_{P1} = \frac{0.06}{0.05} = 1.2$$

$$SSC_{P1} = 1840 \times (1.2)^{2.3} = 1840 \times 1.490 = 2741 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Pixel P2:**

$$X_{P2} = \frac{0.12}{0.08} = 1.5$$

$$SSC_{P2} = 1840 \times (1.5)^{2.3} = 1840 \times 2.541 = 4676 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Pixel P3:**

$$X_{P3} = \frac{0.18}{0.15} = 1.2$$

$$SSC_{P3} = 1840 \times (1.2)^{2.3} = 1840 \times 1.490 = 2741 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The highest SSC (~ 4676 mg/L) is observed at P2, near the river inflow — physically consistent because the inflow is the principal sediment source. P1 (deep central) and P3 (shallow near-dam) both show lower SSC values (~ 2741 mg/L). However, P3's apparent low SSC may be **contaminated by bottom reflectance** in shallow water, illustrating one of the key limitations of the method discussed in Section 3.4. A real study would either (i) mask pixels with depth < 2 m or (ii) apply a depth-correction factor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Trap Efficiency** and derive Brune's empirical equation for a reservoir. Discuss how remote sensing data can be used to **validate Brune's TE estimates** at decadal time scales. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The original gross storage of a reservoir is 100 Mm³, and the mean annual inflow is 250 Mm³. The mean annual SSC of the inflow is 800 mg/L. Using Brune's equation, compute the **annual sediment volume deposited** in the reservoir. Assume the unit weight of the deposit is 13 kN/m³ and the unit weight of water is 9.81 kN/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Trap Efficiency concept (2 marks):** TE is the fraction of incoming sediment retained by the reservoir. Brune's (1953) curve was derived from 44 reservoirs in the USA and shows a monotonic increase of TE with the Capacity–Inflow Ratio (C/I).

**Derivation of Brune's analytical fit (3 marks):**

Brune's original tabulated data are fitted to a **second-order polynomial in C/I**:

$$TE = -0.0301 \left( \frac{C}{I} \right)^2 + 0.1791 \left( \frac{C}{I} \right) + 0.4873$$

The **derivation rationale** is that the relationship is:
- Monotonically increasing.
- Asymptotic to 100 % as $C/I$ grows.
- Zero in the limit of zero storage (operationally, valid only for $C/I \ge 0.1$).

A second-order polynomial captures these properties in the operational range.

**Remote-sensing validation (2 marks):** Decadal satellite imagery (e.g., Landsat archive since 1972) is used to:
- Compute the **change in water-spread area** between two epochs.
- Apply the **Areal Reduction Method** to estimate capacity loss.
- Compare the implied **TE** with Brune's prediction. Discrepancies are attributed to sediment-flushing operations, land-use change in the catchment, or dam-rehabilitation activities.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Capacity-Inflow Ratio:**

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{100}{250} = 0.4$$

**Step 2 — Apply Brune's equation:**

$$TE = -0.0301 (0.4)^2 + 0.1791 (0.4) + 0.4873$$

$$TE = -0.0301 \times 0.16 + 0.07164 + 0.4873$$

$$TE = -0.004816 + 0.07164 + 0.4873 = 0.5541$$

**Step 3 — Compute the annual sediment mass entering the reservoir:**

$$M_{sed} = I \cdot \overline{SSC} = 250 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 \times 800 \text{ mg/L}$$

Convert units: $1$ mg/L $= 1$ g/m³ $= 10^{-3}$ kg/m³.

$$M_{sed} = 250 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 \times 800 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ kg} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Trap Efficiency to get deposited mass:**

$$M_{dep} = TE \cdot M_{sed} = 0.5541 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 1.108 \times 10^5 \text{ tonnes/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Convert mass to volume using the deposit density:**

The mass-to-volume conversion uses the **dry unit weight of the deposit** $\gamma_d = 13$ kN/m³ and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. The dry density in kg/m³ is:

$$\rho_d = \frac{\gamma_d}{g} = \frac{13000}{9.81} = 1325 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

The annual volume of deposit:

$$V_{dep} = \frac{M_{dep}}{\rho_d} = \frac{1.108 \times 10^5 \text{ kg}}{1325 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 83.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 6 — Final statement:** The reservoir loses approximately **83.6 m³ of storage per year** to sedimentation. At this rate, the **100 Mm³ reservoir** would lose 1 % of its capacity in approximately:

$$T_{1\%} = \frac{0.01 \times 100 \times 10^6}{83.6} \approx 11{,}960 \text{ years}$$

This long life is characteristic of large reservoirs — but the rate would be **30–50× higher** for smaller reservoirs with C/I < 0.1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Reservoir Sedimentation questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in numerical problems. SSC is given in mg/L, but the deposit volume calculation requires **kg/m³ or kN/m³**. Converting mg/L to g/m³ (multiply by 1) and then to kg (multiply by $10^{-3}$) is the most common source of arithmetic errors.
> 2. **Skipping atmospheric correction** in the methodology. Examiners expect the student to mention **DOS, 6S, or Flaash** as a discrete pre-processing step.
> 3. **Confusing NDWI and NDVI.** NDWI uses **Green and NIR**; NDVI uses **Red and NIR**. A reversed formula is an instant zero.
> 4. **Using Brune's curve outside its valid range** (C/I < 0.1 or C/I > 1.5). The polynomial fit breaks down at the extremes — always state the boundary condition.
> 5. **Neglecting validation.** A method that produces SSC values without any ground-truth comparison will lose at least **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 6. **Ignoring the consolidation effect.** Lane's formula for deposit density is a frequent follow-up sub-question. Memorize it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Reservoir Sedimentation Using Image Processing**

- [ ] **Reservoir sedimentation** is the progressive loss of live storage due to sediment deposition; it reduces the **economic life** of a dam.
- [ ] **Image processing** provides a **cost-effective, synoptic, and multi-temporal** alternative to bathymetric surveys.
- [ ] The **spectral basis** is that suspended sediments increase reflectance in the **Red and NIR** bands; the NIR band is the most diagnostic.
- [ ] **NDWI** uses **Green and NIR** to delineate water; threshold is typically **0.2**.
- [ ] **NDSSI** uses **SWIR and NIR** to enhance the sediment signal above 50 mg/L.
- [ ] **Atmospheric correction** is mandatory — use **DOS, 6S, or Flaash**.
- [ ] **Band-ratio model** $SSC = a \cdot (R_{NIR}/R_{Red})^b$ is the **most robust** empirical formulation.
- [ ] **Brune's Trap Efficiency** $TE = -0.0301(C/I)^2 + 0.1791(C/I) + 0.4873$ is valid for $0.1 \le C/I \le 1.5$.
- [ ] **Lane's formula** governs the **consolidation of deposited sediment** over time, with $\gamma_i \approx 9$ – 12 kN/m³ initially.
- [ ] **Areal Reduction Method:** $C_t = C_0 \cdot (A_t/A_0)^n$ with $n \approx 1.5$ for silt, $\approx 2.0$ for clay.
- [ ] **Validation** using R², RMSE, and bias is **non-negotiable**; aim for R² $\geq 0.7$.
- [ ] **Common error sources:** sun glint, bottom reflectance, atmospheric haze, mixed pixels, and cloud cover.
- [ ] **Indian case studies** to remember: **Idukki, Bhakra, Mullaperiyar, Hirakud, Nagarjuna Sagar** — all show 15 – 30 % capacity loss over 40 – 60 years.
- [ ] **Sensors to remember:** Landsat 8 OLI (30 m), Sentinel-2 MSI (10–20 m), Resourcesat-2 LISS-III (23.5 m), Cartosat-2 (1 m panchromatic — for water-spread area only).
- [ ] **Future trend:** **Sentinel-1 SAR** imagery enables **cloud-penetrating, all-weather** sedimentation monitoring, and **Google Earth Engine (GEE)** enables **planetary-scale time-series analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** For maximum marks in any Part-B question, **always** (i) state the physical basis, (ii) provide the formula with units, (iii) present a flowchart of the methodology, (iv) solve any numerical part step-by-step, and (v) close with a critical evaluation of the method's limitations. This five-part structure mirrors the KTU mark-splitting pattern and is the single most reliable strategy for securing full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory of Water Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT521 - REMOTE SENSING AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inventory of Water Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Resources Inventory** is defined as the systematic, spatially-explicit process of **identification, mapping, quantification, classification, and monitoring** of all forms of water resources — both **surface water** (rivers, lakes, reservoirs, wetlands, ponds, snowfields, glaciers) and **subsurface/groundwater** (aquifers, recharge zones) — using **multispectral, hyperspectral, thermal, and microwave remote sensing data** integrated with **GIS-based spatial analysis** to support planning, conservation, and sustainable management of the hydrological cycle.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the inventory of water resources is positioned at the intersection of **hydrology, geomorphology, and geospatial engineering**. It is not merely mapping; it is a **decision-support framework** that converts raw satellite pixels into hydrologically meaningful polygon, line, and point feature classes with **attributed quantitative metadata** (area, perimeter, volume, turbidity, depth, salinity, storage capacity).

### 1.1.1 Components Classified Under Inventory

| **Class** | **Sub-classes** | **Typical Spatial Scale** |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Water** | Rivers, streams, canals, lakes, reservoirs, tanks/ponds, wetlands | Local to basin |
| **Snow & Glaciers** | Seasonal snow cover, perennial snowfields, glaciers, glacial lakes | Mountainous catchments |
| **Groundwater Indicators** | Lineaments, drainage density, geomorphic units, recharge zones | Regional |
| **Watershed Features** | Catchment boundaries, sub-basins, drainage network, drainage density | Basin-level |
| **Water Quality Proxies** | Turbidity, chlorophyll-a, suspended sediment, thermal pollution | Point to lake/reservoir |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Cataloging System

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a librarian is asked, "How many books do we have, where are they, and which are damaged?" The librarian would (1) scan every shelf, (2) tag each book with metadata (title, author, condition), and (3) put it on a searchable digital map. The water resources inventory is **exactly the same — but for water**. The library shelves are the Earth's surface; the books are the rivers, lakes, aquifers, and glaciers; the scanner is the **satellite sensor**; the searchable digital map is the **GIS database**.

The **"why"** behind water resources inventory is critical:

- **Sustainable allocation** between agriculture, domestic, and industrial users.
- **Drought and flood preparedness** through periodic change detection.
- **Climate change impact assessment** on cryospheric and hydrological systems.
- **Legal and administrative delineation** of command areas, inter-state basins, and protected zones.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standards are universally applied in water resources inventory and are **bold-highlighted** for KTU reference:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$ (governs sensor band positioning).
- **Standard water density:** $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-4}$ (used in thermal IR brightness-temperature retrieval).
- **Standard pan-evaporation coefficient:** $K_p \approx 0.70$ to $0.80$ (used in reservoir loss estimation).
- **NDWI threshold for open water:** $NDWI \geq 0.3$ (commonly adopted, sensor-dependent).
- **MNDWI threshold for water:** $MNDWI \geq 0.0$ (improves built-up discrimination).
- **Spatial resolution classes:** **Very High (< 5 m)**, High (5–30 m), Medium (30–100 m), Low (100–1000 m).

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Emphasis:** Students are expected to demonstrate competency in (a) selecting appropriate **spectral indices** for water body extraction, (b) understanding **sensor-specific spectral windows** (NIR vs SWIR vs Thermal), (c) performing **change-detection inventory** across multi-date scenes, and (d) integrating the inventory output into a **GIS-based watershed management framework**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Operational Workflow of Water Resources Inventory

The operational sequence used by organizations such as **ISRO, NRSC, CWC (Central Water Commission)**, and **CGWB** is decomposed below into a five-stage architecture. Each stage is supported by a defined set of remote sensing and GIS operations.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition and Pre-Processing

- **Identification of sensor** based on target: e.g., **Landsat-8 OLI/TIRS** for medium-resolution open water, **Sentinel-2 MSI** for improved red-edge discrimination, **Sentinel-1 SAR** for cloud-penetrating water mapping during monsoon, **Resourcesat-2 LISS-III/AWiFS** for Indian regional context, **INSAT-3D/3DR** for thermal monitoring.
- **Radiometric correction** (DN → Top-of-Atmosphere radiance → Surface reflectance).
- **Atmospheric correction** (FLAASH, 6S, Sen2Cor, or Dark Object Subtraction).
- **Geometric correction** and **co-registration** to a standard projection (e.g., **WGS-84 / UTM**).
- **Cloud and shadow masking** (Fmask, s2cloudless, or manual QA bands).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Water pixels are *low-reflectance* features. Even a 2–3% atmospheric path radiance error can falsely eliminate small water bodies. Hence pre-processing is non-negotiable for KTU-level answers.

### Stage 2 — Water Body Extraction (Spectral Indexing)

The **physical principle** is that clear water exhibits:

- **Very low reflectance** in the **NIR** ($0.76\text{–}0.90 \, \mu m$) and **SWIR** ($1.4\text{–}2.5 \, \mu m$) bands.
- **High absorption** in the NIR due to water molecule vibrational overtones.
- This creates a **sharp contrast** between water and surrounding vegetation/soil.

The four canonical water indices are:

1. **NDWI — Normalized Difference Water Index (McFeeters, 1996)**
   $$NDWI = \frac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{NIR}}$$

2. **MNDWI — Modified NDWI (Xu, 2006)**
   $$MNDWI = \frac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{SWIR}}$$

3. **AWEI — Automated Water Extraction Index (Feyisa et al., 2014)**
   $$AWEI_{nsh} = 4 \cdot (\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{SWIR1}) - (0.25 \cdot \rho_{NIR} + 2.75 \cdot \rho_{SWIR2})$$

4. **WRI — Water Ratio Index (Shen & Li, 2010)**
   $$WRI = \frac{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** When asked to choose an index for **urban water body extraction**, always cite **MNDWI or AWEI** — not NDWI. NDWI misclassifies built-up shadows as water.

### Stage 3 — Classification and Vectorization

- **Unsupervised classification** (ISO-DATA, K-means) for quick reconnaissance.
- **Supervised classification** (Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest) for accurate boundary delineation.
- **Object-based classification** (e.g., eCognition) using **segmentation + nearest-neighbor** — preferred for **high-resolution** water body mapping.
- **Raster-to-vector conversion** for polygon storage in GIS.

### Stage 4 — Quantification and Change Detection

- Compute **area (ha/km²), perimeter (km), shoreline length**, and **volume (using bathymetry + DEM)**.
- Apply **multi-temporal change detection** (Image Differencing, Post-Classification Comparison, CVA — Change Vector Analysis).
- Tabulate **seasonal and annual water spread variation** (pre-monsoon, post-monsoon, winter, summer).

### Stage 5 — GIS Integration and Decision Support

- Overlay with **drainage network, watershed boundaries, soil, geology, LULC, slope, rainfall isohyets**.
- Compute **morphometric parameters** (drainage density, bifurcation ratio, elongation ratio).
- Generate **thematic atlases** for command area, recharge zones, and flood-inundation maps.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and **empirically validated** through the cited standard references. They are the **most frequently tested** in KTU ESE Module 4.

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Use / Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NDWI (McFeeters) | $\dfrac{\rho_{GREEN}-\rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{GREEN}+\rho_{NIR}}$ | Open water detection | Dimensionless, $[-1, 1]$ |
| 2 | MNDWI (Xu) | $\dfrac{\rho_{GREEN}-\rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{GREEN}+\rho_{SWIR}}$ | Urban + open water | Dimensionless, $[-1, 1]$ |
| 3 | AWEI (nsh) | $4(\rho_{GREEN}-\rho_{SWIR1}) - (0.25\rho_{NIR}+2.75\rho_{SWIR2})$ | Shadow-resilient water | Dimensionless |
| 4 | WRI (Shen & Li) | $\dfrac{\rho_{GREEN}+\rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR}+\rho_{SWIR}}$ | Built-up / water | Dimensionless |
| 5 | NDVI | $\dfrac{\rho_{NIR}-\rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR}+\rho_{RED}}$ | Vegetation mask | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Drainage Density $D_d$ | $D_d = \dfrac{\Sigma L_i}{A}$ | Basin dissection | $\text{km/km}^2$ |
| 7 | Bifurcation Ratio $R_b$ | $R_b = \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$ | Stream-order network | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Stream Frequency $F_s$ | $F_s = \dfrac{N_u}{A}$ | Texture of drainage | $\text{no./km}^2$ |
| 9 | Elongation Ratio $R_e$ | $R_e = \dfrac{1.128 \sqrt{A}}{L_b}$ | Basin shape | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Form Factor $F_f$ | $F_f = \dfrac{A}{L_b^2}$ | Basin shape | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Circulatory Ratio $R_c$ | $R_c = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | Basin compactness | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Storage Volume $V$ | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot h \cdot (A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2})$ | Reservoir capacity | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 13 | Area from Raster | $A = N_p \cdot r^2 \cdot \cos\phi$ | Planimetric area | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 14 | Radiance to BT | $BT = \dfrac{K_2}{\ln(K_1/L_\lambda + 1)}$ | Thermal emissivity | $\text{K}$ |
| 15 | Perimeter from Raster | $P = N_e \cdot r$ | Boundary length | $\text{m}$ |

> **Notation key:** $N_p$ = number of pixels, $r$ = spatial resolution, $\phi$ = latitude, $L_b$ = basin length, $P$ = perimeter, $A$ = basin area, $N_u$ = number of streams of order $u$, $L_i$ = total length of streams of order $i$, $h$ = reservoir depth, $A_1, A_2$ = successive contour areas, $K_1, K_2$ = sensor-specific thermal calibration constants, $L_\lambda$ = spectral radiance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Watch the unit trap (KTU Pitfall):** The vertical pipe is replaced by `\vert` in markdown tables. In your answer scripts, $D_d$ is **always expressed in $\text{km/km}^2$** — not $\text{m/m}^2$. Converting pixel counts to area, do not forget the **$\cos\phi$** latitude correction for imagery covering large N–S extents.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CWC Reservoir Sedimentation Studies** use periodic satellite-derived water-spread area to estimate siltation.
- **NRSC's Bhuvan-WRIS** is a live national water resources information system built on this exact workflow.
- **Urban flood management** (e.g., Chennai 2015, Kerala 2018) used Sentinel-1 SAR to map inundated extent within hours of the event.
- **Glacier retreat monitoring** in the Himalayas uses multi-temporal Landsat + Sentinel-2 inventories under the **ISRO-SDC programme**.
- **Wetland conservation** under the **Ramsar Convention** mandates geospatial inventories updated every 3 years.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why NDWI is Dimensionless and Bounded

The NDWI is a **normalized ratio**, derived directly from the general form of a Normalized Difference Index (NDI):

$$NDI = \frac{\rho_a - \rho_b}{\rho_a + \rho_b}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $a = \text{GREEN}$ band reflectance and $b = \text{NIR}$ band reflectance.

$$NDWI = \frac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{NIR}}$$

**Step 2:** Since $\rho \geq 0$ (passive remote sensing only records non-negative energy) and $\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{NIR} \neq 0$:

- When $\rho_{NIR} \ll \rho_{GREEN}$ (deep clear water): $NDWI \to +1$ (positive saturation).
- When $\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_{GREEN}$ (dense vegetation / dry soil): $NDWI \to -1$ (negative saturation).
- When $\rho_{NIR} = \rho_{GREEN}$: $NDWI = 0$ (threshold boundary).

**Step 3:** Therefore the bounded range is:

$$-1 \leq NDWI \leq +1$$

This bounded behavior is what makes NDWI **threshold-friendly**: a single cutoff (commonly $NDWI > 0.3$) cleanly separates water from non-water in the raster.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Reservoir Storage Volume from Satellite-Derived Contour Areas

The **Prismoidal / End-Area method** is the engineering standard for computing reservoir volume from successive contour areas derived from satellite-derived DEM and water-spread boundaries.

**Step 1:** Identify the lowest contour level $z_0$ and the highest contour level $z_n$ with $n$ equally spaced intervals of vertical increment $h$.

**Step 2:** Apply the **Prismoidal Formula** for any two consecutive contours $i$ and $i+1$:

$$V_{i,i+1} = \frac{h}{3} \cdot \left( A_i + A_{i+1} + \sqrt{A_i \cdot A_{i+1}} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Sum across all contour pairs to obtain total volume:

$$V_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{h}{3} \left( A_i + A_{i+1} + \sqrt{A_i \cdot A_{i+1}} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Numerical illustration. Let $h = 5 \, \text{m}$, $A_0 = 100 \, \text{ha}$, $A_1 = 80 \, \text{ha}$, $A_2 = 55 \, \text{ha}$, $A_3 = 30 \, \text{ha}$, $A_4 = 10 \, \text{ha}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{0,1} &= \frac{5}{3} \cdot (100 + 80 + \sqrt{100 \cdot 80}) \times 10^4 \\
        &= \frac{5}{3} \cdot (180 + 89.44) \times 10^4 \\
        &= \frac{5}{3} \cdot 269.44 \times 10^4 \\
        &= 4.491 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Repeat for $V_{1,2}, V_{2,3}, V_{3,4}$ and sum:

$$V_{total} = V_{0,1} + V_{1,2} + V_{2,3} + V_{3,4}$$

**Step 6:** Conclude with units: the answer must be reported in **$\text{m}^3$** (or **MCM = million cubic meters**, or **TMC = thousand million cubic feet** for Indian reservoir practice, where $1 \, \text{TMC} = 2.8317 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^3$).

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Drainage Density from Rasterized Streams

**Step 1:** Extract the **drainage network** from a DEM using a flow-direction algorithm (D8, D-inf, or MFD) and threshold the contributing area to define streams.

**Step 2:** Vectorize and measure the total stream length $\Sigma L_i$ (sum of lengths of all stream segments, in km).

**Step 3:** Compute the basin area $A$ (in $\text{km}^2$) from the watershed boundary.

**Step 4:** Apply the drainage density formula:

$$D_d = \frac{\Sigma L_i}{A}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret:
- $D_d < 0.5 \, \text{km/km}^2$: **Coarse drainage** — resistant rocks, low rainfall, high infiltration.
- $0.5 \leq D_d \leq 2.5 \, \text{km/km}^2$: **Moderate drainage** — typical of Indian peninsular basins.
- $D_d > 2.5 \, \text{km/km}^2$: **Fine drainage** — weak rocks, high rainfall, low infiltration, high runoff.

**Step 6:** Engineering implication — high $D_d$ basins generate **flashy hydrographs** with short lag time, requiring smaller, more frequent hydraulic structures.

## 3.4 Worked Example — NDWI Threshold Application on Hypothetical Pixel

A pixel in a Landsat-8 scene has the following Top-of-Atmosphere reflectance values:

- $\rho_{GREEN}$ (Band 3) = $0.08$
- $\rho_{NIR}$ (Band 5) = $0.05$

**Step 1:** Compute NDWI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NDWI &= \frac{0.08 - 0.05}{0.08 + 0.05} \\
     &= \frac{0.03}{0.13} \\
     &= 0.2308
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the threshold criterion. Using $NDWI \geq 0.3$ for strict open water, the pixel **fails** the threshold (it might be a wet soil, shallow water, or turbid pixel).

**Step 3:** Try MNDWI using $\rho_{SWIR1}$ (Band 6) = $0.02$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MNDWI &= \frac{0.08 - 0.02}{0.08 + 0.02} \\
      &= \frac{0.06}{0.10} \\
      &= 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $MNDWI = 0.60 \geq 0$, the pixel **passes** — confirming it is a water body. This demonstrates why MNDWI is **superior to NDWI** in low-contrast situations.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Inventory Tasks

| **Inventory Task** | **Recommended Sensor** | **Band / Wavelength** | **Index Used** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open water body mapping (clear sky) | Landsat-8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI | GREEN + NIR | NDWI | Cloud cover |
| Urban water body mapping | Landsat-8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI | GREEN + SWIR | MNDWI, AWEI | Shadow false positives |
| Monsoon water mapping (cloudy) | Sentinel-1 SAR (C-band) | VV, VH | Threshold backscatter | Speckle noise |
| Snow / glacier inventory | Sentinel-2, Landsat-8, MODIS | SWIR + VIS | NDSI, NSDI | Cloud-snow confusion |
| Surface temperature / thermal pollution | Landsat-8 TIRS, MODIS, INSAT-3D | TIR 10–12 µm | LST retrieval | Mixed pixel |
| High-resolution urban tank mapping | Cartosat-2, WorldView, Pléiades | Panchromatic / Multispectral | Object-based | Costly |
| Water turbidity / sediment | Sentinel-2 MSI, Landsat-8 OLI | RED + NIR | Turbidity Index | Atmospheric correction |
| Groundwater prospect mapping | DEM (SRTM, ALOS-PALSAR) + Geology + Lineament | — | Multi-criteria overlay | Indirect indicator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow — Water Resources Inventory Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition and Study Area] --> B[Sensor Selection and Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Pre-processing: Radiometric, Atmospheric, Geometric]
    C --> D[Spectral Index Computation: NDWI, MNDWI, AWEI, WRI]
    D --> E[Thresholding and Raster Water Mask]
    E --> F[Classification: Supervised, Unsupervised, Object-based]
    F --> G[Raster to Vector Polygon Conversion]
    G --> H[Attribute Computation: Area, Perimeter, Volume]
    H --> I[Multi-temporal Change Detection]
    I --> J[GIS Overlay: Drainage, Geology, Soil, Slope, LULC]
    J --> K[Decision Support: Water Atlas, Recharge Zones, Flood Maps]
    K --> L[Output: Thematic Maps, Statistics, Reports]

    style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style B fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#006064,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Water Index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Water Body Extraction Required] --> B{Is the study area urban or rural?}
    B -- Urban --> C{Is shadow a concern?}
    B -- Rural --> D[Use NDWI: GREEN minus NIR over sum]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use AWEI nsh formula]
    C -- No --> F[Use MNDWI: GREEN minus SWIR over sum]
    D --> G{Cloud cover during acquisition?}
    F --> G
    E --> G
    G -- Heavy cloud --> H[Switch to SAR: Sentinel-1 VV plus VH threshold]
    G -- Clear sky --> I[Apply threshold and finalize water mask]
    H --> I

    style A fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000

    style E fill:#ffa726,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style F fill:#ff9800,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style G fill:#fb8c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style H fill:#f57c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style I fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#e65100,color:#fff
```
